source.text:
Rina Kobayashi: heads up, I think I’m blocked on benchmark fleet perms again. Trying to run the representative suite on the H100 pool and the job fails before it even starts streaming metrics.

Rina Kobayashi: error from the harness runner:
```
PERMISSION_DENIED: service account "bench-runner@redwood-bench.iam.gserviceaccount.com" does not have permission "iam.serviceAccounts.getAccessToken" on resource "projects/redwood-bench/serviceAccounts/bench-runner@redwood-bench.iam.gserviceaccount.com" (or it may not exist)
```

Rina Kobayashi: this is from `benchctl run --fleet gpu-h100 --region us-east --workload chat_streaming_v1`.

Sean Gallagher: ugh. is this from your laptop creds or from the CI runner SA?

Rina Kobayashi: from the benchmark k8s runner (the job launches in `bench-runs` namespace). local dry-run is fine until it tries to upload artifacts.

Sahana Iyer: seeing the same in CI when it tries to fan out the matrix. step that fails is “impersonate bench-runner + write manifest + upload traces”.

Sahana Iyer: log snippet from GitHub action runner:
```
gcloud --impersonate-service-account=bench-runner@redwood-bench.iam.gserviceaccount.com auth print-access-token
ERROR: (gcloud.auth.print-access-token) PERMISSION_DENIED: Permission 'iam.serviceAccounts.getAccessToken' denied on resource (or it may not exist).
```

Allison Grant: which principal is doing the impersonation? (k8s workload identity SA or gha SA)

Sahana Iyer: GHA uses `gha-benchmark@redwood-ci.iam.gserviceaccount.com` -> tries to impersonate `bench-runner@redwood-bench...`

Rina Kobayashi: k8s uses `k8s-bench-runner` -> mapped to `bench-runner@redwood-bench...` (workload identity). but the artifact upload step is also trying to impersonate again (🤦) because harness expects it.

Sean Gallagher: ok two separate issues:
1) GHA SA missing tokenCreator on bench-runner
2) possibly the workload identity mapping isn’t actually in place for the `bench-runs` namespace (or the pod is using the default k8s SA)

Aisha Bello: can we please route this via helpdesk ticket so it doesn’t get lost? feels like the 3rd time we hit this.

Sean Gallagher: yeah. we need a clean “who can run benchmarks” policy and the exact roles.

Allison Grant: +1. also, we should not have the harness trying to impersonate if it already has WI creds. but short term, grant the minimal perms.

Rina Kobayashi: for immediate unblock: what role do we need? `roles/iam.serviceAccountTokenCreator` on `bench-runner` granted to `gha-benchmark`?

Sean Gallagher: yes (on the target SA). and for artifact writes: storage role on `gs://ri-perf-artifacts` (if not already). we should confirm it’s failing on token creation before storage though.

Sergio Costa: I can confirm dashboards are missing new runs bc nothing is landing in the bucket + metrics pipeline never sees the manifest.

Sergio Costa: so this is hard-blocking baseline sweeps.

Aisha Bello: filing ticket now. what’s the exact ask text?

Sean Gallagher: ask:
- Ensure `gha-benchmark@redwood-ci.iam.gserviceaccount.com` can impersonate `bench-runner@redwood-bench.iam.gserviceaccount.com` (grant `roles/iam.serviceAccountTokenCreator` on bench-runner)
- Verify K8s Workload Identity binding for namespace `bench-runs` and k8s SA `bench-runner` -> GCP SA `bench-runner@redwood-bench...`
- Confirm bench-runner has write access to `gs://ri-perf-artifacts` + ability to write run manifests to the metrics sink (if applicable)

Rina Kobayashi: also please confirm no org policy blocks `iamcredentials.googleapis.com` for that project. we saw that once.

Aisha Bello: got it.

INT Helpdesk Bot: Created JIRA ticket INT-8821 "Benchmark fleet service account permissions" <https://jira.redwoodinference.com/browse/INT-8821>

Aisha Bello: ticket filed: INT-8821. included errors + requested roles + namespaces.

Sahana Iyer: adding to ticket: the CI run id that failed + the exact command. also noting it’s blocking `perf-canary` nightly.

Allison Grant: can someone paste the current WI annotation for the k8s SA? if we’re using the default SA in the job template that’s the smoking gun.

Rina Kobayashi: from the job spec (rendered):
```
serviceAccountName: default
```
…welp. that’s it. I thought it was `bench-runner`.

Sean Gallagher: lol ok. so the harness chart / job template is wrong.

Rina Kobayashi: I can fix that in the harness repo, but we still need the GHA impersonation path for the matrix runs.

Sean Gallagher: yep. split:
- You/Sahana patch job template to use `serviceAccountName: bench-runner`
- Helpdesk fixes IAM for GHA -> bench-runner

Sahana Iyer: I’ll patch the workflow + helm values today. will link PR in INT-8821.

Sergio Costa: once fixed, ping me and I’ll re-run the dashboard ingestion backfill for the missing window.

Aisha Bello: I’ll keep an eye on INT-8821 and loop in the IAM folks if it stalls.

Sean Gallagher: thanks. also, once unblocked, let’s add a small preflight check in the harness that fails fast with a clear message when SA is `default` / missing WI.

Rina Kobayashi: +1. I’ll add that as a follow-up task after we unblock the sweep.

source.text:
Rafael Mendes: pulling a thread from the last couple incidents + workshop parking lot: edge cases where it’s clearly multi-cause (capacity + routing, capacity + auth, etc). we need a simple rule for “primary incident_type” vs “contributing factors” so reporting isn’t garbage.

Rafael Mendes: prompt: incident commander is in the channel, sees 5xx, sees router falling back a ton, and fleet is paging “regional GPUs saturated”. what do we want as primary type? :thinking_face:

Mina Haddad: +1 we keep arguing this in real time. my take: if routing is behaving as designed (fallbacks happening because upstream capacity), primary should be capacity/fleet. if routing is misbehaving (wrong region/model selected, stuck to unhealthy pool, fallback loop), primary should be routing.

Vanessa Ortiz: agree in spirit. problem is “routing behaving as designed” is hard to know early. on-call sees fallback counters explode and thinks routing bug; later we find it’s legit because capacity dropped.

Ravi Menon: we should optimize for what can be determined with minimal context during incident, and allow reclass after. but we need a consistent tie-breaker.

Allison Grant: also reminder: metrics labels will only store ONE incident_type for dashboards (cardinality). contributing factors can be postmortem fields / incident-bot text, but primary needs to be stable.

Aisha Bello: +1. for SLO/error budget reporting we only get value if “primary” roughly maps to the domain that would prevent recurrence.

Mei Lin: sharing from the 90-day backfill: biggest disagreement bucket is “capacity shortage vs routing/fallback misbehavior”. ~30% of those had people pick different types depending on whether they looked at router logs vs fleet dashboards first.

Rafael Mendes: ok, propose we define “primary = the first failure that would have avoided customer impact if it didn’t happen” (aka “first domino”). thoughts?

Vanessa Ortiz: careful. sometimes customer impact starts because routing sends load to a region that is fine, but it *makes it* overloaded. like: routing policy shifted traffic incorrectly -> capacity becomes insufficient. first domino is routing.

Mina Haddad: yeah, but in the inverse, capacity shortfall causes routing fallback to kick in (correctly). first domino is capacity.

Ravi Menon: maybe write it as:
1) if routing/control plane made a policy/decision that materially increased load or prevented healthy capacity usage -> Routing/Control Plane primary
2) else if demand exceeded available healthy capacity or provisioning lag -> Capacity/Fleet primary
3) else if both truly simultaneous/unknown -> choose the “dominant mitigation owner” (who can mitigate fastest) and list the other as contributing

Allison Grant: “dominant mitigation owner” is nice. it’s pragmatic during the incident. we can refine in postmortem.

Aisha Bello: we should be explicit: primary type drives: (a) paging routing, (b) SLA timer policy, (c) reporting. if we pick routing as primary when it’s capacity, we’ll page the wrong folks and miss time.

Dylan Brooks: incident-bot side: we can support:
- required `incident_type` (single select)
- optional `contributing_types` (multi select) BUT we might not want it v1 if it creates churn.
- or a freeform “contributing factors” text field with an allowlist prompt.
we can do contributing_types later; but we can add a “secondary_owner” and “contributing_systems” field in postmortem template now.

Rafael Mendes: for v1, i’d rather keep incident-bot minimal. but we can codify the rule and enforce “one primary”. contributing factors captured in postmortem + timeline.

Harish Reddy: from capacity ops: if it’s a true shortage (GPU saturation/provisioning lag), mitigation is usually traffic shaping + capacity add + quota adjustments. routing is along for the ride. so primary=capacity unless we have clear evidence router did something unintended.

Mina Haddad: +1 “clear evidence” should be a trigger:
- router chose a region marked unhealthy
- ignored health signals / stuck to stale config
- fallback reason = policy mismatch or config error (not capacity)
- traffic shifted without corresponding policy change request

Allison Grant: can we map to the new fallback_reason enums? if fallback_reason is 80%+ `capacity` -> primary Capacity/Fleet. if `policy`/`health`/`timeout` dominate -> primary Routing/Control Plane.

Mei Lin: that would help a LOT for consistency in backfill too. we can put this in the decision tree: “check fallback_reason breakdown”.

Ravi Menon: caveat: fallback_reason can be noisy early (sampling + partial rollout). but as a heuristic it’s better than vibes.

Aisha Bello: also need a “multi-cause but pick one” guideline for reporting. suggest: primary should not change more than once unless we learn something materially new. otherwise dashboards will churn.

Rafael Mendes: ok drafting a simple policy snippet:
- During incident: IC sets primary incident_type based on dominant symptom + best available signal.
- If unclear: pick `Unknown/Needs triage` for up to 30 mins, then MUST pick a primary type for ongoing comms/paging.
- When multiple plausible causes: pick primary using tie-breakers:
  A) routing/control plane decision increased load or prevented use of healthy capacity -> Routing/Control Plane
  B) demand exceeded healthy capacity / provisioning lag / GPU saturation -> Capacity/Fleet
  C) if both: choose team that owns the mitigation lever being executed (capacity add vs routing policy change) and list other as contributing factor in summary/postmortem.

Vanessa Ortiz: looks good. one more: if it presents as 429 throttling due to quotas (intentional) vs overload (unintentional), we should classify as “Rate limiting / Quota enforcement” not capacity.

Mina Haddad: +1. and for dedicated: some “capacity” events are actually tenant isolation misconfig -> might be “Routing/Placement policy misbehavior” not fleet shortage.

Ravi Menon: we should add examples:
- Example 1: Regional GPU saturation -> router falls back to secondary region -> elevated latency but no errors. Primary = Capacity/Fleet.
- Example 2: Router policy bug shifts 60% traffic to smaller region -> region overloads -> 5xx. Primary = Routing.
- Example 3: Capacity shortfall + router ignores health check and keeps sending to unhealthy pool. Primary = Routing (clear misbehavior) + contributing = Capacity.

Allison Grant: and explicitly: only primary becomes the metric label `incident_type`. contributing goes to postmortem fields (not labels) to avoid cardinality blowups.

Dylan Brooks: i can add a short “edge cases” section in incident-bot help text: `@incident-bot help classify` with these bullets + a link to the decision tree doc.

Aisha Bello: +1. also for SLA timers: if primary is Capacity/Fleet and it’s a cloud provider event, carve-out rules apply; if primary is Routing, carve-out probably doesn’t. so primary choice has real consequences.

Rafael Mendes: good point. we’ll add a line: “don’t pick primary to ‘optimize’ SLA outcomes; pick the best technical classification. carve-outs handled separately in SLA evaluator.”

Mei Lin: can we capture “confidence” somewhere? even a “low/med/high” in the incident summary would help later.

Rafael Mendes: not v1 in tooling, but IC can write “classification confidence: low” in the summary comment. we can add it to the postmortem template.

incident-bot: Reminder: Starting next week, `incident_type` and `owner` are required before closing Sev0/Sev1 incidents. Use `/incident classify` to update.

Vanessa Ortiz: :thumbsup: once this is in writing, fleet on-call will stop getting paged for routing bugs (and vice versa) hopefully.

Mina Haddad: i’ll paste these examples into the routing appendix too. and i’ll align with the fallback_reason mapping so it’s not conflicting.

Ravi Menon: can we also add “if mitigation executed is ‘disable fallback’ or ‘change routing policy’, routing should be primary unless proven otherwise”? feels aligned with the mitigation-owner tie-breaker.

Rafael Mendes: yep. i’ll capture that as tie-breaker C detail.

Allison Grant: please keep the number of edge-case bullets small. decision tree needs to be usable at 3am.

Rafael Mendes: agreed. i’ll draft a short section + 3 examples and send for quick review here later today.

- Example 3: Capacity shortfall + router ignores health check and keeps sending to unhealthy pool. Primary = Routing (clear misbehavior) + contributing = Capacity.

Allison Grant: and explicitly: only primary becomes the metric label `incident_type`. contributing goes to postmortem fields (not labels) to avoid cardinality blowups.

Dylan Brooks: i can add a short “edge cases” section in incident-bot help text: `@incident-bot help classify` with these bullets + a link to the decision tree doc.

Aisha Bello: +1. also for SLA timers: if primary is Capacity/Fleet and it’s a cloud provider event, carve-out rules apply; if primary is Routing, carve-out probably doesn’t. so primary choice has real consequences.

Rafael Mendes: good point. we’ll add a line: “don’t pick primary to ‘optimize’ SLA outcomes; pick the best technical classification. carve-outs handled separately in SLA evaluator.”

Mei Lin: can we capture “confidence” somewhere? even a “low/med/high” in the incident summary would help later.

Rafael Mendes: not v1 in tooling, but IC can write “classification confidence: low” in the summary comment. we can add it to the postmortem template.

incident-bot: Reminder: Starting next week, `incident_type` and `owner` are required before closing Sev0/Sev1 incidents. Use `/incident classify` to update.

Vanessa Ortiz: :thumbsup: once this is in writing, fleet on-call will stop getting paged for routing bugs (and vice versa) hopefully.

Mina Haddad: i’ll paste these examples into the routing appendix too. and i’ll align with the fallback_reason mapping so it’s not conflicting.

Ravi Menon: can we also add “if mitigation executed is ‘disable fallback’ or ‘change routing policy’, routing should be primary unless proven otherwise”? feels aligned with the mitigation-owner tie-breaker.

Rafael Mendes: yep. i’ll capture that as tie-breaker C detail.

Allison Grant: please keep the number of edge-case bullets small. decision tree needs to be usable at 3am.

Rafael Mendes: agreed. i’ll draft a short section + 3 examples and send for quick review here later today.
text:
Rafael Mendes: pulling a thread from the last couple incidents + workshop parking lot: edge cases where it’s clearly multi-cause (capacity + routing, capacity + auth, etc). we need a simple rule for “primary incident_type” vs “contributing factors” so reporting isn’t garbage.

Rafael Mendes: prompt: incident commander is in the channel, sees 5xx, sees router falling back a ton, and fleet is paging “regional GPUs saturated”. what do we want as primary type? :thinking_face:

Mina Haddad: +1 we keep arguing this in real time. my take: if routing is behaving as designed (fallbacks happening because upstream capacity), primary should be capacity/fleet. if routing is misbehaving (wrong region/model selected, stuck to unhealthy pool, fallback loop), primary should be routing.

Vanessa Ortiz: agree in spirit. problem is “routing behaving as designed” is hard to know early. on-call sees fallback counters explode and thinks routing bug; later we find it’s legit because capacity dropped.

Ravi Menon: we should optimize for what can be determined with minimal context during incident, and allow reclass after. but we need a consistent tie-breaker.

Allison Grant: also reminder: metrics labels will only store ONE incident_type for dashboards (cardinality). contributing factors can be postmortem fields / incident-bot text, but primary needs to be stable.

Aisha Bello: +1. for SLO/error budget reporting we only get value if “primary” roughly maps to the domain that would prevent recurrence.

Mei Lin: sharing from the 90-day backfill: biggest disagreement bucket is “capacity shortage vs routing/fallback misbehavior”. ~30% of those had people pick different types depending on whether they looked at router logs vs fleet dashboards first.

Rafael Mendes: ok, propose we define “primary = the first failure that would have avoided customer impact if it didn’t happen” (aka “first domino”). thoughts?

Vanessa Ortiz: careful. sometimes customer impact starts because routing sends load to a region that is fine, but it *makes it* overloaded. like: routing policy shifted traffic incorrectly -> capacity becomes insufficient. first domino is routing.

Mina Haddad: yeah, but in the inverse, capacity shortfall causes routing fallback to kick in (correctly). first domino is capacity.

Ravi Menon: maybe write it as:
1) if routing/control plane made a policy/decision that materially increased load or prevented healthy capacity usage -> Routing/Control Plane primary
2) else if demand exceeded available healthy capacity or provisioning lag -> Capacity/Fleet primary
3) else if both truly simultaneous/unknown -> choose the “dominant mitigation owner” (who can mitigate fastest) and list the other as contributing

Allison Grant: “dominant mitigation owner” is nice. it’s pragmatic during the incident. we can refine in postmortem.

Aisha Bello: we should be explicit: primary type drives: (a) paging routing, (b) SLA timer policy, (c) reporting. if we pick routing as primary when it’s capacity, we’ll page the wrong folks and miss time.

Dylan Brooks: incident-bot side: we can support:
- required `incident_type` (single select)
- optional `contributing_types` (multi select) BUT we might not want it v1 if it creates churn.
- or a freeform “contributing factors” text field with an allowlist prompt.
we can do contributing_types later; but we can add a “secondary_owner” and “contributing_systems” field in postmortem template now.

Rafael Mendes: for v1, i’d rather keep incident-bot minimal. but we can codify the rule and enforce “one primary”. contributing factors captured in postmortem + timeline.

Harish Reddy: from capacity ops: if it’s a true shortage (GPU saturation/provisioning lag), mitigation is usually traffic shaping + capacity add + quota adjustments. routing is along for the ride. so primary=capacity unless we have clear evidence router did something unintended.

Mina Haddad: +1 “clear evidence” should be a trigger:
- router chose a region marked unhealthy
- ignored health signals / stuck to stale config
- fallback reason = policy mismatch or config error (not capacity)
- traffic shifted without corresponding policy change request

Allison Grant: can we map to the new fallback_reason enums? if fallback_reason is 80%+ `capacity` -> primary Capacity/Fleet. if `policy`/`health`/`timeout` dominate -> primary Routing/Control Plane.

Mei Lin: that would help a LOT for consistency in backfill too. we can put this in the decision tree: “check fallback_reason breakdown”.

Ravi Menon: caveat: fallback_reason can be noisy early (sampling + partial rollout). but as a heuristic it’s better than vibes.

Aisha Bello: also need a “multi-cause but pick one” guideline for reporting. suggest: primary should not change more than once unless we learn something materially new. otherwise dashboards will churn.

Rafael Mendes: ok drafting a simple policy snippet:
- During incident: IC sets primary incident_type based on dominant symptom + best available signal.
- If unclear: pick `Unknown/Needs triage` for up to 30 mins, then MUST pick a primary type for ongoing comms/paging.
- When multiple plausible causes: pick primary using tie-breakers:
  A) routing/control plane decision increased load or prevented use of healthy capacity -> Routing/Control Plane
  B) demand exceeded healthy capacity / provisioning lag / GPU saturation -> Capacity/Fleet
  C) if both: choose team that owns the mitigation lever being executed (capacity add vs routing policy change) and list other as contributing factor in summary/postmortem.

Vanessa Ortiz: looks good. one more: if it presents as 429 throttling due to quotas (intentional) vs overload (unintentional), we should classify as “Rate limiting / Quota enforcement” not capacity.

Mina Haddad: +1. and for dedicated: some “capacity” events are actually tenant isolation misconfig -> might be “Routing/Placement policy misbehavior” not fleet shortage.

Ravi Menon: we should add examples:
- Example 1: Regional GPU saturation -> router falls back to secondary region -> elevated latency but no errors. Primary = Capacity/Fleet.
- Example 2: Router policy bug shifts 60% traffic to smaller region -> region overloads -> 5xx. Primary = Routing.
- Example 3: Capacity shortfall + router ignores health check and keeps sending to unhealthy pool. Primary = Routing (clear misbehavior) + contributing = Capacity.

Allison Grant: and explicitly: only primary becomes the metric label `incident_type`. contributing goes to postmortem fields (not labels) to avoid cardinality blowups.

Dylan Brooks: i can add a short “edge cases” section in incident-bot help text: `@incident-bot help classify` with these bullets + a link to the decision tree doc.

Aisha Bello: +1. also for SLA timers: if primary is Capacity/Fleet and it’s a cloud provider event, carve-out rules apply; if primary is Routing, carve-out probably doesn’t. so primary choice has real consequences.

Rafael Mendes: good point. we’ll add a line: “don’t pick primary to ‘optimize’ SLA outcomes; pick the best technical classification. carve-outs handled separately in SLA evaluator.”

Mei Lin: can we capture “confidence” somewhere? even a “low/med/high” in the incident summary would help later.

Rafael Mendes: not v1 in tooling, but IC can write “classification confidence: low” in the summary comment. we can add it to the postmortem template.

incident-bot: Reminder: Starting next week, `incident_type` and `owner` are required before closing Sev0/Sev1 incidents. Use `/incident classify` to update.

Vanessa Ortiz: :thumbsup: once this is in writing, fleet on-call will stop getting paged for routing bugs (and vice versa) hopefully.

Mina Haddad: i’ll paste these examples into the routing appendix too. and i’ll align with the fallback_reason mapping so it’s not conflicting.

Ravi Menon: can we also add “if mitigation executed is ‘disable fallback’ or ‘change routing policy’, routing should be primary unless proven otherwise”? feels aligned with the mitigation-owner tie-breaker.

Rafael Mendes: yep. i’ll capture that as tie-breaker C detail.

Allison Grant: please keep the number of edge-case bullets small. decision tree needs to be usable at 3am.

Rafael Mendes: agreed. i’ll draft a short section + 3 examples and send for quick review here later today.

source.content:
Sean Gallagher: looping folks in here re: perf-canary alert routing. we need to be super explicit about what pages on-call vs what just posts to triage.

Sean Gallagher: context: rep workload suite is going into nightly + (eventually) release gating. if we page for every -2% blip we’ll burn the team.

Aisha Bello: +1. also want alignment w/ existing latency SLO paging (prod) vs canary (non-prod). canary should be “actionable for release health” not “noise”.

Allison Grant: agree. can we enumerate alert types first? from what Sergio added in observability-pack i saw:
- regression (TTFT/TPOT/throughput)
- variance high
- job failed / no data
- schema/manifest validation failure

Sergio Costa: yep. panels are already splitting those. i can map to alert rules: INFO/WARN/CRIT.

Connor O'Brien: from release gating POV: for release branches, a CRIT regression should block the release automatically. do we also page? i’d vote yes *only* for release branch gating failures.

Sahana Iyer: + job failures that prevent gating should page too (e.g., GPU pipeline down / runner auth broken). otherwise release train gets stuck and nobody notices until morning.

Mateo Alvarez: for nightly on main, i’d rather it goes to triage channel + linear ticket. paging is too heavy unless it’s a clear “this will hit prod” situation.

Rina Kobayashi: perf-canary currently emits regression events with fields: suite, workload, model, region, sku, metric, delta_pct, sample_n, run_url. we can route based on those tags.

Sean Gallagher: proposal v0:
1) PAGE (PagerDuty) only when:
  a) `gating_mode=true` (release branch) AND (job failed OR missing data OR manifest/schema invalid OR regression CRIT)
  b) nightly: only for “no data for > 6h” (pipeline stuck), not for regressions
2) TRIAGE channel for everything else (nightly regressions + high variance)

Aisha Bello: i like the “no data > 6h” paging rule. can we also add “no data for a gated run > 30m” (since release checks are expected to finish in that window)?

Sahana Iyer: 30m seems right. perf runs usually 20-25m if fleet isn’t contended. 45m max?

Allison Grant: i’d do: gated: page at 45m no-data. nightly: page at 6h no-data. (and still post a triage note at first failure so people see it.)

Sergio Costa: easy. alert expressions can do time-since-last-success per (suite, mode). we should be careful about grouping so we don’t page 12 times.

Sean Gallagher: grouping / dedupe key suggestion:
- gated paging: group by `mode + suite` (not by model/workload) so it’s a single incident
- triage posts: ok to be more granular (model/workload) but cap to top N per hour

Perf Canary Bot: Nightly summary: representative-suite v1 completed (us-east/H100). 2 WARN regressions detected. Details: https://grafana.redwood.ai/d/perf-baseline/perf-canary?run=1764022711

Mateo Alvarez: ^ this is exactly why i don’t want pages. 2 WARNs nightly is normal while we’re calibrating.

Connor O'Brien: define CRIT vs WARN thresholds here, otherwise routing is ambiguous. what are we shipping?

Allison Grant: from the regression policy draft: WARN at >= +5% p95/p99 for TTFT/TPOT sustained across 3 runs; CRIT at >= +10% sustained (or >= +15% on single run) + passes min sample size.

Aisha Bello: can we make it metric-specific?
- TTFT p99 is most sensitive for user experience
- TPOT p99 is noisy in streaming if client pacing creeps in
- throughput should probably be WARN only unless it’s huge

Rina Kobayashi: we can route severity by metric:
- TTFT p99: WARN +5%, CRIT +10%
- TPOT p99: WARN +7%, CRIT +12% (and only for server-measured TPOT)
- throughput tokens/s: WARN -5%, CRIT -10%
(and require sample_n >= 30 runs worth of requests / whatever the policy says)

Sahana Iyer: + add an explicit “variance high” alert that never pages, just posts: ‘results not trustworthy’ + link to noise scratchpad/runbook.

Sean Gallagher: ok, so routing matrix:

PAGER (PD service: Inference Platform On-Call)
- GATED perf-canary run failed (job status != success)
- GATED no results after 45m (per suite)
- GATED schema/manifest validation failure (that blocks gating)
- GATED regression CRIT on TTFT p99 / TPOT p99 / throughput (per the thresholds)

TRIAGE (channel: #perf-canary-triage)
- any nightly regression WARN/CRIT
- any high variance detection
- any kernel selection fallback spikes (FYI)
- nightly no results after 60m (heads up), page only at 6h

Aisha Bello: for the PD paging, can we add policy “auto-resolve if next gated rerun passes”? we don’t want long-lived incidents for transient fleet stuff.

Sergio Costa: yep. can set the alert to resolve on next success datapoint. PD will close.

Connor O'Brien: who’s primary responder for PD pages? release captain? runtime on-call? (we need a clear rotation.)

Sean Gallagher: propose it pages Inference Platform On-Call (same rotation that handles runtime deploy issues). release captain gets notified in-channel but not paged.

Mateo Alvarez: works. for triage channel: can we have bot auto-file Linear when CRIT nightly happens (even if not paging)?

Rina Kobayashi: i can add that to perf-canary: if severity=CRIT and mode=nightly, create Linear in ENG queue w/ run URL + diff summary.

Allison Grant: please include run manifest link in alert text (env capture). otherwise we’ll spend 30m asking “what driver was this”.

Sergio Costa: ack. alert payload will include:
- run_url (grafana)
- manifest_url (artifact)
- git_sha (runtime + harness)
- region/sku
- top deltas table

Perf Canary Bot: :memo: Action items
- Sergio: update alert rules + routing to match matrix (PD vs #perf-canary-triage)
- Rina: add Linear auto-ticket for nightly CRIT + include manifest_url
- Allison/Aisha: review final wording + severity thresholds

Sean Gallagher: cool. i’ll document this in the runbook + pin in #perf-canary-triage once it’s live. goal is “pages only when release is blocked or pipeline is dead”. :thumbsup: (reactions: Allison Grant, Aisha Bello, Sahana Iyer)

Sean Gallagher: We need to align on alerting for Optimize quality gates before beta. Main worry: noisy pages from flaky eval suites. What do we want to page on vs ticket vs dashboard-only?

Allison Grant: +1. If we page on every gate failure we’re going to get crushed. Many failures are “expected” (legit regression) and should block apply, but not page SRE.

Sergio Costa: Agree. I’m thinking split signals:
1) gate enforcement reliability (system health) -> page
2) quality regressions (gate failing) -> notify owners, not page
3) eval flakiness spike -> ticket + Slack to #eng-ml

Connor O'Brien: From release side: gate failures are part of the intended workflow. What breaks us is when gates can’t run (timeouts, stuck, GPU starvation) and it blocks rollouts / one-click apply.

Sean Gallagher: Exactly. Paging should be “platform degraded” not “product did its job.”

Diego Ramos: For online canary signals (later), same deal: page if signal pipeline is busted, not if it flags regression.

Allison Grant: Proposed rule of thumb:
- If it prevents customers from deploying safe changes for >N mins across multiple accounts/routes -> page
- If it prevents a specific optimization from applying because it’s unsafe -> no page

Jonas Weber: On flakiness: we’re adding flake score + rerun policy in eval-harness (PR-418). We can export `flake_score` and “reruns attempted” so alerting can key off “high flake rate” instead of raw failure.

Sergio Costa: What’s a good definition? Something like % of gate runs that ended in “inconclusive/flaky” after max reruns.

Jonas Weber: Yeah. Eval-harness will emit outcome buckets: `passed`, `failed`, `inconclusive_flaky`, `infra_error`.

Allison Grant: Great, because infra_error is the one we should page on if it spikes.

Sean Gallagher: Let’s enumerate failure reasons that should NOT page:
- `quality_regression` (expected)
- `threshold_not_met` (expected)
- `manual_override_granted` (expected-ish)

And reasons that MAY page:
- `infra_error`
- `eval_timeout`
- `gate_orchestrator_error`
- `artifact_upload_failed` (maybe warn)

Connor O'Brien: + `gpu_capacity_unavailable` if it creates backlogs. We’ve seen CI jobs get stuck waiting for GPUs.

Sahana Iyer: For CI, we already have separate alerting on the GPU queue. For prod gate runs, we should track queue depth + time-in-queue. Page if p95 time-to-start > X for Y minutes.

Allison Grant: +1. “Gate execution latency SLO” is what we should enforce (there’s a separate thread on that). If gates take 45 mins, customers will escalate.

Sergio Costa: So alerts:
A) Gate system health
- `gate_run_start_latency_seconds` p95 > 10m for 20m (page)
- `gate_run_end_to_end_seconds` p95 > 30m for 30m (page?)
B) Infra errors
- `gate_run_outcome{outcome="infra_error"}` rate > baseline (page)
C) Flake
- `gate_run_outcome{outcome="inconclusive_flaky"}` rate > threshold (ticket)

Sean Gallagher: Sounds right. We should also have a “stuck state machine” detector: runs in `running` > max_runtime. That indicates deadlocks/worker loss.

Sergio Costa: Yep. We can do:
`max_over_time(gate_run_running_age_seconds[15m]) > 3600` -> page.

Allison Grant: Careful: long-running is normal for full gates on big prompt sets. But we can have `tier=smoke|full` labels and different limits.

Jonas Weber: Full gates target is ~20-25 min runtime, but can be 40 min on long-context suites. Smoke gates should be <6-8 min.

Sean Gallagher: Then thresholds:
- smoke: page if p95 > 12m for 30m
- full: page if p95 > 45m for 30m
And “no progress” if running_age > 90m regardless.

Connor O'Brien: Also need to avoid paging during known deploy windows / brownouts. Can we do multi-window burn rate instead of single threshold?

Allison Grant: Yes. Use two windows:
- fast: 5m / 1h
- slow: 30m / 6h
Page when both burn. (Standard SRE practice.)

Sergio Costa: For infra_error, something like:
`(sum(rate(gate_runs_total{outcome="infra_error"}[5m])) / sum(rate(gate_runs_total[5m]))) > 0.05`
AND same ratio over 30m > 0.02.

Sean Gallagher: Works. Make sure it’s scoped to production gate runs only (exclude staging + CI). Labels like `env=prod` and `source=optimize`.

Sahana Iyer: + please add a silence/route filter for beta accounts. During internal beta we’ll be intentionally poking it.

Allison Grant: We can add `account_tier` and `is_internal` tags; alerts should be based on external impact unless we’re doing a rehearsal.

Diego Ramos: For “quality regression” notifications, can we still post to Slack but not page? Like to #optimize-alerts or #eng-ml?

Sean Gallagher: Yes. “Non-paging notifications” are good. Post a summary with links to artifacts + config diff so owners can triage async.

Jonas Weber: Please include prompt set id + eval-harness run id. Otherwise we spend half the time hunting.

Sergio Costa: Noted. We’ll include fields in the log payload and wire to Slack notifications.

Allison Grant: Another anti-noise tactic: dedupe by (config_change_id, model_family, route). If the same gate run retries 3 times, don’t send 3 notifications.

Connor O'Brien: :thumbsup: Also for timeouts in a single region (EU-West), we don’t want global paging unless it’s widespread. Alerts should be per-region with a higher-level aggregate.

Sean Gallagher: Great point. Page severity based on blast radius:
- Sev2 page if >=2 regions impacted OR >=X% of external gate runs failing with infra_error
- Otherwise Sev3 ticket + regional followup

PagerDuty Bot: (configured) Created incident rule draft: "Optimize Quality Gates: infra_error ratio burn" (not yet enabled)

Allison Grant: Nice. Let’s make sure runbook has explicit “is this real regression or infra?” decision tree. If it’s infra, SRE; if it’s regression, route to Applied ML / product owners.

Sean Gallagher: I’ll add to runbook: first check outcome bucket distribution + flake score; second check GPU queue + eval-harness health; third check Optimize gate orchestrator logs.

Talia Benson: For the launch checklist, can we capture: dashboards live, paging rules enabled (but with beta silences), and ownership for non-paging notifications?

Sean Gallagher: Yep. I’ll summarize:
- Paging: infra_error, stuck runs, queue latency burn
- Ticket/Slack: flake spikes, artifact upload issues
- Non-paging Slack: regression failures (quality), override spikes

Sergio Costa: I’ll implement alert rules in observability-pack PR, will tag you both for review. Also adding a “flake rate” panel so we can tune thresholds without waking people up.

Allison Grant: :+1: Please default the flake alert to ticket-only at first. We can tighten later after we quarantine the worst prompt sets.

Jonas Weber: Works for us. We’ll maintain a list of quarantined prompt set ids; would be good if dashboards can filter them out.

Sean Gallagher: Perfect. Let’s keep paging noise low until we have stable suites. End goal: “safe-by-default” without punishing oncall.

source.messages:
Sean Gallagher: jumping in on the hardware tuning profiles rollout — we need concrete rollback criteria + links before we can sign off on canary. “We’ll watch dashboards” isn’t enough. Can someone from Runtime/Infra drop:
- explicit thresholds (p95/p99, error rate, OOM/restarts, throughput)
- which dashboards are the source of truth
- and the *fast path* for oncall to disable profiles per pool/region (no rebuilds)

Neha Kapoor: +1. also please specify time windows (sustained vs spike) and who has permission to flip the kill switch. this is going into oncall guidance.

Aisha Bello: yup. need to know which panels to watch and what “red” looks like. right now the perf-canary gate doc is vague for post-deploy.

Irene Choi: ack. we can support pool-level toggles in fleet manager but need the exact mechanism/keys. Benji/Ethan can you confirm what the runtime reads?

Benji Okafor: runtime side: profile selection is controlled via:
- fleet default config (per pool) `runtime.profile_pack.version` + `runtime.profile_pack.enabled`
- override env var (highest precedence) `RW_PROFILE_PACK_ENABLED` and `RW_PROFILE_FORCE_PROFILE`
- fallback to baseline defaults if detection/compat fails

Benji Okafor: for quick disable per pool: set `runtime.profile_pack.enabled=false` in that pool’s config + restart the runtime pods. no new build.

Ethan Park: ^ and `RW_PROFILE_FORCE_PROFILE=baseline` exists as an emergency override (bypasses detection). we added it specifically for oncall.

Vanessa Ortiz: we can wire the pool config change to a rolling restart via the deployment controller (Hosted + Dedicated). for Private, customers can do same via Helm values but oncall can’t touch.

Aisha Bello: ok but what are the rollback thresholds? need numbers.

Sofia Ivanova: proposed initial thresholds (post-deploy canary / staged rollout):
1) tail latency: p95 end-to-end latency regression > +10% vs previous 24h baseline for 15 min (same model+route cohort)
2) p99 regression > +15% for 10 min
3) error rate (5xx + upstream timeouts): > 0.25% for 10 min OR > 0.10% sustained 30 min
4) OOM / restart loops: any new OOM kill events in canary pool after enable => rollback (0 tolerance)
5) throughput: tokens/sec per GPU down > 8% for 20 min on stable load (exclude low-traffic)

Allison Grant: + add “queueing/backpressure” indicator. if queue depth/queue wait explodes we should rollback even if p95 hasn’t caught up yet.

Kira Volkov: perf-canary gates already check some of this pre-merge, but for runtime deploy we should reuse the same SLO metrics + add “profile_selection” dimension to slice.

Sean Gallagher: helpful. can we make it more explicit about *where* to measure? i don’t want people arguing if it’s app latency vs runtime latency.

Aisha Bello: +1. we need a single dashboard set that oncall uses.

Ethan Park: dashboards (Grafana) we’ve been using internally:
- Runtime Overview (pool): https://grafana.redwood.ai/d/runtime-overview/runtime-overview?var-pool=$POOL
- Tail latency by model/route: https://grafana.redwood.ai/d/llm-latency/llm-latency?var-pool=$POOL
- Profile selection + override source: https://grafana.redwood.ai/d/profile-pack/profile-selection?var-pool=$POOL
- GPU OOM + container restarts: https://grafana.redwood.ai/d/gpu-health/gpu-health?var-pool=$POOL
- Scheduler queue depth/backpressure: https://grafana.redwood.ai/d/scheduler/scheduler-queues?var-pool=$POOL

Allison Grant: for alerts, we should reference the same metrics names. can you paste the exact metric keys for p95/p99 and error rate?

Benji Okafor: metric names (as instrumented in runtime):
- `inference_request_latency_ms{quantile="0.95"}` and `...{quantile="0.99"}` (end-to-end runtime)
- `inference_request_errors_total` / `inference_requests_total`
- `runtime_oom_kills_total` (from runtime signal) + `container_restarts_total`
- `tokens_generated_total` / `gpu_seconds_total` (tokens/sec/gpu derived in dashboard)
- `scheduler_queue_wait_ms{quantile="0.95"}`

Neha Kapoor: we should be careful on “baseline” definition. for rollback thresholds, compare to the immediately previous deployment window (or 1h rolling median) not “yesterday” if traffic mix changes.

Sofia Ivanova: fair. suggest baseline = same pool, same model+route, previous 60 min (profile disabled) if available; otherwise previous 24h median for that cohort. dashboard already has both lines.

Sean Gallagher: ok now “fast path”: assume I’m oncall at 3am. p95 is up +15% in a canary pool. what do I do, exactly, and how long to take effect?

Kira Thompson: draft playbook (happy to paste into runbook once we agree):
1) Confirm profile is enabled and which profile selected (Profile selection dashboard)
2) If regression meets threshold, disable per pool:
   - set `runtime.profile_pack.enabled=false` (fleet config) OR set `RW_PROFILE_PACK_ENABLED=false` if we need immediate override
   - if still hot, force baseline: `RW_PROFILE_FORCE_PROFILE=baseline`
3) Trigger rolling restart in that pool (deployment controller)
4) Watch: p95/p99 + error rate + restarts for 15 min; if recovered, leave disabled + open incident follow-up
ETA: config push <2 min, rolling restart 5–12 min depending on pool size

Irene Choi: + permissioning: SRE oncall + infra oncall can flip pool config. we should NOT require runtime engineers.

Allison Grant: do we have a single “kill switch” we can document, not two different toggles? less confusion.

Benji Okafor: fleet config `runtime.profile_pack.enabled=false` is the standard. env var override is for “config plane is broken” or if we need to override precedence quickly. we can document both with “use A unless A fails”.

Aisha Bello: what about partial rollback (only B200 experimental) vs everything?

Ethan Park: per-profile gating exists:
- `runtime.profile_pack.allow_experimental=false`
- `RW_PROFILE_ALLOW_EXPERIMENTAL=false`
So you can keep H100/L40S profiles on, while disabling B200.

title: #eng-sre: 1763810094
preview:
Sean Gallagher: jumping in on the hardware tuning profiles rollout — we need concrete rollback criteria + links before we can sign off on canary. “We’ll watch dashboards” isn’t enough. Can someone from Runtime/Infra drop:
- explicit thresholds (p95/p99, error rate, OOM/restarts, throughput)
- which dashboards are the source of truth
- and the *fast path* for oncall to disable profiles per pool/region (no rebuilds)

Neha Kapoor: +1. also please specify time windows (sustained vs spike) and who has permission to flip the kill switch. this is going into oncall guidance.

Aisha Bello: yup. need to know which panels to watch and what “red” looks like. right now the perf-canary gate doc is vague for post-deploy.

Irene Choi: ack. we can support pool-level toggles in fleet manager but need the exact mechanism/keys. Benji/Ethan can you confirm what the runtime reads?

Benji Okafor: runtime side: profile selection is controlled via:
- fleet default config (per pool) `runtime.profile_pack.version` + `runtime.profile_pack.enabled`
- override env var (highest precedence) `RW_PROFILE_PACK_ENABLED` and `RW_PROFILE_FORCE_PROFILE`
- fallback to baseline defaults if detection/compat fails

Benji Okafor: for quick disable per pool: set `runtime.profile_pack.enabled=false` in that pool’s config + restart the runtime pods. no new build.

Ethan Park: ^ and `RW_PROFILE_FORCE_PROFILE=baseline` exists as an emergency override (bypasses detection). we added it specifically for oncall.

Vanessa Ortiz: we can wire the pool config change to a rolling restart via the deployment controller (Hosted + Dedicated). for Private, customers can do same via Helm values but oncall can’t touch.

Aisha Bello: ok but what are the rollback thresholds? need numbers.

Sofia Ivanova: proposed initial thresholds (post-deploy canary / staged rollout):
1) tail latency: p95 end-to-end latency regression > +10% vs previous 24h baseline for 15 min (same model+route cohort)
2) p99 regression > +15% for 10 min
3) error rate (5xx + upstream timeouts): > 0.25% for 10 min OR > 0.10% sustained 30 min
4) OOM / restart loops: any new OOM kill events in canary pool after enable => rollback (0 tolerance)
5) throughput: tokens/sec per GPU down > 8% for 20 min on stable load (exclude low-traffic)

Allison Grant: + add “queueing/backpressure” indicator. if queue depth/queue wait explodes we should rollback even if p95 hasn’t caught up yet.

Kira Volkov: perf-canary gates already check some of this pre-merge, but for runtime deploy we should reuse the same SLO metrics + add “profile_selection” dimension to slice.

Sean Gallagher: helpful. can we make it more explicit about *where* to measure? i don’t want people arguing if it’s app latency vs runtime latency.

Aisha Bello: +1. we need a single dashboard set that oncall uses.

Ethan Park: dashboards (Grafana) we’ve been using internally:
- Runtime Overview (pool): https://grafana.redwood.ai/d/runtime-overview/runtime-overview?var-pool=$POOL
- Tail latency by model/route: https://grafana.redwood.ai/d/llm-latency/llm-latency?var-pool=$POOL
- Profile selection + override source: https://grafana.redwood.ai/d/profile-pack/profile-selection?var-pool=$POOL
- GPU OOM + container restarts: https://grafana.redwood.ai/d/gpu-health/gpu-health?var-pool=$POOL
- Scheduler queue depth/backpressure: https://grafana.redwood.ai/d/scheduler/scheduler-queues?var-pool=$POOL

Allison Grant: for alerts, we should reference the same metrics names. can you paste the exact metric keys for p95/p99 and error rate?

Benji Okafor: metric names (as instrumented in runtime):
- `inference_request_latency_ms{quantile="0.95"}` and `...{quantile="0.99"}` (end-to-end runtime)
- `inference_request_errors_total` / `inference_requests_total`
- `runtime_oom_kills_total` (from runtime signal) + `container_restarts_total`
- `tokens_generated_total` / `gpu_seconds_total` (tokens/sec/gpu derived in dashboard)
- `scheduler_queue_wait_ms{quantile="0.95"}`

Neha Kapoor: we should be careful on “baseline” definition. for rollback thresholds, compare to the immediately previous deployment window (or 1h rolling median) not “yesterday” if traffic mix changes.

Sofia Ivanova: fair. suggest baseline = same pool, same model+route, previous 60 min (profile disabled) if available; otherwise previous 24h median for that cohort. dashboard already has both lines.

Sean Gallagher: ok now “fast path”: assume I’m oncall at 3am. p95 is up +15% in a canary pool. what do I do, exactly, and how long to take effect?

Kira Thompson: draft playbook (happy to paste into runbook once we agree):
1) Confirm profile is enabled and which profile selected (Profile selection dashboard)
2) If regression meets threshold, disable per pool:
   - set `runtime.profile_pack.enabled=false` (fleet config) OR set `RW_PROFILE_PACK_ENABLED=false` if we need immediate override
   - if still hot, force baseline: `RW_PROFILE_FORCE_PROFILE=baseline`
3) Trigger rolling restart in that pool (deployment controller)
4) Watch: p95/p99 + error rate + restarts for 15 min; if recovered, leave disabled + open incident follow-up
ETA: config push <2 min, rolling restart 5–12 min depending on pool size

Irene Choi: + permissioning: SRE oncall + infra oncall can flip pool config. we should NOT require runtime engineers.

Allison Grant: do we have a single “kill switch” we can document, not two different toggles? less confusion.

Benji Okafor: fleet config `runtime.profile_pack.enabled=false` is the standard. env var override is for “config plane is broken” or if we need to override precedence quickly. we can document both with “use A unless A fails”.

Aisha Bello: what about partial rollback (only B200 experimental) vs everything?

Ethan Park: per-profile gating exists:
- `runtime.profile_pack.allow_experimental=false`
- `RW_PROFILE_ALLOW_EXPERIMENTAL=false`
So you can keep H100/L40S profiles on, while disabling B200.

Neha Kapoor: add to rollback criteria: ANY canary incident where we flip kill switch => immediate pause on rollout until postmortem review. don’t keep marching.

Sean Gallagher: agreed.

Sofia Ivanova: one more nuance: tail latency spikes sometimes come from streaming hiccups. can we add “streaming disconnect rate” threshold?

Aisha Bello: yes please.

Allison Grant: +1. We’ve been paged for SSE disconnects that look like latency.

Benji Okafor: we have `streaming_client_disconnects_total` and `streaming_active_connections`. propose rollback if disconnect rate > 2x baseline for 15 min AND coincident p95 regression.

Sean Gallagher: ok. action items:
- Ethan/Kira T: add the thresholds + dashboard links + exact kill switch steps into the rollout runbook
- Allison/Aisha: map alert rules to these thresholds (so we page when it crosses)
- Irene/Vanessa: confirm pool config + rolling restart workflow is documented for Hosted/Dedicated
Target: EOD tomorrow so we can proceed with staged rollout next week.

Neha Kapoor: :thumbsup: please also include “who to page” and the expected comms in #incidents if rollback occurs.

Kira Thompson: ack, will update runbook draft and send for review.

Aisha Bello: thanks. once I see the runbook update I’ll sign off on the canary gating for SRE.

source.messages:
Rafael Mendes: quick pulse on taxonomy adoption (first week w/ the incident-bot required field). @Mei Lin can you drop the numbers + where people are getting stuck?

Mei Lin: yep — pulled from incident-bot + #incidents threads since enforcement started (Mon 09:00 PT -> today).

Mei Lin: totals: 41 incidents created (all severities).
- incident_type set at creation: 32/41 (78%)
- incident_type set by the time incident moved to “mitigated”: 37/41 (90%)
- incident_type + subtype both set by closure: 29/41 (71%)
- unknown/unclassified at closure: 8/41 (20%)

Mei Lin: top “unknown” reasons from the freeform notes field (n=8):
1) “capacity vs routing fallback misbehavior” (3)
2) “latency regression vs telemetry false positive” (2)
3) “streaming disconnects: client vs gateway vs runtime” (2)
4) “private connectivity: customer DNS/TLS vs our edge” (1)

Mei Lin: quick cut by tier (based on impacted env tagged in incident-bot):
- Hosted: 26 incidents, 85% typed by mitigate
- Dedicated: 9 incidents, 78% typed by mitigate (most unknowns here)
- Private: 6 incidents, 83% typed by mitigate

Aisha Bello: +1 this matches what I saw in the dashboard draft. the “unknown” bucket is too big to be useful if it stays ~20%.

Allison Grant: 20% unknown at closure is… not terrible for week 1, but we should treat it like an error budget. goal should be <5% within 4-6 wks.

Rafael Mendes: agreed. the “capacity vs routing” ambiguity is exactly the thing the decision tree was supposed to kill :grimacing:

Vanessa Ortiz: from Fleet POV, the overlap is real during regional events. people see 5xx + fallbacks + queueing and don’t know if primary is “capacity shortage” or “routing misbehavior”.

Mina Haddad: routing side: a lot of these are actually “fallback behaving as designed, but capacity is insufficient so customer still sees timeouts.” that should be “capacity/fleet shortage” w/ routing as contributing.

Kira Thompson: can we make that explicit in the decision tree? like a question: “is fallback selecting a compatible target and returning expected errors/latency under load?” -> if yes, primary is capacity.

Sergio Costa: I can add a panel to the adoption dashboard showing “unknown reason” freeform top terms, but we should reduce the need for freeform.

Dylan Brooks: on tooling: incident-bot currently allows `unknown` if you type `other` in the modal notes. we did that so people aren’t blocked, but it’s too easy.

Devon Price: we can gate it harder: require selecting a “best guess” top-level type, and allow subtype = `unknown` w/ reason. right now people are picking type=unknown.

Rafael Mendes: yes please. v1 policy was “type required unless force majeure / you’re literally mid-incident and bot is broken”. subtype can be unknown, but type shouldn’t.

Allison Grant: careful w/ the metrics labels too. if “unknown” becomes the escape hatch, it’ll dominate and we won’t learn anything. (but also don’t add 15 new subtypes to chase edge cases)

Mei Lin: for the “latency vs telemetry false positive” ones — the incident notes usually say “p95 up, but no user complaints / traces missing”. that seems like we should have a clearer split between:
- actual latency regression (customer impact)
- telemetry/metrics blind spot (instrumentation/collection)

Aisha Bello: decision tree tweak: first question should be “is there direct customer impact signal? (5xx, 429s, timeouts, complaint volume, synthetic failures)” vs “monitoring-only”. if monitoring-only, classify as telemetry/metrics blind spot unless proven otherwise.

Sofia Ivanova: streaming disconnects: two of those were actually client SDK changes per support thread. but during incident, oncall only sees “stream resets up”. maybe decision tree should say: if resets correlate w/ a specific user-agent/sdk version -> classify as “streaming disconnects / client-induced” (and owner Support/SDK?)

Rafael Mendes: reminder: taxonomy scope doesn’t have “SDK” as a primary owner. we can still classify as streaming disconnects, owner maybe Platform Edge/Gateway, and list SDK as contributing factor. but we need a rule.

Devon Price: incident-bot supports contributing systems field (freeform today). we can make that a dropdown list later, but for now we can add guidance text in the modal: “choose primary type based on where mitigation happens fastest.”

Vanessa Ortiz: +1 “where mitigation happens fastest” is a good heuristic. capacity events are mitigated by Fleet actions (shift capacity, throttle, provision, region shed). routing misbehavior is mitigated by config rollback / policy fix.

Mina Haddad: action item for decision tree: add a routing check:
- if router is selecting targets that violate policy (wrong region/model/tier) OR fallback_reason != expected -> routing/fallback misbehavior
- if fallback_reason=capacity/health and routing respects policy, then capacity/fleet shortage

Allison Grant: we should also log the “fallback_reason” enum more prominently in the incident-bot suggestion. otherwise folks won’t know to look.

Dylan Brooks: we can add a bot snippet: “Routing signals: fallback_reason distribution (capacity/health/policy/timeout)” with a link to the dashboard. I’ll file a follow-up.

Kira Thompson: for Private connectivity: the one unknown was “DNS/TLS errors after upgrade” — we should add a bullet under Private decision tree: “if errors are handshake/DNS and only 1 customer deployment, start with private connectivity/config; owner Private Deployments + Networking consulted.”

Aisha Bello: can we get targets for next week?
- type set by mitigation: 95%
- type+subtype by closure: 85%
- unknown at closure: <10%

Rafael Mendes: works for me. @Mei Lin can you keep a weekly adoption snapshot? and call out the top 3 ambiguous clusters.

Mei Lin: yep. I’ll automate a weekly paste (Mon mornings) + link the sheet.

Sergio Costa: dashboard link for folks: https://grafana.redwoodinference.net/d/incident-taxonomy/adoption (internal). still WIP, but adoption + unknown buckets are there.

Devon Price: bot changes I propose:
1) disallow incident_type=unknown (type required)
2) allow subtype=unknown but require “why” note
3) add helper text: “pick primary type; use contributing systems for secondary causes”
4) surface fallback_reason + top symptoms in suggestion card

Allison Grant: +1. also please add a lint that normalizes old types if people type variants. saw “controlplane” and “control-plane” in notes.

Dylan Brooks: noted. I’ll open a PR for (1)-(3) today, and a second PR for the fallback_reason snippet once routing metrics are stable.

Rafael Mendes: decision tree doc action items:
- add explicit fork for “routing behaving correctly but capacity insufficient” => capacity/fleet shortage
- add explicit fork for “monitoring-only latency” => telemetry/metrics blind spot unless proven customer impact
- add private connectivity bullet on DNS/TLS single-tenant vs multi-tenant
@Kira Thompson can you take first pass edits? I’ll review.

Kira Thompson: yep, I’ll patch the Confluence decision tree draft and drop it here for review by EOD.

Mina Haddad: I’ll write a short “routing vs capacity” example with screenshots (fallback_reason panel + what misbehavior looks like) so oncall has something concrete.

Vanessa Ortiz: same, I can add a “capacity event checklist” snippet (GPU saturation / provisioning lag / queue depth) to help folks classify quickly.

Aisha Bello: last ask: can we add a small postmortem review step for week 1/2? like SRE reviews classification for Sev0/Sev1 and corrects if needed.

Rafael Mendes: yes. for the next 2 weeks: SRE IC (or @Aisha Bello) will sanity check taxonomy fields on Sev0/Sev1 PMs. we’ll treat corrections as signal to improve decision tree, not blame.

Mei Lin: dropping the raw numbers export (internal): https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/incident-taxonomy-adoption-week1 (includes the 8 unknowns with notes).

Allison Grant: :thumbsup: thanks. let’s keep v1 small and fix the decision points rather than inventing new categories.

Rafael Mendes: +1. thanks all — next check-in same time next week.

Rafael Mendes: works for me. @Mei Lin can you keep a weekly adoption snapshot? and call out the top 3 ambiguous clusters.

Mei Lin: yep. I’ll automate a weekly paste (Mon mornings) + link the sheet.

Sergio Costa: dashboard link for folks: https://grafana.redwoodinference.net/d/incident-taxonomy/adoption (internal). still WIP, but adoption + unknown buckets are there.

Devon Price: bot changes I propose:
1) disallow incident_type=unknown (type required)
2) allow subtype=unknown but require “why” note
3) add helper text: “pick primary type; use contributing systems for secondary causes”
4) surface fallback_reason + top symptoms in suggestion card

Allison Grant: +1. also please add a lint that normalizes old types if people type variants. saw “controlplane” and “control-plane” in notes.

Dylan Brooks: noted. I’ll open a PR for (1)-(3) today, and a second PR for the fallback_reason snippet once routing metrics are stable.

Rafael Mendes: decision tree doc action items:
- add explicit fork for “routing behaving correctly but capacity insufficient” => capacity/fleet shortage
- add explicit fork for “monitoring-only latency” => telemetry/metrics blind spot unless proven customer impact
- add private connectivity bullet on DNS/TLS single-tenant vs multi-tenant
@Kira Thompson can you take first pass edits? I’ll review.

Kira Thompson: yep, I’ll patch the Confluence decision tree draft and drop it here for review by EOD.

Mina Haddad: I’ll write a short “routing vs capacity” example with screenshots (fallback_reason panel + what misbehavior looks like) so oncall has something concrete.

Vanessa Ortiz: same, I can add a “capacity event checklist” snippet (GPU saturation / provisioning lag / queue depth) to help folks classify quickly.

Aisha Bello: last ask: can we add a small postmortem review step for week 1/2? like SRE reviews classification for Sev0/Sev1 and corrects if needed.

Rafael Mendes: yes. for the next 2 weeks: SRE IC (or @Aisha Bello) will sanity check taxonomy fields on Sev0/Sev1 PMs. we’ll treat corrections as signal to improve decision tree, not blame.

Mei Lin: dropping the raw numbers export (internal): https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/incident-taxonomy-adoption-week1 (includes the 8 unknowns with notes).

Allison Grant: :thumbsup: thanks. let’s keep v1 small and fix the decision points rather than inventing new categories.

Rafael Mendes: +1. thanks all — next check-in same time next week.
text:
Rafael Mendes: quick pulse on taxonomy adoption (first week w/ the incident-bot required field). @Mei Lin can you drop the numbers + where people are getting stuck?

Mei Lin: yep — pulled from incident-bot + #incidents threads since enforcement started (Mon 09:00 PT -> today).

Mei Lin: totals: 41 incidents created (all severities).
- incident_type set at creation: 32/41 (78%)
- incident_type set by the time incident moved to “mitigated”: 37/41 (90%)
- incident_type + subtype both set by closure: 29/41 (71%)
- unknown/unclassified at closure: 8/41 (20%)

Mei Lin: top “unknown” reasons from the freeform notes field (n=8):
1) “capacity vs routing fallback misbehavior” (3)
2) “latency regression vs telemetry false positive” (2)
3) “streaming disconnects: client vs gateway vs runtime” (2)
4) “private connectivity: customer DNS/TLS vs our edge” (1)

Mei Lin: quick cut by tier (based on impacted env tagged in incident-bot):
- Hosted: 26 incidents, 85% typed by mitigate
- Dedicated: 9 incidents, 78% typed by mitigate (most unknowns here)
- Private: 6 incidents, 83% typed by mitigate

Aisha Bello: +1 this matches what I saw in the dashboard draft. the “unknown” bucket is too big to be useful if it stays ~20%.

Allison Grant: 20% unknown at closure is… not terrible for week 1, but we should treat it like an error budget. goal should be <5% within 4-6 wks.

Rafael Mendes: agreed. the “capacity vs routing” ambiguity is exactly the thing the decision tree was supposed to kill :grimacing:

Vanessa Ortiz: from Fleet POV, the overlap is real during regional events. people see 5xx + fallbacks + queueing and don’t know if primary is “capacity shortage” or “routing misbehavior”.

Mina Haddad: routing side: a lot of these are actually “fallback behaving as designed, but capacity is insufficient so customer still sees timeouts.” that should be “capacity/fleet shortage” w/ routing as contributing.

Kira Thompson: can we make that explicit in the decision tree? like a question: “is fallback selecting a compatible target and returning expected errors/latency under load?” -> if yes, primary is capacity.

Sergio Costa: I can add a panel to the adoption dashboard showing “unknown reason” freeform top terms, but we should reduce the need for freeform.

Dylan Brooks: on tooling: incident-bot currently allows `unknown` if you type `other` in the modal notes. we did that so people aren’t blocked, but it’s too easy.

Devon Price: we can gate it harder: require selecting a “best guess” top-level type, and allow subtype = `unknown` w/ reason. right now people are picking type=unknown.

Rafael Mendes: yes please. v1 policy was “type required unless force majeure / you’re literally mid-incident and bot is broken”. subtype can be unknown, but type shouldn’t.

Allison Grant: careful w/ the metrics labels too. if “unknown” becomes the escape hatch, it’ll dominate and we won’t learn anything. (but also don’t add 15 new subtypes to chase edge cases)

Mei Lin: for the “latency vs telemetry false positive” ones — the incident notes usually say “p95 up, but no user complaints / traces missing”. that seems like we should have a clearer split between:
- actual latency regression (customer impact)
- telemetry/metrics blind spot (instrumentation/collection)

Aisha Bello: decision tree tweak: first question should be “is there direct customer impact signal? (5xx, 429s, timeouts, complaint volume, synthetic failures)” vs “monitoring-only”. if monitoring-only, classify as telemetry/metrics blind spot unless proven otherwise.

Sofia Ivanova: streaming disconnects: two of those were actually client SDK changes per support thread. but during incident, oncall only sees “stream resets up”. maybe decision tree should say: if resets correlate w/ a specific user-agent/sdk version -> classify as “streaming disconnects / client-induced” (and owner Support/SDK?)

Rafael Mendes: reminder: taxonomy scope doesn’t have “SDK” as a primary owner. we can still classify as streaming disconnects, owner maybe Platform Edge/Gateway, and list SDK as contributing factor. but we need a rule.

Devon Price: incident-bot supports contributing systems field (freeform today). we can make that a dropdown list later, but for now we can add guidance text in the modal: “choose primary type based on where mitigation happens fastest.”

Vanessa Ortiz: +1 “where mitigation happens fastest” is a good heuristic. capacity events are mitigated by Fleet actions (shift capacity, throttle, provision, region shed). routing misbehavior is mitigated by config rollback / policy fix.

Mina Haddad: action item for decision tree: add a routing check:
- if router is selecting targets that violate policy (wrong region/model/tier) OR fallback_reason != expected -> routing/fallback misbehavior
- if fallback_reason=capacity/health and routing respects policy, then capacity/fleet shortage

Allison Grant: we should also log the “fallback_reason” enum more prominently in the incident-bot suggestion. otherwise folks won’t know to look.

Dylan Brooks: we can add a bot snippet: “Routing signals: fallback_reason distribution (capacity/health/policy/timeout)” with a link to the dashboard. I’ll file a follow-up.

Kira Thompson: for Private connectivity: the one unknown was “DNS/TLS errors after upgrade” — we should add a bullet under Private decision tree: “if errors are handshake/DNS and only 1 customer deployment, start with private connectivity/config; owner Private Deployments + Networking consulted.”

Aisha Bello: can we get targets for next week?
- type set by mitigation: 95%
- type+subtype by closure: 85%
- unknown at closure: <10%

Rafael Mendes: works for me. @Mei Lin can you keep a weekly adoption snapshot? and call out the top 3 ambiguous clusters.

Mei Lin: yep. I’ll automate a weekly paste (Mon mornings) + link the sheet.

Sergio Costa: dashboard link for folks: https://grafana.redwoodinference.net/d/incident-taxonomy/adoption (internal). still WIP, but adoption + unknown buckets are there.

Devon Price: bot changes I propose:
1) disallow incident_type=unknown (type required)
2) allow subtype=unknown but require “why” note
3) add helper text: “pick primary type; use contributing systems for secondary causes”
4) surface fallback_reason + top symptoms in suggestion card

Allison Grant: +1. also please add a lint that normalizes old types if people type variants. saw “controlplane” and “control-plane” in notes.

Dylan Brooks: noted. I’ll open a PR for (1)-(3) today, and a second PR for the fallback_reason snippet once routing metrics are stable.

Rafael Mendes: decision tree doc action items:
- add explicit fork for “routing behaving correctly but capacity insufficient” => capacity/fleet shortage
- add explicit fork for “monitoring-only latency” => telemetry/metrics blind spot unless proven customer impact
- add private connectivity bullet on DNS/TLS single-tenant vs multi-tenant
@Kira Thompson can you take first pass edits? I’ll review.

Kira Thompson: yep, I’ll patch the Confluence decision tree draft and drop it here for review by EOD.

Mina Haddad: I’ll write a short “routing vs capacity” example with screenshots (fallback_reason panel + what misbehavior looks like) so oncall has something concrete.

Vanessa Ortiz: same, I can add a “capacity event checklist” snippet (GPU saturation / provisioning lag / queue depth) to help folks classify quickly.

Aisha Bello: last ask: can we add a small postmortem review step for week 1/2? like SRE reviews classification for Sev0/Sev1 and corrects if needed.

Rafael Mendes: yes. for the next 2 weeks: SRE IC (or @Aisha Bello) will sanity check taxonomy fields on Sev0/Sev1 PMs. we’ll treat corrections as signal to improve decision tree, not blame.

Mei Lin: dropping the raw numbers export (internal): https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/incident-taxonomy-adoption-week1 (includes the 8 unknowns with notes).

Allison Grant: :thumbsup: thanks. let’s keep v1 small and fix the decision points rather than inventing new categories.

Rafael Mendes: +1. thanks all — next check-in same time next week.

source.messages:
Rafael Mendes: post-launch metrics check for hot-route protection (GA enablement window ended ~2h ago). Can folks sanity check: tail latency + 5xx for protected routes vs last week? any weirdness w/ shed/429 reason codes?

Sergio Costa: looking at the "Hot Route Protection - Enterprise" dashboard (us-east + eu-west). Early read is good.
- /v1/chat/completions enterprise p99: 3.9s -> 2.6s (24h rolling)
- /v1/embeddings enterprise p99: 1.3s -> 0.9s
- 5xx rate: down ~45% on protected routes (mostly timeouts)
Annotations line up w/ flag enablement.

Aisha Bello: +1. Error budget burn improved too.
- Chat enterprise SLO (99.9%): burn rate was ~2.1x during peaks last week; now hovering ~0.7x since rollout.
- Best-effort getting throttled earlier (expected), but seeing fewer fleet-wide latency spikes.
I’m not seeing any paging storms from the new alerts either (nice).

incident-bot: Overload protection activations (last 60m):
- region=us-east route_group=chat tier=best_effort action=admission_control events=7 max_shed_rate=18% duration_max=6m dashboard=https://grafana.redwood.ai/d/hot-route-protect
- region=eu-west route_group=embeddings tier=best_effort action=quota_gate events=2 max_429_rps=220 duration_max=2m
Runbook: https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/SRE/Runbook+Overload+Protection+and+Admission+Control

Logan Wright: telemetry view: overload_reason_code breakdown looks clean (no new high-cardinality offenders).
Top 429 reasons since GA:
- best-effort: OVERLOAD_ADMISSION (62%), QUOTA_EXCEEDED (34%), POLICY_DENY (4%)
- enterprise: QUOTA_EXCEEDED (88%), OVERLOAD_ADMISSION (8%), ROUTE_BUDGET (4%)
So enterprise is mostly quota, not overload shedding.

Rafael Mendes: love to see enterprise not getting punted. For best-effort 18% shed rate spikes — does that correlate w/ GPU saturation?

Vanessa Ortiz: checked fleet signals (us-east). Those 6m windows correspond to GPU util ~92-95% + queue depth crossing the trigger threshold. Autoscaler was already adding nodes but warmup lag meant the routing gate kicked in first.
Net effect: enterprise queue depth stays flat, best-effort queue depth stops growing (no cascade).

Ethan Park: gateway side looks stable. No increase in gateway CPU, policy eval cache hit rate ~97%.
We did see a small uptick in 429s overall (+12% vs baseline) but that’s concentrated in best-effort and matches the new behavior.

Aisha Bello: any reports from Support yet on “why am I getting more 429s”? I expect some, but want to make sure messaging points to retry/backoff + reason code headers.

Logan Wright: headers are present in samples I pulled:
`x-redwood-throttle-reason: overload_admission`
`retry-after: 1`
Also seeing the structured body in most clients. There are a few missing `retry-after` (looks like old edge pods?) — might be a rollout straggler.

Sergio Costa: I’ll add a panel to track “429 missing retry-after header” rate. If it’s >0.5% we should chase it.

Rafael Mendes: action items / follow-ups:
1) Create ticket to investigate missing `retry-after` on some 429s (assign gateway). We should not make Support guess.
2) Tune eu-west embeddings threshold slightly? We’re seeing quota_gate events; might be config mismatch or just traffic burst.
3) Add a short internal note for on-call: what shed % is “expected” before we page (so folks don’t panic).

Ethan Park: I’ll open (1) as ENG follow-up. Suspect some pods still on legacy throttle middleware. We can confirm by build hash.

Aisha Bello: for (3), I can PR the runbook w/ a “normal vs abnormal” section. E.g. best-effort shed up to 20% for <10m w/ stable enterprise p99 is OK; page if enterprise shed >1% sustained 5m or enterprise p99 burn >2x for 10m.

Vanessa Ortiz: on (2) — eu-west had a capacity dip earlier (one GPU pool drained for maintenance). If we’re stable now, I’d prefer we don’t loosen thresholds until we have a week of data. Maybe we just add an annotation for that maintenance window.

Sergio Costa: +1. Also seeing enterprise p95 stable in eu-west, so protections did what we wanted.

Rafael Mendes: cool. Can someone link the post-launch review ticket? want everything captured.

Aisha Bello: ENG-4931 is the post-launch metrics review + followups. I’ll comment with today’s deltas + these actions.

Logan Wright: I’ll also drop a note re: cardinality staying flat + the minor `retry-after` gap.

Rafael Mendes: thanks. If no fires by EOD, we can call this “healthy” and shift to the cleanup items. :thumbsup:

source.text:
Sean Gallagher: seeing a bunch of variance in the baseline sweeps this week (esp p99 TTFT). before we chase runtime ghosts, can we align on “benchmark fleet hygiene” + a standard warmup/isolation protocol?

Rina Kobayashi: +1. same commit / same manifest, but L40S runs in us-east swing ~8-12% tokens/s. feels like placement/noisy neighbor.

Claire Dubois: methodology-wise we need to be able to say “this delta is real” vs “this was a different box having a bad day”. what are the top suspected sources?

Allison Grant: in order of likelihood from past infra perf stuff:
- GPU clocks not pinned / auto-boost variance
- power limits inconsistent across hosts
- thermal throttling (esp if other jobs just ran)
- NUMA / CPU contention (shared host, bad pinning)
- storage/network noise if artifacts/logging is heavy mid-run
- kernel cache/warm cache effects (first run after deploy)

Aisha Bello: also: are we mixing MIG/non-MIG hosts in the same “SKU” bucket? that’s bitten us in SLO dashboards before.

Sean Gallagher: MIG should be off for benchmark fleet, but I don’t think we *assert* it anywhere.

Mateo Alvarez: we don’t. harness assumes “one job == one node”, but scheduler can still pack if the queue isn’t exclusive.

Sana Farid: from noise model side: we can tolerate some variance, but right now we’re seeing multimodal distributions (looks like two different regimes). that usually screams placement/clocking.

Rina Kobayashi: concrete example: H100 eu-west, chat-streaming workload, concurrency=32. Run A p50 TPOT ~28ms, Run B ~31ms. Both “pass” but p99 swings more.

Sean Gallagher: ok, proposal:
1) make benchmark fleet “exclusive” (no other workloads) + label/taint nodes
2) standard warmup steps for every run, and record them in manifest
3) pin GPU clocks + enforce persistence + log power/temps at start/end

Sergio Costa: for dashboards, if we can export “gpu_clock_locked=true/false” + “host_profile=bench” as tags, I can make a variance panel that breaks out by that.

Allison Grant: +1. also add “container co-tenancy count” if we can. even if you *intend* exclusivity, it’s good to assert.

Sean Gallagher: warmup protocol draft (tell me if this is sane):
- On node init:
  - `nvidia-smi -pm 1`
  - lock graphics+sm clocks to a known range (per SKU) via `nvidia-smi -lgc <min,max>`
  - set app clocks if supported / align power limit (`nvidia-smi -pl`), log the value
  - verify MIG disabled: `nvidia-smi -L` + check MIG mode
- Run warmup workload for 8-10 min:
  - use the *same model* as the actual run
  - fixed seed, moderate concurrency
  - discard metrics, but keep logs of achieved clock/temps
- Then execute measured trials (N>=10) with 60s cool-down between trials
- Capture at start/end of each trial: clocks, temps, power draw, CPU freq, and `numa` topology

Aisha Bello: +1 on start/end sampling. can we also sample once mid-run? we’ve seen clocks sag 3-4 min in.

Allison Grant: mid-run sampling is good, but keep it lightweight. `nvidia-smi dmon` in background to a local file (then upload at end) works.

Rina Kobayashi: how do we do cool-down? we don’t want to flush caches that are realistic, but we also don’t want thermal creep.

Claire Dubois: we should define what baseline intends: “warm steady-state” vs “cold start”. earlier thread decided we report both cold and warm scenarios, right?

Sean Gallagher: yeah. so:
- “cold” scenario: first measured trial after deploy + after clearing KV/prefix caches (harness-level)
- “warm” scenario: after warmup, steady-state
Thermal cool-down: just a short idle (60s) to stabilize, not a full cool-to-ambient.

Mateo Alvarez: +1. baseline is mostly steady-state serving. we can still publish cold TTFT separately.

Sana Farid: re: trial count, if we’re going to gate on small deltas, we need enough samples to estimate variance. N=10 might be borderline for p99.

Allison Grant: agree. p99 from N=10 is basically vibes unless each trial has lots of requests. maybe define in terms of “total requests observed per cohort” rather than trials.

Rina Kobayashi: current harness does ~3k requests per trial for chat streaming at those concurrencies. so N=5 already gives ~15k reqs.

Sana Farid: ok that’s better. can we just say: minimum 20k requests per cohort, and the harness can decide how many trials.

Sean Gallagher: works for me.

Sergio Costa: tagging ask for dashboarding:
- `benchmark_host_id`
- `gpu_uuid`
- `gpu_sku`
- `gpu_clock_lock` (actual min/max)
- `power_limit_watts`
- `mig_mode`
- `exclusive_node=true/false`
- `warmup_minutes`
- `warmup_workload_id`
