title: #eng-sre: 1763512044
preview:
Sean Gallagher: seeing a bunch of variance in the baseline sweeps this week (esp p99 TTFT). before we chase runtime ghosts, can we align on “benchmark fleet hygiene” + a standard warmup/isolation protocol?

Rina Kobayashi: +1. same commit / same manifest, but L40S runs in us-east swing ~8-12% tokens/s. feels like placement/noisy neighbor.

Claire Dubois: methodology-wise we need to be able to say “this delta is real” vs “this was a different box having a bad day”. what are the top suspected sources?

Allison Grant: in order of likelihood from past infra perf stuff:
- GPU clocks not pinned / auto-boost variance
- power limits inconsistent across hosts
- thermal throttling (esp if other jobs just ran)
- NUMA / CPU contention (shared host, bad pinning)
- storage/network noise if artifacts/logging is heavy mid-run
- kernel cache/warm cache effects (first run after deploy)

Aisha Bello: also: are we mixing MIG/non-MIG hosts in the same “SKU” bucket? that’s bitten us in SLO dashboards before.

Sean Gallagher: MIG should be off for benchmark fleet, but I don’t think we *assert* it anywhere.

Mateo Alvarez: we don’t. harness assumes “one job == one node”, but scheduler can still pack if the queue isn’t exclusive.

Sana Farid: from noise model side: we can tolerate some variance, but right now we’re seeing multimodal distributions (looks like two different regimes). that usually screams placement/clocking.

Rina Kobayashi: concrete example: H100 eu-west, chat-streaming workload, concurrency=32. Run A p50 TPOT ~28ms, Run B ~31ms. Both “pass” but p99 swings more.

Sean Gallagher: ok, proposal:
1) make benchmark fleet “exclusive” (no other workloads) + label/taint nodes
2) standard warmup steps for every run, and record them in manifest
3) pin GPU clocks + enforce persistence + log power/temps at start/end

Sergio Costa: for dashboards, if we can export “gpu_clock_locked=true/false” + “host_profile=bench” as tags, I can make a variance panel that breaks out by that.

Allison Grant: +1. also add “container co-tenancy count” if we can. even if you *intend* exclusivity, it’s good to assert.

Sean Gallagher: warmup protocol draft (tell me if this is sane):
- On node init:
  - `nvidia-smi -pm 1`
  - lock graphics+sm clocks to a known range (per SKU) via `nvidia-smi -lgc <min,max>`
  - set app clocks if supported / align power limit (`nvidia-smi -pl`), log the value
  - verify MIG disabled: `nvidia-smi -L` + check MIG mode
- Run warmup workload for 8-10 min:
  - use the *same model* as the actual run
  - fixed seed, moderate concurrency
  - discard metrics, but keep logs of achieved clock/temps
- Then execute measured trials (N>=10) with 60s cool-down between trials
- Capture at start/end of each trial: clocks, temps, power draw, CPU freq, and `numa` topology

Aisha Bello: +1 on start/end sampling. can we also sample once mid-run? we’ve seen clocks sag 3-4 min in.

Allison Grant: mid-run sampling is good, but keep it lightweight. `nvidia-smi dmon` in background to a local file (then upload at end) works.

Rina Kobayashi: how do we do cool-down? we don’t want to flush caches that are realistic, but we also don’t want thermal creep.

Claire Dubois: we should define what baseline intends: “warm steady-state” vs “cold start”. earlier thread decided we report both cold and warm scenarios, right?

Sean Gallagher: yeah. so:
- “cold” scenario: first measured trial after deploy + after clearing KV/prefix caches (harness-level)
- “warm” scenario: after warmup, steady-state
Thermal cool-down: just a short idle (60s) to stabilize, not a full cool-to-ambient.

Mateo Alvarez: +1. baseline is mostly steady-state serving. we can still publish cold TTFT separately.

Sana Farid: re: trial count, if we’re going to gate on small deltas, we need enough samples to estimate variance. N=10 might be borderline for p99.

Allison Grant: agree. p99 from N=10 is basically vibes unless each trial has lots of requests. maybe define in terms of “total requests observed per cohort” rather than trials.

Rina Kobayashi: current harness does ~3k requests per trial for chat streaming at those concurrencies. so N=5 already gives ~15k reqs.

Sana Farid: ok that’s better. can we just say: minimum 20k requests per cohort, and the harness can decide how many trials.

Sean Gallagher: works for me.

Sergio Costa: tagging ask for dashboarding:
- `benchmark_host_id`
- `gpu_uuid`
- `gpu_sku`
- `gpu_clock_lock` (actual min/max)
- `power_limit_watts`
- `mig_mode`
- `exclusive_node=true/false`
- `warmup_minutes`
- `warmup_workload_id`
If we have these in the run manifest and results export, I can slice variance quickly.

Allison Grant: also: CPU isolation. if we’re on k8s, we should request guaranteed QoS + CPU manager static policy + pinning. otherwise scheduler overhead itself is noise.

Sean Gallagher: yep. for benchmark queue we can enforce:
- `requests == limits` for CPU/mem
- `cpuManagerPolicy=static`
- `topologyManagerPolicy=restricted`
And for bare metal jobs, we can use cpusets.

Mateo Alvarez: will that be available in the benchmark cluster by early Q1?

Sean Gallagher: mostly, yes. we’ll need one cluster config change + a node pool dedicated to bench.

InfraBot: (auto) Change request created: BENCH-CLUSTER-1127 "Enable CPU manager static + topology restricted on benchmark node pool" (owner: Sean Gallagher)

Aisha Bello: placement: can we force “one job per node” with hard anti-affinity? I’m nervous we’ll *think* we’re exclusive and we’re not.

Sean Gallagher: yep. we can set a taint on bench nodes and tolerate only from bench jobs, and also enforce `podAntiAffinity` on hostname.

Rina Kobayashi: can we get a quick doc / checklist? right now every engineer is running “their own special ceremony” and it’s chaos.

Claire Dubois: +1. needs to be canonical in methodology. also needs to state what to do when runs violate the protocol.

Allison Grant: suggestion: “run rejected” criteria:
- clocks not locked
- MIG enabled
- more than 1 container on node
- gpu temp > threshold at start (e.g. >75C)
- power limit deviates from baseline for that SKU

Sean Gallagher: I’ll draft a runbook + add a preflight check that fails fast if those aren’t satisfied.

Sana Farid: please include the “accepted variance envelope” we’re using so people don’t rerun forever. if preflight passes and we still see weirdness, that’s a different escalation path.

Sergio Costa: if you can log all that preflight state as a single line JSON, we can parse into metrics easily.

Mateo Alvarez: action items?

Sean Gallagher: action items:
- Sean: write “Benchmark Fleet Hygiene” runbook + preflight checks (clocks/MIG/exclusivity) and propose cluster changes (taints/anti-affinity/CPU pinning)
- Rina: update harness to run warmup phase + record warmup metadata in manifest
- Sergio: add dashboard panels for variance by host/clock lock/power limit
- Sana/Claire: update methodology doc with min request counts + rejection criteria + how to classify noise vs regression

Rina Kobayashi: :thumbsup: I’ll start with warmup + host sampling. do we have SKU-specific clock lock values somewhere?

Allison Grant: we can derive from partner recs. for now, safe defaults:
- H100: lock to a stable band (don’t max out). we’ve used ~1.3–1.4GHz SM in other benches.
- A100/L40S similar. but we should confirm w/ the fleet configs.

Sean Gallagher: I’ll pull current fleet power/clock configs and post a table. if we need partner guidance, we have that Nvidia best-practices notes from Dec.

Aisha Bello: once this is in place, can we flag “noncompliant runs” in the run tracker sheet? would help triage.

Sergio Costa: yep, if the manifest has a `compliance_status=pass/fail` (or reasons), I can surface it.

Sean Gallagher: cool. I’ll post the doc draft in this thread later today.

source.content:
Sergio Costa: can we do a final pass on the enterprise hot-route dashboards + paging thresholds today? want to lock before GA enablement window.

Sergio Costa: links:
- Grafana "Hot Route Protection / Enterprise" (us-east + eu-west): https://grafana.redwood.dev/d/hotroute-enterprise
- Alert rules PR is merged, but thresholds are still editable in config: https://github.com/redwood/observability-pack/pull/1832

Rafael Mendes: yep. goal is: page when enterprise is actually at risk, *not* when best-effort is getting shed as designed.

Aisha Bello: +1. last canary we got close to paging storm when shed_rate jumped but enterprise p99 was fine.

Allison Grant: quick checklist:
1) latency panels are tier-sliced (enterprise vs best_effort)
2) error rate panels separate 429/quota vs 429/overload vs 5xx
3) annotations for protection activation (admission_control_on, shed_on)
4) burn alerts aligned to SLO doc

Logan Wright: telemetry side looks good. we’re emitting `overload_reason` with bounded set (quota, rate_limit, admission_control, shed_queue_depth, shed_gpu_sat, shed_scheduler_lag). no customer_id tags. :thumbsup:

Peter Holtz: question: are we paging on "admission control active" alone? because in steady state during mild pressure we expect it to flip for best_effort.

Sergio Costa: currently rule is:
- Page if `admission_control_active{tier="enterprise"}` > 0 for 10m
- Warn if `admission_control_active{tier="best_effort"}` > 0 for 10m
- Page if `shed_rate{tier="enterprise"}` > 0.5% for 5m
- Warn if `shed_rate{tier="best_effort"}` > 2% for 10m

Rafael Mendes: I’m not comfortable paging on enterprise admission control *alone* if it’s just gating new work to preserve SLO and not actually impacting success rate/latency. it’s a mitigation lever.

Aisha Bello: agree. admission control for enterprise should be rare, but paging should be tied to customer impact. can we make it page only if it correlates w/ SLO burn or elevated 5xx?

Allison Grant: suggestion (consistent w/ our standards):
- page on *symptoms* (latency SLO burn / 5xx)
- warn on *mitigations* (admission control active, best_effort shedding)

Vanessa Ortiz: from fleet side: admission_control may trip earlier if queue depth signal is noisy in one AZ. we saw that in eu-west canary. would rather not page on that alone.

Mina Haddad: routing note: enterprise admission control should basically mean "we’re out of budget". but in practice we may trip it for 1-2 mins during config propagation or capacity signal jitter.

Sergio Costa: ok. proposed change:
- Enterprise admission control: WARN (not page) if > 0 for 10m
- Page only if ALSO enterprise p99 latency above threshold OR 5xx above threshold.

Aisha Bello: what exact latency threshold are we using? right now dashboard shows p95/p99 and also "SLO p99" panel.

Sergio Costa: for protected routes in GA we’re targeting:
- /v1/chat/completions (enterprise): p99 < 2.5s (non-stream) and time-to-first-token p99 < 1.2s (stream)
- /v1/embeddings (enterprise): p99 < 900ms
- /v1/rerank (enterprise): p99 < 1.2s
(these align w/ the SLO page draft)

Allison Grant: careful: our paging alert should be SLO burn rate based, not hard p99 threshold only. p99 threshold is good for dashboard, but the alert should be burn.

Rafael Mendes: +1 burn-rate paging + a "hard stop" page for 5xx spikes.

Sergio Costa: current burn alerts:
- fast burn: 14x over 5m / 1h (page)
- slow burn: 2x over 1h / 6h (page)
for the enterprise route SLOs.

Aisha Bello: that’s fine. do we also have a 5xx absolute page? last incident was a cascade that manifested as 5xx before burn alert triggered.

Sergio Costa: there’s a rule: enterprise 5xx > 0.8% for 5m (page). but I’m unsure about 0.8% vs 0.5%.

Rafael Mendes: set it to 0.5% for 5m for enterprise *protected routes only*. that’s the point of this project.

Vanessa Ortiz: ok with 0.5%. if it’s noisy, we’ll adjust post-GA.

Peter Holtz: and 429s shouldn’t count in 5xx obviously. make sure the query excludes 429 and also excludes customer-canceled 499.

Logan Wright: confirm: `http_status=~"5.."` excludes 429/499. if we do `error_class="server"` that also excludes 499. current query uses status filter.

Allison Grant: we should also not page on best_effort 429 spikes. that’s expected.

Sergio Costa: yep. best_effort 429 overload spikes are annotated and warn-only.

Mina Haddad: do we have a page on "shed_rate enterprise > 0"? because even 0.2% enterprise shed might be a big deal for certain customers.

Aisha Bello: paging on >0 can page on tiny sample sizes. better to keep at 0.5%/5m plus burn.

Rafael Mendes: agree. also if enterprise shed is happening, the burn alert should catch it quickly.

Sergio Costa: proposal final thresholds:
P1 page:
- enterprise protected routes: 5xx rate > 0.5% for 5m
- enterprise SLO burn (fast 14x 5m/1h, slow 2x 1h/6h)
P2 warn:
- `admission_control_active{tier="enterprise"}` > 0 for 10m
- `shed_rate{tier="enterprise"}` > 0.2% for 10m
- `shed_rate{tier="best_effort"}` > 2% for 10m (no page)
- `policy_eval_p99` > 15ms for 10m (this was the one that bit us last week)

Allison Grant: I like it. add one more warn: "queue_depth above threshold w/ rising scheduler lag" so on-call can intervene before burn.

Logan Wright: we have `runtime_queue_depth` and `scheduler_lag_ms` panels already. can add an alert but make it warn only.

Rafael Mendes: +1 warn-only is fine.

Incident Bot: Reminder: GA enablement window scheduled for 2025-11-18 17:00–18:00 UTC. Runbook: https://confluence.redwood.dev/display/SRE/runbook-overload-protection-and-admission-control

Aisha Bello: can we also add a short note to the runbook about "expected" patterns during rollout?
- best_effort 429 increase
- enterprise shed should stay ~0
- admission control toggling briefly during flag flip is ok

Rafael Mendes: yes. Sergio can you add a section and link it in the go/no-go?

Sergio Costa: yep I’ll update runbook + dashboard annotations text today.

Mina Haddad: one more: for eu-west, the queue depth signal was more jittery; are thresholds region-specific or global?

Sergio Costa: currently global. but we can parameterize by region if needed. for GA I’d keep global, and we’ll tune post-launch if eu-west false warns.

Vanessa Ortiz: ok. please make sure alerts include region label so the page includes "eu-west" vs "us-east".

Logan Wright: alerts have `region` in labels. :thumbsup:

Allison Grant: please ensure we’re not labeling by model_version or route_path raw (cardinality). use route_group.

Logan Wright: confirmed: route_group is one of {chat_completions, embeddings, rerank}. no raw path label.

Rafael Mendes: decisions locked:
- enterprise page: 5xx > 0.5% / 5m, plus SLO burn (fast+slow)
- mitigation signals (admission_control, shed enterprise low) are warn-only unless tied to burn
- best_effort shedding never pages
Sergio to update runbook + alert config by EOD.

Sergio Costa: ack. will post in #eng-releases when updated + tag @Rafael Mendes for sign-off.

Aisha Bello: thanks. once updated I’ll do a quick "would this have paged" backtest vs the 11/03 incident.

Rafael Mendes: perfect. ship it.

Rafael Mendes: game day planning for hot-route overload sim (this week). goal: validate protections behave as designed (enterprise stays within SLO, best-effort sheds first + predictable 429s). can folks confirm who’s driving traffic + what we’re using to declare success/failure?

Aisha Bello: +1. also need clarity on what metrics we’re gating on. last time we had too many dashboards open and no single “stop/go” call.

Sergio Costa: i can own the SLO checks + scoreboard doc during the exercise.

Allison Grant: i’ll be observer + note taker, and can sanity check alerting behavior (no paging storms for expected best-effort shedding).

Rafael Mendes: propose roles:
- Driver (traffic / fault injection): Elena
- SLO/alerts operator: Sergio
- Runtime/scheduler watcher: Mateo
- Gateway/429 semantics watcher: Amara
- Fleet/capacity signals watcher: Vanessa
- Game master / go-no-go + comms: me
Thoughts?

Elena Popov: can drive. plan was k6 job from the loadtest runner in us-east, targeting `/v1/chat/completions` route group `chat.hot`.
- Phase 1: baseline at ~steady 60% of normal peak
- Phase 2: ramp to 140–160% for 15 min
- Phase 3: step function spike (2x) for 3 min
We’ll tag requests with `x-rw-sim: gameday-hrp-01` so we can filter.

Vanessa Ortiz: i can watch fleet signals. which region are we doing? (us-east only?) and which GPU pool?

Rafael Mendes: us-east only for this exercise. use hosted shared pool + protection flags enabled for allowlisted accounts. keep it contained.

Peter Holtz: reminder: if we start tripping admission control too hard, routing will aggressively gate best-effort first. expected, but we need to verify enterprise budget isn’t being impacted by queue depth thresholds.

Mateo Alvarez: i’ll watch scheduler lag + queue depth. can also keep an eye on long-prompt tail behavior (we saw p99 pop when prompt lengths drifted).

Amara Diallo: i’ll monitor 429 payload/headers + reason codes. for the exercise we should confirm:
- `x-redwood-throttle-reason: overload_admission` shows up for best-effort sheds
- enterprise should mostly be 200s; if 429 happens it should be `quota_*` not overload (unless we intentionally exceed enterprise budget)

Aisha Bello: can we write down explicit success criteria? like: “enterprise p95 < X ms, 5xx < Y%, error budget burn < Z for 30 min” etc.

Sergio Costa: yup. pulling from the enterprise SLO page:
- `/v1/chat/completions` enterprise: availability >= 99.9% (rolling 30d), latency SLO p95 <= 1.2s (per 5m window)
For game day we can use a shorter window but keep the same thresholds.
Proposed pass/fail gates during Phase 2+3:
PASS if:
1) enterprise p95 <= 1.2s and p99 <= 2.5s for duration of spike (allow 2 min warmup)
2) enterprise 5xx <= 0.2% (per 5m)
3) enterprise overload 429 rate <= 0.1% (should basically be 0)
4) best-effort sheds via 429 (overload) ramp up smoothly, no sustained 5xx burst
FAIL if:
- enterprise p95 > 1.2s for >5 min OR p99 > 2.5s for >5 min
- any enterprise 5xx burst >1% for 2 consecutive minutes
- protection triggers but we still see fleetwide latency cascade (p99 across pool blows out)

Allison Grant: + include “no unexpected paging”. burn-rate alerts should not page for best-effort shedding. if burn alerts page for enterprise, that’s a fail (or at least “needs tuning”).

Rafael Mendes: agreed. also: if we hit a weird intermediate state where enterprise stays green but we see policy eval latency spiking or cardinality issues, we should call it out as “pass w/ follow-ups”.

Elena Popov: confirming traffic shape: do we want to include a mix of sequence lengths? i can do:
- 70%: 300–600 prompt tokens
- 25%: 1.5k–2k prompt tokens
- 5%: 6k prompt tokens (to stress KV/cache)
And keep completion length capped at 256.

Mateo Alvarez: yes, that matches what tends to hurt scheduler lag. please keep the 6k prompts constant so we can correlate.

Vanessa Ortiz: what’s our safety lever if capacity signals go sideways? i can coordinate a temporary pool cap or scale-out, but ideally we don’t mask behavior.

Rafael Mendes: safety plan:
- hard stop if enterprise p99 > 3s for 3+ min OR enterprise 5xx > 1% sustained
- Elena ramps down load immediately
- if still degraded, we toggle off `hot_route_protection` flag (rollback) and page infra if needed
We’ll run during low-risk window, but still treat as a real incident if we destabilize.

Amara Diallo: can we ensure the allowlisted enterprise account is something we control (internal tenant)? i don’t want to accidentally impact a real customer.

Rafael Mendes: yes — use the internal enterprise test tenant `rw-ent-sim-01`. it’s already in the allowlist.

Aisha Bello: what dashboards are the “source of truth” during the exercise? please link.

Sergio Costa: primary:
- Grafana: Hot Route Protection / Enterprise scoreboard (us-east) https://grafana.redwood.example/d/hrp-enterprise-scoreboard
- Grafana: Shed reasons + 429 breakdown https://grafana.redwood.example/d/overload-shed-reasons
- SLO burn view (enterprise chat) https://slo.redwood.example/slo/chat-enterprise
I’ll keep a running checklist in the thread.

Allison Grant: also please annotate dashboards with `gameday-hrp-01` so post-exercise we can scrub the graphs quickly.

Peter Holtz: +1. and let’s explicitly validate “graceful degradation” semantics for streaming clients. we’ve had tickets about inconsistent termination.

Amara Diallo: i can run a tiny streaming client in parallel to confirm that shed responses terminate cleanly + include reason headers. i’ll report back in-thread.

Rafael Mendes: logistics:
- Date/time: Thu 10:00–11:00 PT (30 min setup, 20 min exercise, 10 min debrief)
- Comms: post in #incidents as “planned exercise” + notify oncall at start/end
- Recording: Allison captures notes + action items
Does anyone object?

Vanessa Ortiz: works. i’ll make sure fleet metrics are filtered to us-east and the right pool.

Mateo Alvarez: works.

Aisha Bello: works. can we clarify what “declare success” means at the end? i suggest:
- if all PASS gates met + no emergency rollback, we declare success
- if we had to roll back, declare fail
- if we meet enterprise SLO but see operational issues (e.g., noisy alerts), success w/ follow-ups

Rafael Mendes: yes. we’ll do a quick “green/yellow/red” at debrief:
- Green: enterprise SLO stable + expected shedding + no paging
- Yellow: enterprise stable but something needs tuning (alerts, thresholds, 429 semantics)
- Red: enterprise impacted or rollback needed

incident-bot: Reminder set by Rafael Mendes for Thu 10:00 PT: "Game day: hot-route overload simulation (HRP)"

Rafael Mendes: ok locking plan. Elena please share the k6 command/config in this thread by EOD + confirm it’s tagged. Sergio, can you prep the scoreboard checklist doc + paste the PASS/FAIL gates at the top?

Elena Popov: will do.

Sergio Costa: on it. :thumbsup:

Allison Grant: i’ll start a notes doc + will paste action items here after debrief.

source.messages:
Rafael Mendes: Heads up: starting **next week (Mon)**, `incident_type` will be **required** in incident-bot for all *new* incidents.

Rafael Mendes: TL;DR
- When you create an incident, the Slack modal will require **incident_type** (+ subtype where applicable)
- incident-bot will auto-resolve **primary owner team** from the mapping table (you can override if it’s wrong)
- You won’t be able to mark an incident **Resolved/Closed** without a type + owner (Sev0/Sev1 also needs postmortem link or placeholder)

Rafael Mendes: Why: we can’t keep doing “freeform tags” and then arguing after the fact. This is part of the Reliability Program push for consistent classification + ownership.

Rafael Mendes: Quick refs:
- v1 taxonomy defs + decision tree (WIP but usable): https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/SRE/Incident+taxonomy+v1
- owner mapping + remediation SLAs: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/SRE/Incident+ownership+mapping+SLA+matrix

Rafael Mendes: Rollout details:
- Applies to Hosted/Dedicated/Private incidents (customer-impacting + high-sev internal reliability incidents)
- Existing open incidents won’t be forced, but please add type when you have a moment
- First week: we’ll be watching “Unknown/Unclassified” volume and tightening the decision tree where it’s confusing

Rafael Mendes: Qs here. :eyes:

incident-bot: :information_source: Incident creation modal update scheduled for rollout window: Mon 09:00–10:00 PT. See change log: https://github.com/incident-bot/pr-812

Dylan Brooks: Adding: incident-bot will *suggest* a likely type based on initial symptoms (5xx spike, p95 latency, streaming resets, auth failures). You can accept or change. We’ll iterate on suggestions.

Devon Price: If you want to test it before Monday, DM me and I can enable the modal for you in #incident-bot-sandbox.

Kira Thompson: For folks asking “where do I find the type definitions mid-incident”: we’re adding a /runbook link section in the bot output keyed off incident_type.

Allison Grant: Friendly reminder: we kept top-level categories small on purpose. Please don’t try to encode specifics in subtype text (we want low-cardinality + stable).

Vanessa Ortiz: Question: for capacity events where we’re *also* seeing router fallbacks, which do we pick? Capacity shortage vs routing/fallback misbehavior?

Rafael Mendes: Good q. Rule for v1:
- Choose **primary** type based on what is gating mitigation.
- If mitigation is “add GPUs / move capacity / disable burst / regional rebalance” => **Capacity/Fleet shortage**
- If mitigation is “router is making the wrong choice (bad policy/health signal), or fallback is failing when it should work” => **Routing/Fallback misbehavior**

Rafael Mendes: Then record the other as *contributing factor* in the incident channel topic / postmortem. We’re not doing multi-primary types in v1.

Sofia Ivanova: +1. Similar for runtime regressions: if the problem is model runtime OOM / KV cache thrash / scheduler stall and fix is rollback runtime/model version => classify as **Serving runtime regression** even if it manifests as latency.

Aisha Bello: Q: when is it required to be set? At incident creation or can IC fill in later once we know more?

Devon Price: Required at creation (Slack modal won’t let you submit without it), but you can pick **Unknown/Unclassified** *temporarily*.

Devon Price: Follow-up enforcement: bot will nag to update type once signals come in (and you can’t close without a non-unknown type unless Rafael or Dylan does an override).

Rafael Mendes: And process expectation: IC (or delegate) should update from Unknown -> real type within **~15 min** once triage is underway.

Mei Lin: Q: do we want “Unknown” used for ambiguous early minutes? worried it’ll become the default.

Rafael Mendes: Yep, we’re watching that. If we see Unknown staying set through resolution, we’ll tighten:
- decision tree
- bot suggestions
- and we’ll follow up in postmortem review.

Allison Grant: Also: “Unknown” is *not* a dumping ground. If it’s clearly 5xx spike + overload, pick **Availability/Overload** even if you don’t know root cause.

Vanessa Ortiz: Another one: what about cloud provider incidents / region-wide outage? Is there a special type?

Rafael Mendes: For v1, use the closest operational symptom:
- If we can’t serve due to region-level infra degradation => **Region/Connectivity** (if networking) or **Capacity/Fleet shortage** (if it’s GPU provisioning / quota / fleet health)

Rafael Mendes: And note “external dependency” in the incident summary. SLAs have carve-outs for provider-wide events, but classification stays consistent.

Kira Thompson: We also updated the region failover runbook with “how to classify” examples (Fleet vs Routing vs Network): https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/INFRA/Region+failover+runbook

Aisha Bello: Q: does this change paging? e.g. if I pick a type owned by Routing, does it page Routing automatically?

Dylan Brooks: Not immediately for week 1. We’re plumbing type -> owner into paging in a follow-up. Right now it resolves owner + suggests escalation channel, but it won’t auto-repage unless you click the button.

Dylan Brooks: Goal is to avoid accidental repages when type is wrong in early minutes.

Sofia Ivanova: Q: can runtime on-call override owner when a subtype is mapped incorrectly? We’ve had some edge cases.

Devon Price: Yes. Modal shows the resolved owner and has an “Override owner” option w/ reason. Please also drop a note in #eng-platform if mapping is wrong so we can fix the config.

Allison Grant: + if you see a mapping that creates metric label explosions (someone tries to make a subtype per model), flag it. We’re enforcing allowlists.

Vanessa Ortiz: What about internal reliability incidents that don’t hit customers yet (e.g. telemetry blind spot, canary failure)? Still need type?

Rafael Mendes: If it triggers an incident channel + paging (Sev2+ typically), yes. Taxonomy includes **Telemetry/Metrics blind spot** specifically for that.

Aisha Bello: Q: what’s the minimum info needed to avoid being blocked? During a Sev0, we don’t want friction.

Devon Price: Minimum is top-level type. Subtype is required only for a handful of types (the modal will prompt). If you’re unsure, pick the nearest top-level bucket + add a one-liner in the summary.

Mei Lin: FYI we’ll be backfilling last 90 days to validate the category distribution. If you see weird buckets dominating, that’s a signal we need to refine.

Rafael Mendes: Last thing: office hours this Thu 14:00 PT in #eng-sre (30m). Bring your “this incident doesn’t fit” examples.

Rafael Mendes: Thanks all — please don’t DM me “can we add 40 more categories” :)

incident-bot: :white_check_mark: Reminder: Starting Mon, incident closure requires fields: `incident_type`, `owner`, and for Sev0/Sev1: `postmortem_link` (or tracking ticket).

source.content:
Aisha Bello: heads up: I think we introduced a metric cardinality regression w/ the new overload events metrics post-merge yesterday. Mimir ingestion is starting to complain + dashboards are timing out.

Aisha Bello: saw series count jump ~25-30x on `redwood_overload_event_total` + `redwood_request_outcome_total` for the protected route dashboards. new labels look suspicious: `route` is coming through as raw path (including some per-tenant prefixes?) and I’m also seeing `tenant_id` in the label set.

Sergio Costa: yep, confirms on my side. `/dashboards/hot-route-protection` panels are basically unusable rn (query times out for 6h window). I also see “too many series” warnings from the recording rules.

Allison Grant: we can’t have `tenant_id` (or anything request-specific) on a fleet-wide counter. metrics standard for overload events was: `route_group`, `tier`, `region`, `reason`, `priority_class` only. raw `route` label is also a no.

Caleb Johnson: +1. Mimir ruler logs:
```
level=warn msg="promql engine: query timed out" rule=hot_route:shed_rate_5m
level=error msg="ingester push: per-tenant series limit exceeded" tenant=prod
```

Aisha Bello: do we know which PR introduced this? I’m correlating it with the overload metrics rollout + the gateway 429 reason codes.

Logan Wright: digging. I think it came in via the runtime export + gateway wiring. the metric itself is fine but the label population is not bounded.

Logan Wright: I’m seeing `route` label set to the full request path at the gateway (e.g. `/v1/chat/completions?api_version=...`) and `tenant_id` from auth context leaking into the tags. That will explode.

Nadia Rahman: confirmed in telemetry pipeline too. there’s a new dimension called `customer` getting mapped to `tenant_id` before export. that should never have made it past review.

Allison Grant: ok, action items:
1) immediate stop-bleed: relabel drop at ingestion for `tenant_id` + `route` (raw) (or map `route` -> `route_group` if available).
2) code hotfix: emit `route_group` only + remove `tenant_id` label completely.
3) dashboards/recording rules: update queries to use `route_group` everywhere; add temporary compatibility panels if needed.

Rafael Mendes: can we do step (1) within the hour? I’d rather not page on this but we’re going to lose signal / alerts are already flapping.

Caleb Johnson: I can add a metric_relabel config to drop labels in the OTEL collector / prom agent path. It’s not perfect (we still ingest the series until the relabel is live), but it should cap growth immediately.

Peter Holtz: careful: dropping labels will create “missing series” for existing alert expressions (they currently match on `route`). we’ll need to patch alerts/recording rules basically at the same time.

Sergio Costa: I can do a quick PR to the dashboard pack + recording rules to shift to `route_group`. For the last ~12h, the data is kinda polluted but we can at least get the panels back.

Aisha Bello: ok plan:
- Caleb: add relabel drop `tenant_id` and normalize `route` -> `route_group` where present. if we can’t normalize, drop `route` entirely.
- Logan/Nadia: hotfix PR in redwood monorepo to remove `tenant_id` tag + ensure route attribution is by `route_group` (enum) not raw.
- Sergio: patch dashboards/alerts to not depend on raw `route`.

Logan Wright: ack. I’ll open a hotfix PR asap. likely change is in the gateway metric emitter + a small mapping helper used by overload events.

Nadia Rahman: also suggest adding a unit test / lint in metrics lib: reject labels matching `*id` unless explicitly allowlisted, and reject strings containing `/v1/` as a label value (raw route smell).

Allison Grant: +1. and please add a note to the metrics standard doc after this (we keep rediscovering this failure mode).

Caleb Johnson: pushing stop-bleed now. will roll to us-east first. ETA ~15m.

Sergio Costa: while that’s rolling, I’ll update the dashboard queries. I’ll also add an annotation panel: “data gap/label change during 1762912xxx hotfix window” so folks don’t over-interpret.

Peter Holtz: for “backfill dashboards”: we can’t backfill Prom series, but we *can* backfill the incident review charts via Clickhouse request logs (route_group + tier + 429 reason) for the last day. might be enough for postmortem + trend.

Aisha Bello: good call. let’s do that for the exec recap if needed.

Logan Wright: PR is up: removes `tenant_id` label and replaces `route` with `route_group` (derived from our route group config) for overload + outcome metrics. also added guardrail to drop querystring if route_group mapping fails.

Nadia Rahman: reviewing now. also adding a “cardinality budget” check in CI for metrics schema diffs (basic allowlist for labels).

Rafael Mendes: please prioritize rollout. Mimir is already throttling and we’re losing unrelated metrics.

deploy-bot: Deployed config change to drop labels [tenant_id, route] for overload metrics in us-east. (rollout: 25% -> 100%)

Caleb Johnson: us-east relabel drop is live. series growth stabilized within ~5m. starting eu-west next.

Sergio Costa: dashboard PR merged in observability-pack. panels now key off `route_group` + `tier`. still see a gap around the relabel deploy window but queries are fast again.

Allison Grant: great. once code hotfix lands, we can remove the relabel hack (but keeping drop on `tenant_id` forever is fine).

Aisha Bello: what’s the ETA on the code hotfix merge + rollout?

Logan Wright: merged to main, release train hotfix requested. should be in next canary deploy (couple hours). I’ll post when it’s in us-east.

Peter Holtz: reminder: SLO burn alerts may still be noisy until the recording rules catch up everywhere. I’ll silence the specific ones for 1h if needed.

Rafael Mendes: thanks all. once stable, can we add a short retro item: “cardinality review as part of overload metrics checklist” + link to this thread.

Aisha Bello: +1. I’ll file a follow-up in ENG backlog: guardrail + doc update + clickhouse backfill query for incident analytics.

Caleb Johnson: eu-west relabel drop complete.

deploy-bot: Deployed hotfix build to us-east canary: removed `tenant_id` metric label + switched `route` -> `route_group` for overload/outcome metrics.

Sergio Costa: dashboards look good now. I’ll keep an eye on query latency and series count overnight.

Allison Grant: closing the loop: once we confirm 24h stability, we can revert any temporary Mimir limits changes (if any) and mark the incident as “near miss”.

source.text:
Aisha Bello: heads up: since we flipped canary protection in us-east, we’re getting a lot of near-pages from the “overload shedding” alerts. This is expected best-effort 429s doing their job, but the alerts are basically instantaneous and noisy.

Rafael Mendes: yeah, on-call got pinged twice this morning. we need to make sure “best-effort getting shed” != page. the whole point is to keep enterprise stable.

Allison Grant: agree. we should separate:
1) notification/annotation when protection activates (for awareness)
2) paging only when enterprise SLO is at risk OR protection is behaving unexpectedly (e.g., enterprise being shed, or 5xx rises)

Sergio Costa: the current rule is basically `shed_rate_total > X for 5m` and it doesn’t filter tier. so any burst that triggers best-effort admission control flaps it.

Logan Wright: +1. we’re emitting `ri_overload_shed_requests_total{tier=...,route_group=...,reason=...}` and `ri_overload_admission_control_active{route_group=...}`. The alert should use tier filter and probably reason filter (quota vs overload). Also note: shedding == 429 with reason `overload`.

Peter Holtz: what do we want to page on exactly? for me:
- enterprise shed rate > 0.5% sustained (something’s wrong)
- enterprise p99 latency burn (SLO burn-rate)
- elevated 5xx on protected route groups
- protection active AND queue depth keeps climbing (i.e., not effective)

Sean Gallagher: +1, and let’s switch to burn-rate alerts for the SLOs. instantaneous thresholds are a recipe for paging storms during expected behavior.

Sergio Costa: we already have the SLO objects for protected routes in the toolkit PR. we can wire multi-window burn alerts:
- page: 2% error budget in 1h AND 5% in 6h (fast + slow)
- ticket: 2% in 6h AND 5% in 24h
…and exclude 429 overload for best-effort from error SLI.

Aisha Bello: careful: for enterprise SLI, 429 overload should count as failure (if we’re shedding enterprise, that violates “guaranteed behavior”). but for best-effort, 429 overload is expected and shouldn’t page.

Allison Grant: exactly. two different policies. we can keep a non-paging monitor like “best-effort shed rate high” -> post to #incidents as FYI with link to dashboard; but no pager.

Rafael Mendes: can we route that through incident-bot instead of paging?

incident-bot: Noted. I can post structured notifications for `overload_protection_active` transitions and include route_group, region, and dashboard/runbook links.

Logan Wright: metric-wise, we can do:
- NOTIFY: `sum by (route_group,region) (max_over_time(ri_overload_admission_control_active[5m])) == 1` (or detect 0->1 transition)
- PAGE (enterprise shed): `sum(rate(ri_overload_shed_requests_total{tier="enterprise",reason="overload"}[5m])) / sum(rate(ri_requests_total{tier="enterprise"}[5m])) > 0.005` for 10m
- PAGE (protection ineffective): protection active AND `queue_depth_p95` keeps rising for 15m

Peter Holtz: also add an inhibition rule: if “best-effort shedding high” is firing, suppress any “best-effort 429 rate” page-level alerts (if we keep any). but ideally there should be no page-level best-effort throttling alert at all.

Sergio Costa: right now we have a legacy “429 rate > 2%” alert on the gateway that’s tier-agnostic. we should either:
- scope it to enterprise only, OR
- convert it to ticket-level for best-effort with a higher threshold

Aisha Bello: please scope it. the last two pages were exactly that alert.

Allison Grant: proposal:
1) Delete/disable tier-agnostic 429 paging on protected route groups.
2) Add enterprise-only 429 overload paging (low threshold, longer sustain).
3) Keep best-effort shedding as Slack notification + dashboard annotation.
4) SLO burn-rate pages on enterprise latency + 5xx only.

Sean Gallagher: make sure we document this in the runbook too. on-call needs a clear “if you see protection activation notification, check enterprise SLO panel; no action needed if enterprise stable.”

Rafael Mendes: agreed. Sergio/Aisha can you update alert rules today and ping #eng-oncall? we’ve got another canary expansion tomorrow.

Sergio Costa: yep. I’ll push changes in observability-pack + update the paging routing. Also adding labels `tier` + `route_group` to the dashboard annotations.

Aisha Bello: I’ll handle the gateway 429 alert scoping (enterprise only) and verify it’s using `reason="overload"` vs quota.

Logan Wright: fyi: reason codes are `quota_exceeded`, `rate_limited`, `overload_shed`, `admission_denied`. For the throttling alert, you want `overload_shed` + `admission_denied` for enterprise. quota/rate limit should be separate (customer config / expected behavior).

Peter Holtz: also please set a minimum request volume condition so we don’t page on tiny denominators during low-traffic windows.

Sergio Costa: good call. adding `requests > 1000/5m` gate.

Allison Grant: once this lands, we should do a quick retro on false positives this week and tune thresholds. but the big win is burn-rate + tier scoping.

Rafael Mendes: :thumbsup: thanks all. post the final rule text here when ready.

Sergio Costa: draft page rule:
`(enterprise_overload_429_rate_5m > 0.005) AND (enterprise_requests_5m > 1000)` for 10m
and SLO page:
`burnrate(enterprise_route_latency,1h) > 5 AND burnrate(...,6h) > 2`
Will PR shortly.

Aisha Bello: ack. I’ll disable the tier-agnostic 429 pager once your PR merges, so we don’t leave a gap.

Sean Gallagher: loop me in when it’s deployed; I want to sanity check on-call noise tomorrow morning.

Rafael Mendes: cool. closing the loop after deploy.

source.messages:
Rafael Mendes: Heads up — sharing agenda + pre-reads for the incident taxonomy workshop. Goal is to leave with v1 categories + crisp definitions + ownership mapping approach (at least for the top ~10 types). Please skim pre-reads and drop comments inline before the session.

Rafael Mendes: When: Thu 12/12 10:00–11:30 PT
Where: Zoom https://redwood.zoom.us/j/94211860012 (passcode in calendar)

Rafael Mendes: Pre-read (10–15 min):
1) Confluence kickoff (scope + non-goals): https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/ENGSRE/Incident+taxonomy+and+ownership+mapping+-+Kickoff
2) Confluence draft taxonomy v1 (definitions + inclusion/exclusion): https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/ENGSRE/Incident+taxonomy+v1+definitions
3) Drive analysis (top incidents clustering, last 2 quarters): https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1SRE-top-incidents-analysis-last-2-quarters
4) Drive decision tree draft (WIP): https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1SRE-incident-type-decision-tree-draft

Rafael Mendes: Proposed agenda:
- 0–10: framing + what we need to decide today (bounded set, low cardinality)
- 10–35: top-level categories review (what stays / what merges)
- 35–55: definitions + inclusion/exclusion criteria (the "if you see X, pick Y" part)
- 55–70: ownership mapping rules (primary owner vs contributing) + disputed buckets
- 70–80: remediation SLA matrix inputs (severity x tier) + what needs PM approval
- 80–90: next steps: tooling integration + rollout / training plan

Rafael Mendes: Specifically looking for input on:
- overlap between "Capacity/Fleet shortage" vs "Routing/fallback misbehavior"
- where to place "Telemetry blind spots" (incident type vs contributing factor)
- streaming disconnects: gateway/network vs client behavior boundaries

Talia Benson: Thanks Rafael. Added the meeting to the reliability program calendar + invited the leads we discussed (Routing/Fleet/Obs/Runtime/CS). If anyone thinks we’re missing a stakeholder for v1 decisions, reply here.

Mei Lin: I’ll drop a quick slide into the workshop doc w/ the top 12 candidate clusters from the backfill sheet + % of total incidents. The ones that look like “misc/unknown” are mostly telemetry gaps or mixed-cause.

Allison Grant: +1 on the "bounded set" reminder. For the metric labels, please keep top-level incident_type to ~10–15 max and subtypes as an allowlist (not freeform). Otherwise we’ll regret it. :grimacing:

Aisha Bello: Re: agenda — can we reserve 5 mins to align on how this maps to SLOs (availability vs latency SLO) so I can wire it cleanly into the dashboards/error budget breakdown? Doesn’t need to be deep, just “which types count towards which SLO bucket.”

Rafael Mendes: Yep. I’ll tweak agenda: 75–80 SLO mapping sanity check (Aisha lead).

Kira Thompson: For pre-read, can we also link the runbook updates doc for region failover/capacity? It might help on the Fleet vs Routing debate.

Rafael Mendes: Good call. Adding:
5) Runbooks — region failover + capacity incident classification (draft): https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/ENGINFRA/Region+failover+incident+type+runbook+updates

Vanessa Ortiz: Re: overlap question — for me the key is “do we have GPUs and just routed weird” vs “we don’t have GPUs in region or provisioning is behind.” I’ll add a comment in the taxonomy doc w/ signals we can use (GPU saturation, pending provisioning, admission control). 

Mina Haddad: On the Routing side, I’d like to be careful that “fallback activated” isn’t automatically a routing incident. Fallback due to capacity health is expected behavior; routing incident is when fallback policy/selection is wrong or gets stuck flapping. I’ll annotate w/ examples.

Sofia Ivanova: Streaming disconnects: can we make sure the decision tree asks “are we seeing runtime restarts / gateway 499/502 / client SDK timeouts” so on-call isn’t guessing? I can bring 2 recent examples for boundary setting.

Dev Patel: From Support POV: anything we pick should be explainable in 1 sentence to customers. E.g., “capacity constraints in region” vs “routing policy misconfiguration.” If we call it “telemetry blind spot,” we need a customer-safe label or comms guidance.

Allison Grant: +1. Also: “telemetry blind spot” as primary type is fine when it materially blocks detection/mitigation (e.g., dashboards down during outage), but please make sure it’s not used as a catch-all. Maybe we force an “impact type” plus “telemetry issue” as contributing.

Rafael Mendes: Noted. Let’s add an explicit rule: pick primary incident_type by customer impact + system behavior; telemetry issues go in contributing_factors unless the incident is *primarily* loss of observability (e.g., false green / paging broken).

Talia Benson: Logistical: please add comments by EOD Wed so Rafael can bake them into the workshop doc. Also if you have contentious examples, paste a short timeline + what you think the classification should be.

Mei Lin: For people who want more detail, here’s the backfill sheet (last 90d) w/ proposed labels + confidence — still messy but good for workshop discussion: https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1SRE-classification-backfill-sheet-last-90-days

Aisha Bello: I’ll pull 2 charts (MTTR by symptom, top recurring types) and bring to the call. If anyone has must-have breakdowns, DM me.

Rafael Mendes: Perfect. See you all Thursday. And please don’t bikeshed names too hard — focus on decision tree clarity. :wink:

Allison Grant: :thumbsup:

Vanessa Ortiz: :thumbsup:

Mina Haddad: :thumbsup:

Vanessa Ortiz: Re: overlap question — for me the key is “do we have GPUs and just routed weird” vs “we don’t have GPUs in region or provisioning is behind.” I’ll add a comment in the taxonomy doc w/ signals we can use (GPU saturation, pending provisioning, admission control). 

Mina Haddad: On the Routing side, I’d like to be careful that “fallback activated” isn’t automatically a routing incident. Fallback due to capacity health is expected behavior; routing incident is when fallback policy/selection is wrong or gets stuck flapping. I’ll annotate w/ examples.

Sofia Ivanova: Streaming disconnects: can we make sure the decision tree asks “are we seeing runtime restarts / gateway 499/502 / client SDK timeouts” so on-call isn’t guessing? I can bring 2 recent examples for boundary setting.

Dev Patel: From Support POV: anything we pick should be explainable in 1 sentence to customers. E.g., “capacity constraints in region” vs “routing policy misconfiguration.” If we call it “telemetry blind spot,” we need a customer-safe label or comms guidance.

Allison Grant: +1. Also: “telemetry blind spot” as primary type is fine when it materially blocks detection/mitigation (e.g., dashboards down during outage), but please make sure it’s not used as a catch-all. Maybe we force an “impact type” plus “telemetry issue” as contributing.

Rafael Mendes: Noted. Let’s add an explicit rule: pick primary incident_type by customer impact + system behavior; telemetry issues go in contributing_factors unless the incident is *primarily* loss of observability (e.g., false green / paging broken).

Talia Benson: Logistical: please add comments by EOD Wed so Rafael can bake them into the workshop doc. Also if you have contentious examples, paste a short timeline + what you think the classification should be.

Mei Lin: For people who want more detail, here’s the backfill sheet (last 90d) w/ proposed labels + confidence — still messy but good for workshop discussion: https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1SRE-classification-backfill-sheet-last-90-days

Aisha Bello: I’ll pull 2 charts (MTTR by symptom, top recurring types) and bring to the call. If anyone has must-have breakdowns, DM me.

Rafael Mendes: Perfect. See you all Thursday. And please don’t bikeshed names too hard — focus on decision tree clarity. :wink:

Allison Grant: :thumbsup:

Vanessa Ortiz: :thumbsup:

Mina Haddad: :thumbsup:

Rafael Mendes: When: Thu 12/12 10:00–11:30 PT
Where: Zoom https://redwood.zoom.us/j/94211860012 (passcode in calendar)

Rafael Mendes: Pre-read (10–15 min):
1) Confluence kickoff (scope + non-goals): https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/ENGSRE/Incident+taxonomy+and+ownership+mapping+-+Kickoff
2) Confluence draft taxonomy v1 (definitions + inclusion/exclusion): https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/ENGSRE/Incident+taxonomy+v1+definitions
3) Drive analysis (top incidents clustering, last 2 quarters): https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1SRE-top-incidents-analysis-last-2-quarters

Rafael Mendes: Proposed agenda:
- 0–10: framing + what we need to decide today (bounded set, low cardinality)
- 10–35: top-level categories review (what stays / what merges)
- 35–55: definitions + inclusion/exclusion criteria (the "if you see X, pick Y" part)
- 55–70: ownership mapping rules (primary owner vs contributing) + disputed buckets
- 70–80: remediation SLA matrix inputs (severity x tier) + what needs PM approval
- 80–90: next steps: tooling integration + rollout / training plan

- overlap between "Capacity/Fleet shortage" vs "Routing/fallback misbehavior"
- where to place "Telemetry blind spots" (incident type vs contributing factor)
- streaming disconnects: gateway/network vs client behavior boundaries

source.text:
Allison Grant: heads up on the incident taxonomy work — adding `incident_type` as a label on request/route metrics is going to be a cardinality footgun if we’re not super strict.

Allison Grant: even if we say “top-level only”, in practice folks will want subtype, team, model, region, route, etc. and then Mimir/Prom storage + query perf gets spicy.

Rafael Mendes: +1, this came up in the kickoff. can you spell out the constraints we should bake into ENG-4826 so we don’t end up arguing per-PR?

Allison Grant: yep. a few concrete points:
- no freeform labels. no “stringly typed” `incident_type="${whatever}"` from the app.
- allowlist (enum) for `incident_type` values, owned centrally.
- `subtype` should NOT be a metric label on high-volume series. if we need it, put it in logs/span attributes.
- also avoid labeling *request-level* counters/histograms with incident_type. that implies every request knows the current incident classification (and makes cardinality explode when multiple incidents overlap).

Sergio Costa: wait, if we don’t put it on request metrics, how do we do the dashboards (errors by incident_type)? that was the whole point.

Aisha Bello: we can still do “incidents by type” from incident-bot / incident events, not by labeling every request. think: incident lifecycle metrics (opened/mitigated/resolved) tagged with type/owner/tier/sev.

Aisha Bello: for impact (5xx/latency) you join in the dashboard by time window + service/route, not by label.

Mei Lin: +1. for the last-90-days backfill i’m already clustering by symptom (5xx spike, p95 jump, stream resets) from existing metrics. we don’t need to permanently tag request metrics with taxonomy.

Sergio Costa: got it. so panel is like “incidents opened by type/owner” + another panel “error budget burn” and you correlate visually.

Allison Grant: exactly. and for deep dives you click through to the incident + pick the window, then overlay p95/5xx.

Dylan Brooks: from tooling side, we can still propagate `incident_type` in alert payloads + incident-bot fields, without pushing it into every metric series.

Dylan Brooks: also: if we *do* add `incident_type` anywhere in metrics, it should be a separate, low-QPS metric stream (incident events), not request errors.

Rafael Mendes: makes sense. what would an allowlist look like? who owns it + how do we keep it stable?

Allison Grant: proposal:
- single canonical enum for v1 top-level types (availability_5xx, latency, streaming, routing_fallback, capacity, control_plane, auth_rbac, telemetry_blind_spot, private_connectivity)
- `unknown` bucket required
- changes to enum require Observability review + SRE signoff (same as schema changes)

Devon Price: +1 on `unknown`. we need that anyway for incident-bot when folks can’t classify in the first 5 min.

Sergio Costa: would we enforce allowlist at ingestion? or in the app?

Allison Grant: both ideally. but minimum viable:
- in code: enum type, no strings
- in metrics pipeline: drop/normalize anything not in allowlist to `unknown` (and emit a warning log)

Rafael Mendes: if we normalize to unknown, do we lose signal that someone is doing something wrong?

Allison Grant: we can count “invalid label attempts” as a separate counter (no labels) and page the owning team if it spikes.

Aisha Bello: + a weekly report: “% unknown / % invalid” by oncall team. that’s a good forcing function without breaking storage.

Mei Lin: also please don’t make `owner_team` a label on metrics. people/org changes will churn. owner mapping should live in incident-bot/control-plane config.

Rafael Mendes: ok, action items:
1) Allison to drop a short writeup for cardinality guardrails + allowlist plan
2) Sergio/Aisha to update dashboard spec to rely on incident lifecycle metrics, not request labeling
3) Dylan/Devon to ensure incident-bot + alert payload plumbing uses same enum + unknown

Allison Grant: works for me. i’ll post in-thread a draft guardrails section today.

Sergio Costa: :thumbsup: thanks, this saves us from a slow-motion observability outage later.

Aisha Bello: :100: also +1 to “subtype in logs only”.

Allison Grant: one more thing: if anyone is thinking “we can just sample”… sampling doesn’t fix cardinality if labels are unbounded. allowlist is non-negotiable.

Rafael Mendes: :thumbsup: noted. we’ll bake it into the acceptance criteria for ENG-4826 so it’s not optional.

source.text:
Rafael Mendes: Kicking off incident learnings review from the 11/03 Sev1 (fleetwide latency cascade). We need to decide which routes qualify as “protected” in MVP so we can scope rate limits / priority routing. Can we align today?

incident-bot: :spiral_note_pad: Reference: 2025-11-03 Sev1 “fleetwide latency cascade from hot route” (postmortem draft linked in #incidents). Key symptom: p99 chat latency + elevated 5xx across shared pools.

Aisha Bello: +1. MVP needs to be small and very explicit. In the incident we basically got taken out by one or two hot paths + bursty tenants. If we pick too many routes, we’ll drown in config/alert tuning.

Sean Gallagher: Agree. Let’s anchor it to “routes that can degrade the whole fleet” + “routes with contractual enterprise SLOs”. That’s the safety case.

Allison Grant: From observability side, we also want to avoid high-cardinality per-model/per-tenant metrics as a prerequisite for MVP. Route group granularity is fine.

Rafael Mendes: Proposed initial candidates:
- `/v1/chat/completions`
- `/v1/embeddings`
- `/v1/rerank`
Possibly `/v1/completions` (legacy) but I’d prefer not unless we have strong evidence.

Mina Haddad: Routing POV: chat and embeddings are definitely the ones that saturate fastest. Rerank is smaller volume but can be spiky and has weird latency sensitivity depending on payload sizes.

Ethan Park: Gateway POV: we can enforce tier-aware limits cleanly if the route set is stable and low count. The more “special cases” we add, the more we risk surprise 429s.

Vanessa Ortiz: Fleet data from us-east last 2 weeks (rough): chat is ~55–65% of tokens, embeddings is ~20–30% QPS but cheap per req, still pressures scheduler due to concurrency. Rerank is low overall but certain enterprise tenants burst hard at top of hour.

Logan Wright: Telemetry we have today can break down by route + tier. We don’t reliably have “workload shape” tags without cardinality risk. So if we’re picking routes, make sure they’re separable at the gateway with low-cardinality labeling.

Aisha Bello: For MVP, I’d like to explicitly define “protected route = protected route group + enterprise tier class”. Best-effort traffic should shed earlier with consistent reasons.

Rafael Mendes: +1. The main decision today: which route groups are in scope for protection triggers and SLO dashboards.

Sean Gallagher: Criteria check:
1) High traffic share OR high tail impact on shared pools
2) Enterprise customers actively using it
3) Known overload failure mode where admission control helps
4) Clear customer semantics (429 vs 5xx)
Chat and embeddings are slam dunk. Rerank maybe.

Mina Haddad: On rerank: when it overloads, it tends to queue behind chat in shared pools and then we get weird feedback loops. Protecting rerank might be more about preventing it from stealing capacity from chat, not necessarily giving rerank strict guarantees.

Allison Grant: If rerank is in MVP, we need separate dashboards/panels. Otherwise it’ll be invisible and support will treat it as “random 429s”.

Ethan Park: Also note: rerank endpoint sometimes shares infra path with embeddings codepath (depending on service). If we exclude rerank, we need to make sure it doesn’t bypass protection and still cause capacity spikes.

Vanessa Ortiz: Another data point: in the Sev1, the triggering workload was chat with long prompts + streaming. Embeddings didn’t trigger it directly but it amplified queue depth when we were already hot.

Aisha Bello: That suggests we protect chat first and use embeddings as “early shed” under pressure so chat stays stable.

Rafael Mendes: So MVP “protected” could mean:
- Chat = protected (enterprise gets reserved budget/priority)
- Embeddings = controlled under pressure (enterprise may still have a budget but smaller)
- Rerank = optional, maybe “best-effort only” initially?

Sean Gallagher: Careful: “protected” terminology matters. We should decide whether “protected route” means “has explicit enterprise budget + paging” vs “has shedding rules.”

Allison Grant: +100. For dashboards/alerting, I want a crisp set of “enterprise-protected routes” that page when SLO burn is bad. Separate from “routes that get throttled during overload.”

Logan Wright: We can emit reason codes either way, but alerting should only page on enterprise-protected SLOs. Best-effort shedding should generate annotations, not pages.

Mina Haddad: Suggestion:
- Enterprise-protected: `/v1/chat/completions` only for MVP
- Overload-managed (shed early): `/v1/embeddings`, `/v1/rerank`, `/v1/completions` (legacy) but no paging
Then expand protected set after we see it work.

Aisha Bello: I like that. Less risk on alert storms.

Ethan Park: Gateway config is easier if we still list embeddings/rerank in route groups (so reason codes are consistent) but we only mark chat as `priority=enterprise_protected`.

Vanessa Ortiz: From fleet side, I’d also like embeddings to have a hard cap per tier in MVP. Otherwise we’ll keep seeing it “fill the cracks” during partial degradation.

Rafael Mendes: Sounds like consensus forming.

Sean Gallagher: Let’s write it down:
MVP Protected (enterprise SLO + paging):
- `/v1/chat/completions`
MVP Managed (tier-aware limits + shedding, no paging):
- `/v1/embeddings`
- `/v1/rerank`
- `/v1/completions` (legacy) only if it shares infra path; otherwise leave as-is

Allison Grant: + add explicit note: if we include legacy completions, we need to validate volume. Otherwise we risk wasting cycles.

Logan Wright: I can pull a quick metric snapshot for `/v1/completions` vs chat (QPS + tokens). If it’s <5% and mostly internal, we can skip.

Mina Haddad: For `/v1/embeddings`, do we treat enterprise as protected at all? Or just “less-shed than best-effort”? In the incident, embeddings wasn’t the customer complaint, chat was.

Aisha Bello: MVP: enterprise embeddings should get predictable 429s before it harms chat, but I’m not ready to promise embeddings SLO improvements as part of this rollout.

Rafael Mendes: Agree. We can document it as “enterprise embeddings prioritized relative to best-effort, but not an SLO-protected hot route in MVP.”

Ethan Park: Works. From gateway semantics, we can still send `429` with `x-redwood-retry-after-ms` and reason codes like `overload_shed` vs `quota_exceeded`.

Allison Grant: If you’re adding reason codes, please keep tag set minimal. No tenant_id, no prompt_length bucket explosions without review.

Logan Wright: Noted. We’ll stick to `route_group`, `tier`, `reason`, `region`, maybe `model_family` at most.

Vanessa Ortiz: Fleet signals: queue depth + scheduler lag are the best triggers. GPU saturation alone is noisy (esp. mixed batches). We should tie “managed routes shed” to queue depth threshold.

Mina Haddad: Routing can take a “pressure” signal and start denying best-effort first. For MVP, we can implement deterministic ordering: best-effort -> self-serve -> enterprise (last), and enterprise chat has reserved budget.

Aisha Bello: Action items?

Rafael Mendes: Capturing:
1) Logan to pull `/v1/completions` volume + impact and report back.
2) Allison + Aisha to draft MVP dashboard/alerting: only chat enterprise SLO pages; managed routes show shedding annotations.
3) Mina + Ethan to confirm route groups + policy labels for MVP (chat protected vs embeddings/rerank managed).
4) Vanessa to confirm recommended pressure signals + initial thresholds for us-east.

Sean Gallagher: Add: document the definition in Confluence so Support/CS doesn’t interpret “embeddings protected” incorrectly.

Rafael Mendes: Good call. I’ll add a short paragraph + post in #support once we’re aligned.

Allison Grant: :thumbsup: please loop me on the dashboard spec so we don’t reinvent panels later.

Aisha Bello: :thumbsup: let’s keep MVP tight, we can expand after game day.

Rafael Mendes: Thanks all. I’ll summarize decision + drop it in the project channel / Linear epic.

Aisha Bello: +1. MVP needs to be small and very explicit. In the incident we basically got taken out by one or two hot paths + bursty tenants. If we pick too many routes, we’ll drown in config/alert tuning.

Sean Gallagher: Agree. Let’s anchor it to “routes that can degrade the whole fleet” + “routes with contractual enterprise SLOs”. That’s the safety case.

Allison Grant: From observability side, we also want to avoid high-cardinality per-model/per-tenant metrics as a prerequisite for MVP. Route group granularity is fine.

Rafael Mendes: Proposed initial candidates:
- `/v1/chat/completions`
- `/v1/embeddings`
- `/v1/rerank`
Possibly `/v1/completions` (legacy) but I’d prefer not unless we have strong evidence.

Mina Haddad: Routing POV: chat and embeddings are definitely the ones that saturate fastest. Rerank is smaller volume but can be spiky and has weird latency sensitivity depending on payload sizes.

Ethan Park: Gateway POV: we can enforce tier-aware limits cleanly if the route set is stable and low count. The more “special cases” we add, the more we risk surprise 429s.

Vanessa Ortiz: Fleet data from us-east last 2 weeks (rough): chat is ~55–65% of tokens, embeddings is ~20–30% QPS but cheap per req, still pressures scheduler due to concurrency. Rerank is low overall but certain enterprise tenants burst hard at top of hour.

Logan Wright: Telemetry we have today can break down by route + tier. We don’t reliably have “workload shape” tags without cardinality risk. So if we’re picking routes, make sure they’re separable at the gateway with low-cardinality labeling.

Aisha Bello: For MVP, I’d like to explicitly define “protected route = protected route group + enterprise tier class”. Best-effort traffic should shed earlier with consistent reasons.

Rafael Mendes: +1. The main decision today: which route groups are in scope for protection triggers and SLO dashboards.

Sean Gallagher: Criteria check:
1) High traffic share OR high tail impact on shared pools
2) Enterprise customers actively using it
3) Known overload failure mode where admission control helps
4) Clear customer semantics (429 vs 5xx)
Chat and embeddings are slam dunk. Rerank maybe.

Mina Haddad: On rerank: when it overloads, it tends to queue behind chat in shared pools and then we get weird feedback loops. Protecting rerank might be more about preventing it from stealing capacity from chat, not necessarily giving rerank strict guarantees.

Allison Grant: If rerank is in MVP, we need separate dashboards/panels. Otherwise it’ll be invisible and support will treat it as “random 429s”.

Ethan Park: Also note: rerank endpoint sometimes shares infra path with embeddings codepath (depending on service). If we exclude rerank, we need to make sure it doesn’t bypass protection and still cause capacity spikes.

Vanessa Ortiz: Another data point: in the Sev1, the triggering workload was chat with long prompts + streaming. Embeddings didn’t trigger it directly but it amplified queue depth when we were already hot.

Aisha Bello: That suggests we protect chat first and use embeddings as “early shed” under pressure so chat stays stable.

Rafael Mendes: So MVP “protected” could mean:
- Chat = protected (enterprise gets reserved budget/priority)
- Embeddings = controlled under pressure (enterprise may still have a budget but smaller)
- Rerank = optional, maybe “best-effort only” initially?

Sean Gallagher: Careful: “protected” terminology matters. We should decide whether “protected route” means “has explicit enterprise budget + paging” vs “has shedding rules.”

Allison Grant: +100. For dashboards/alerting, I want a crisp set of “enterprise-protected routes” that page when SLO burn is bad. Separate from “routes that get throttled during overload.”

Logan Wright: We can emit reason codes either way, but alerting should only page on enterprise-protected SLOs. Best-effort shedding should generate annotations, not pages.

Mina Haddad: Suggestion:
- Enterprise-protected: `/v1/chat/completions` only for MVP
- Overload-managed (shed early): `/v1/embeddings`, `/v1/rerank`, `/v1/completions` (legacy) but no paging
Then expand protected set after we see it work.

Aisha Bello: I like that. Less risk on alert storms.

Ethan Park: Gateway config is easier if we still list embeddings/rerank in route groups (so reason codes are consistent) but we only mark chat as `priority=enterprise_protected`.

Vanessa Ortiz: From fleet side, I’d also like embeddings to have a hard cap per tier in MVP. Otherwise we’ll keep seeing it “fill the cracks” during partial degradation.

Rafael Mendes: Sounds like consensus forming.

Sean Gallagher: Let’s write it down:
MVP Protected (enterprise SLO + paging):
- `/v1/chat/completions`
MVP Managed (tier-aware limits + shedding, no paging):
- `/v1/embeddings`
- `/v1/rerank`
- `/v1/completions` (legacy) only if it shares infra path; otherwise leave as-is

Allison Grant: + add explicit note: if we include legacy completions, we need to validate volume. Otherwise we risk wasting cycles.

Logan Wright: I can pull a quick metric snapshot for `/v1/completions` vs chat (QPS + tokens). If it’s <5% and mostly internal, we can skip.

Mina Haddad: For `/v1/embeddings`, do we treat enterprise as protected at all? Or just “less-shed than best-effort”? In the incident, embeddings wasn’t the customer complaint, chat was.

Aisha Bello: MVP: enterprise embeddings should get predictable 429s before it harms chat, but I’m not ready to promise embeddings SLO improvements as part of this rollout.

Rafael Mendes: Agree. We can document it as “enterprise embeddings prioritized relative to best-effort, but not an SLO-protected hot route in MVP.”

Ethan Park: Works. From gateway semantics, we can still send `429` with `x-redwood-retry-after-ms` and reason codes like `overload_shed` vs `quota_exceeded`.

Allison Grant: If you’re adding reason codes, please keep tag set minimal. No tenant_id, no prompt_length bucket explosions without review.

Logan Wright: Noted. We’ll stick to `route_group`, `tier`, `reason`, `region`, maybe `model_family` at most.

Vanessa Ortiz: Fleet signals: queue depth + scheduler lag are the best triggers. GPU saturation alone is noisy (esp. mixed batches). We should tie “managed routes shed” to queue depth threshold.

Mina Haddad: Routing can take a “pressure” signal and start denying best-effort first. For MVP, we can implement deterministic ordering: best-effort -> self-serve -> enterprise (last), and enterprise chat has reserved budget.

Aisha Bello: Action items?

Rafael Mendes: Capturing:
1) Logan to pull `/v1/completions` volume + impact and report back.
2) Allison + Aisha to draft MVP dashboard/alerting: only chat enterprise SLO pages; managed routes show shedding annotations.
3) Mina + Ethan to confirm route groups + policy labels for MVP (chat protected vs embeddings/rerank managed).
4) Vanessa to confirm recommended pressure signals + initial thresholds for us-east.

Sean Gallagher: Add: document the definition in Confluence so Support/CS doesn’t interpret “embeddings protected” incorrectly.

Rafael Mendes: Good call. I’ll add a short paragraph + post in #support once we’re aligned.

Allison Grant: :thumbsup: please loop me on the dashboard spec so we don’t reinvent panels later.

Aisha Bello: :thumbsup: let’s keep MVP tight, we can expand after game day.

Rafael Mendes: Thanks all. I’ll summarize decision + drop it in the project channel / Linear epic.

source.messages:
Rafael Mendes: Need to land remediation SLAs today-ish for the taxonomy doc. Main open q: what’s *realistic* for Sev1 in Dedicated (TTM + TTF). Also: do we carve out cloud-provider outages / upstream capacity events? Looking for opinions + data.

Lauren Bishop: Dedicated customers are going to hold us to whatever we publish internally, even if not 1:1 in contract. If we say “30m mitigate” for Sev1 Dedicated we will miss it a lot.

Vanessa Ortiz: +1. Sev1 Dedicated is often capacity/fleet in a specific region or cluster. Sometimes mitigation is “move traffic / add burst / swap pool” which can be 45-90m depending on GPU availability.

Harish Reddy: For Dedicated, mitigation frequently requires provisioning (or rebalancing) which is not instant. If we set <60m, we’ll create churn and “SLA breach alert fatigue.”

Aisha Bello: Can we define Sev1 Dedicated TTM as 60m *for customer-impacting errors/overload*, but allow “clock pause” for confirmed upstream? Or is that too squishy?

Nikhil Sharma: Caution on “pause the clock.” We should keep it simple for ICs. If we add carve-outs, needs a checkbox + explicit criteria, otherwise people will use it as an escape hatch.

Mei Lin: Pulled quick numbers from last ~2 quarters (Dedicated Sev1-ish): median time to “customer sees improvement” was ~52m, p75 ~88m. That’s including a couple gnarly regional capacity days.

Rafael Mendes: That’s helpful. Do you have count / sample size?

Mei Lin: 17 incidents tagged Dedicated customer-impacting with Sev1/“high” in the notes. It’s messy b/c we didn’t consistently capture mitigate vs resolve, but I used first sustained drop in 5xx/429 + CS note “recovered” as proxy.

Lauren Bishop: Based on that, 60m seems *barely* doable if we’re disciplined. But we need to be clear what “mitigate” means. For Dedicated, mitigation might be “stabilize with rate-limit / shed load + communicate capacity recovery ETA.”

Rachel Kim: +1 define it. Also, for Dedicated, sometimes “mitigation” is moving the tenant to a different pool (which is a bigger hammer). Are we comfortable that as a standard mitigation expectation?

Vanessa Ortiz: It’s not always possible, but for Sev1 we should at least have it in the playbook as an option. Sometimes you can’t if the tenant has hard region pinning.

Sean Gallagher: The SLA should drive the behavior we want. For Sev1 Dedicated: expect fast stabilization + a clear plan. I’d rather set 60m and miss occasionally than set 120m and accept slow response.

Aisha Bello: Maybe split the TTM definition:
- TTM = time to *stop active harm* (5xx/429/timeout trend reverses OR traffic is intentionally limited with customer approval)
- TTF = time to full recovery/root fix
… but that might be too wordy for the matrix.

Rafael Mendes: Wordy is ok in Confluence, but the incident-bot UI needs to be crisp.

Fiona McCarthy: On carve-outs: if we add “cloud provider outage” carve-out, define it narrowly. Also watch for cases that start as “provider issue” but are actually us (quota misconfig, bad fallback policy). We shouldn’t pause clocks based on hunch.

Nikhil Sharma: +100. If we do carve-out, require evidence link (AWS/GCP status page, provider ticket #, or internal infra confirmation).

Harish Reddy: For capacity events caused by cloud capacity, mitigation is still on us (shift regions, reduce load, reroute). So I’d say: no carve-out for mitigate. Maybe carve-out only for fix?

Lauren Bishop: That aligns with what CS tells customers anyway: “we’re mitigating now; root cause may be upstream.”

Rachel Kim: Agreed. Carve-out for TTF only, because full fix might be “wait for provider to restore capacity,” but mitigation should be “reduce impact.”

Vanessa Ortiz: If we do that, it needs a taxonomy subtype for the reporting: e.g. `Capacity/Fleet > Upstream capacity constraint` or similar, otherwise we’ll bury it.

Rafael Mendes: That’s in-scope (capacity/fleet shortages). We can add a “upstream constraint” flag as contributing factor, but we’re trying to keep taxonomy low-cardinality.

Aisha Bello: We can keep taxonomy stable and use an incident field like `external_dependency=true` (boolean) for carve-out logic. That’s not a metric label.

incident-bot: (context) Proposed fields in incident-bot: `severity`, `tier`, `incident_type`, `incident_subtype`, `owner_team`, `external_dependency`.

Nikhil Sharma: If we ship `external_dependency`, make it require a link + approval (maybe SRE-oncall or IC?) otherwise it’s going to get overused.

Sean Gallagher: +1 approval gate for external_dependency pause. IC can request; SRE duty can approve.

Rafael Mendes: Ok, let’s talk numbers.
Proposal draft:
- Sev1 Dedicated TTM: 60 minutes
- Sev1 Dedicated TTF: 72 hours (3 days)
Carve-out: if `external_dependency=true` approved, TTF clock pauses; TTM does *not* pause.
Thoughts?

Lauren Bishop: 72h TTF for Sev1 Dedicated feels aggressive for issues that require code changes + staged rollout. But maybe that’s the point.

Vanessa Ortiz: For fleet/capacity, “fix” can be “add capacity permanently / adjust pool policy.” That can be <72h if we treat it as priority. But if it’s a hardware swap / supplier lead time, impossible.

Harish Reddy: For those, the “fix” might be “re-baseline commitment + implement guardrails” vs “provider ships more H100s.” So TTF definition matters.

Aisha Bello: In SLO-land we usually define fix as “prevent recurrence at same scale within 30 days” which is too fuzzy. For this program, maybe TTF = “production change deployed OR config change applied that removes the triggering condition.” For pure upstream capacity, that’s where pause comes in.

Rachel Kim: Customer expectation: if it’s Sev1, we want either a rollback or a config mitigation same day, and a clear corrective action plan within a couple days. 72h seems defensible if we allow external_dependency pause.

Mei Lin: Historical: time to “permanent fix” (as documented in postmortem) for Dedicated Sev1 is usually 2-6 days. 72h will tighten the loop.

Fiona McCarthy: On upstream outages, make sure comms language is aligned: “investigating elevated error rates; underlying cloud provider degradation suspected/confirmed” and avoid definitive statements early.

Nikhil Sharma: One more: define what counts as cloud-provider outage. If AWS status is green but we have a support case, does that qualify? Probably yes, with ticket #.

Rafael Mendes: Sounds like:
- `external_dependency=true` criteria: (a) provider status incident *or* (b) provider support ticket opened + Infra/Fleet confirms symptoms consistent with upstream + no evidence of our own regression.
- Approval: SRE duty or incident commander + SRE-oncall ack.

Lauren Bishop: Works for me. Also please route this through PM-912 so we don’t surprise Sales/CS with internal expectations.

Sean Gallagher: :thumbsup: Let’s ship v1 with those numbers and revisit after 30 days of data.

Rafael Mendes: Cool. I’ll update the SLA matrix draft with Sev1 Dedicated TTM=60m, TTF=72h, and carve-out as discussed (pause TTF only, approval + evidence). Will link here for final eyes.

Aisha Bello: When you paste the link, tag me—I’ll sanity-check against breach alerting (ENG-4829).

Vanessa Ortiz: Tag Fleet too; want to make sure we’re not setting ourselves up for “always breach” during regional capacity constraints.

Rafael Mendes: will do.
