Vanessa Ortiz: Capacity-wise, GPUs were fine. This was mostly web/origin load + CDN miss.

Sean Gallagher: What we changed during incident:
1) Hotfixed marketing site to NOT send `Authorization` header to `benchmarks` endpoint.
2) Updated CDN behavior to ignore `Authorization` for cache key on that path and force-cache with safe TTL.
3) Purged bad variants + prewarmed top regions.

Neha Kapoor: Also adjusted origin to return `Cache-Control: public, max-age=300, stale-while-revalidate=600` for the public results artifact, regardless of auth context.

Logan Wright: Added logging at edge/origin to break down cache HIT/MISS by path + presence of auth header. (We didn’t have clean attribution before.)

Sean Gallagher: Action items (please reply if you own one / want to take):
- [P0] Make results endpoint hard-public and strip auth at the server (ignore Authorization header entirely) so we can’t regress via client behavior. Owner: ??
- [P0] Add automated canary that simulates “logged-in browser” headers/cookies and asserts edge cache HIT rate > X%. Owner: SRE (Sean) w/ Web (Felix)
- [P1] Add dashboard tiles: CDN hit ratio for benchmark artifacts, origin RPS, origin queue depth, and error budget burn during launches. Owner: Logan
- [P1] Add a runbook section for “cache miss storm” + purge/prewarm steps + decision tree. Owner: Sean
- [P1] Confirm with Security/Compliance that forcing cache on these public artifacts is acceptable + document in data retention policy (no sensitive IDs). Owner: Neha to loop Naomi
- [P2] Add circuit breaker in marketing site: if results fetch is slow/fails, fall back to a static snapshot + show banner. Owner: Felix

Felix Schneider: I can take the P2 circuit breaker + also add a unit test that blocks adding auth headers to `benchmarks.*` routes.

Logan Wright: I’ll take the dashboard tiles. I’m missing one metric: CDN hit ratio per POP. I can get overall hit ratio today; per-POP might take a bit.

Neha Kapoor: I’ll ping Naomi re: caching posture + make sure the redaction checklist covers this path. Agree P0 should be server-side ignore Authorization.

Sean Gallagher: I’ll own the canary + runbook updates. For the P0 server-side strip/ignore auth: looping in platform folks separately, but if anyone here has cycles we should do it ASAP before next wave of traffic.

Vanessa Ortiz: For prewarm: do we want to schedule it 15 min before planned campaigns/webinar starts? Could be a simple job hitting the artifact per region.

Sean Gallagher: Yes. Let’s add that as a P1: scheduled prewarm for top 3 regions + after any artifact version bump. (I’ll add to the runbook + we can implement once the caching is fully deterministic.)

Omar El-Sayed: One more note: the cache key mismatch also impacted our perf charts because retries were amplifying load. Maybe add `Retry-After` + backoff guidance for the web fetch.

Felix Schneider: Makes sense. I can add exponential backoff + cap retries to 1 for this fetch.

Sean Gallagher: Cool. I’ll write up the short incident summary in the formal retro doc, but wanted to capture the root cause + fixes here. Please add anything I missed.
text:
Sean Gallagher: Post-incident quick notes while it’s fresh (benchmark landing page latency / intermittent 5xx during launch traffic).

Sean Gallagher: TL;DR root cause: CDN cache config + cache key mismatch led to a cache-miss storm after deploy. We expected the benchmark JSON payload to be edge-cached, but requests were effectively going to origin for most page loads.

Incident Bot: Incident INC-1187 status changed: MONITORING -> RESOLVED (owner: @sean.g)

Neha Kapoor: +1. Specifically: CDN rule for `/benchmarks/results.json` wasn’t honoring `stale-while-revalidate` because origin was returning `Cache-Control: private, max-age=0` for a subset of responses (when auth header present). Those ended up uncacheable at the edge.

Logan Wright: The auth header was coming from the marketing site fetch wrapper. Even though results endpoint is “public”, the wrapper was attaching `Authorization: Bearer <anon>` in some browsers (leftover from logged-in console session). That caused CDN to bypass caching + also created distinct cache keys (Vary: Authorization) = basically unbounded variants.

Felix Schneider: Confirmed in HARs. If you’ve ever logged into Console in that browser, the request picked up the console auth token cookie and our fetch layer set Authorization.

Omar El-Sayed: That also explains why synthetic checks looked fine (no auth headers), but real users got hammered.

Sean Gallagher: Impact summary (rough):
- ~12 min elevated p95 on landing page (4–9s, some timeouts)
- spikes of 5xx from origin due to saturation + queueing
- increased 429s briefly when we tightened origin rate limit as a stopgap

Vanessa Ortiz: Capacity-wise, GPUs were fine. This was mostly web/origin load + CDN miss.

Sean Gallagher: What we changed during incident:
1) Hotfixed marketing site to NOT send `Authorization` header to `benchmarks` endpoint.
2) Updated CDN behavior to ignore `Authorization` for cache key on that path and force-cache with safe TTL.
3) Purged bad variants + prewarmed top regions.

Neha Kapoor: Also adjusted origin to return `Cache-Control: public, max-age=300, stale-while-revalidate=600` for the public results artifact, regardless of auth context.

Logan Wright: Added logging at edge/origin to break down cache HIT/MISS by path + presence of auth header. (We didn’t have clean attribution before.)

Sean Gallagher: Action items (please reply if you own one / want to take):
- [P0] Make results endpoint hard-public and strip auth at the server (ignore Authorization header entirely) so we can’t regress via client behavior. Owner: ??
- [P0] Add automated canary that simulates “logged-in browser” headers/cookies and asserts edge cache HIT rate > X%. Owner: SRE (Sean) w/ Web (Felix)
- [P1] Add dashboard tiles: CDN hit ratio for benchmark artifacts, origin RPS, origin queue depth, and error budget burn during launches. Owner: Logan
- [P1] Add a runbook section for “cache miss storm” + purge/prewarm steps + decision tree. Owner: Sean
- [P1] Confirm with Security/Compliance that forcing cache on these public artifacts is acceptable + document in data retention policy (no sensitive IDs). Owner: Neha to loop Naomi
- [P2] Add circuit breaker in marketing site: if results fetch is slow/fails, fall back to a static snapshot + show banner. Owner: Felix

Felix Schneider: I can take the P2 circuit breaker + also add a unit test that blocks adding auth headers to `benchmarks.*` routes.

Logan Wright: I’ll take the dashboard tiles. I’m missing one metric: CDN hit ratio per POP. I can get overall hit ratio today; per-POP might take a bit.

Neha Kapoor: I’ll ping Naomi re: caching posture + make sure the redaction checklist covers this path. Agree P0 should be server-side ignore Authorization.

Sean Gallagher: I’ll own the canary + runbook updates. For the P0 server-side strip/ignore auth: looping in platform folks separately, but if anyone here has cycles we should do it ASAP before next wave of traffic.

Vanessa Ortiz: For prewarm: do we want to schedule it 15 min before planned campaigns/webinar starts? Could be a simple job hitting the artifact per region.

Sean Gallagher: Yes. Let’s add that as a P1: scheduled prewarm for top 3 regions + after any artifact version bump. (I’ll add to the runbook + we can implement once the caching is fully deterministic.)

Omar El-Sayed: One more note: the cache key mismatch also impacted our perf charts because retries were amplifying load. Maybe add `Retry-After` + backoff guidance for the web fetch.

Felix Schneider: Makes sense. I can add exponential backoff + cap retries to 1 for this fetch.

Sean Gallagher: Cool. I’ll write up the short incident summary in the formal retro doc, but wanted to capture the root cause + fixes here. Please add anything I missed.

source.messages:
Sean Gallagher: Heads up — we need launch-week dashboards + alerts for the benchmark landing page + interactive table before EOD tomorrow. Focus: page latency, 5xx, CDN error rates. Also any cache/origin weirdness since we’re serving results JSON.

Sean Gallagher: @Neha Kapoor can you sanity-check alert thresholds / oncall coverage? @Felix Schneider want to confirm the key endpoints + what “good” looks like for the UX.

Neha Kapoor: yep. Let’s keep it tight + actionable. Primary is end-user experience: page load + table data fetch. Secondary: origin health + CDN.

Felix Schneider: endpoints:
- marketing site page: `/benchmarks/latency-per-dollar` (plus `/benchmarks` redirect)
- results fetch from site hits `GET /api/benchmarks/v1/public/latest.json` (served by the benchmark results service + cached)
- CSV export: `GET /api/benchmarks/v1/public/latest.csv`

Felix Schneider: UI is pretty unhappy if JSON fetch p95 > ~800ms (table feels “stuck”). Page itself should be <2s TTFB ideally.

Vanessa Ortiz: FYI traffic expectation from Marketing is “spiky” during email + socials. Also some APAC partner blast. We reserved some headroom but let’s alert early on cache miss rate so we don’t melt the origin.

Logan Wright: for metrics, we’ve got:
- CDN (Cloudflare) logs into Datadog: `cf.edge.status`, `cf.edge.ttfb_ms`, `cf.cache_status` tagged by `path`.
- Origin (marketing web) APM: `web.request` w/ `route`.
- Results service: `bench_results.request` + Redis cache hit/miss.

Logan Wright: can build a single dashboard with 3 sections: (1) end-to-end (CDN), (2) origin (marketing web), (3) results service/cache.

Tyler Benson: +1. Also can we add a simple “launch health” tile for Marketing ops? Something like p95 TTFB + 5xx rate + cache hit %. I can link it in the launch war-room channel.

Sean Gallagher: cool. Proposal for alerting (Datadog monitors -> PagerDuty SRE oncall):
1) CDN edge 5xx for benchmark paths
2) Origin 5xx for benchmark paths
3) CDN edge TTFB p95 elevated
4) Results JSON endpoint latency p95 elevated
5) Cache hit ratio drops (miss storm indicator)

Sean Gallagher: @Logan Wright can you draft the exact queries? I’ll wire PagerDuty routing.

Datadog Bot: Sean Gallagher created dashboard “Benchmark Launch — Web + CDN Health”

Logan Wright: draft monitors (all filtered to benchmark traffic only):

Logan Wright: 1) CDN edge 5xx rate (Cloudflare)
Query:
`(sum:cf.edge.requests{path:/benchmarks*,env:prod}.as_count() - sum:cf.edge.requests{path:/benchmarks*,env:prod,status:2xx}.as_count() - sum:cf.edge.requests{path:/benchmarks*,env:prod,status:3xx}.as_count() - sum:cf.edge.requests{path:/benchmarks*,env:prod,status:4xx}.as_count()) / sum:cf.edge.requests{path:/benchmarks*,env:prod}.as_count()`
Threshold: > 0.5% for 5m (warn), > 1% for 5m (alert)

Logan Wright: 2) CDN error responses specifically 524/522 (origin timeout)
Query:
`sum:cf.edge.requests{path:/benchmarks*,env:prod,status:522}.as_count() + sum:cf.edge.requests{path:/benchmarks*,env:prod,status:524}.as_count()`
Threshold: > 20 in 5m (warn), > 50 in 5m (alert)

Alicia Perez: for the landing page, can we also watch Core Web Vitals? Not sure we have that fully instrumented but at least RUM LCP/TTFB would be great.

Logan Wright: we have RUM for the marketing site. Adding to dashboard (not paging):
- RUM: `view.loading_time` p75 for view `benchmarks-latency-per-dollar`
- RUM: `resource.duration` for `latest.json` fetch

Logan Wright: 3) CDN edge TTFB p95
Query:
`p95:cf.edge.ttfb_ms{path:/benchmarks*,env:prod}`
Threshold: > 1200ms for 10m (warn), > 2000ms for 10m (alert)

Logan Wright: 4) Results JSON endpoint latency p95 (APM)
Query:
`p95:bench_results.request.duration{route:GET_/api/benchmarks/v1/public/latest.json,env:prod}`
Threshold: > 600ms for 10m (warn), > 1000ms for 10m (alert)

Logan Wright: 5) Cache hit ratio
We emit `bench_results.cache.hit` and `.miss` counters.
Query:
`sum:bench_results.cache.hit{env:prod}.as_count() / (sum:bench_results.cache.hit{env:prod}.as_count() + sum:bench_results.cache.miss{env:prod}.as_count())`
Threshold: < 0.90 for 5m (warn), < 0.80 for 5m (alert)

Neha Kapoor: thresholds look reasonable. For paging: I’d page on (a) elevated 5xx OR (b) sustained p95 TTFB > 2s OR (c) cache hit < 80%. Warns can stay Slack-only.

Sean Gallagher: +1. We should also catch “silent badness” where we’re 200s but super slow. So TTFB alert paging seems fine.

Harish Reddy: any chance we’ll see 429s from WAF/rate limit during spikes? That’ll look like “broken” to users.

Felix Schneider: if we start rate limiting benchmark JSON too aggressively, the table will fail. Can we monitor 429 separately so we can tune quickly?

Logan Wright: yep add non-paging monitor for 429 rate:
`sum:cf.edge.requests{path:/benchmarks*,env:prod,status:429}.as_count()`
and for the JSON endpoint specifically:
`sum:cf.edge.requests{path:/api/benchmarks/v1/public/latest.json,env:prod,status:429}.as_count()`

Sean Gallagher: 429 monitor should notify eng-sre + eng-platform (not PD) unless it’s huge.

PagerDuty: Sean Gallagher updated service “SRE On-Call” routing rule: source=Datadog monitors tagged `benchmark_launch:true`

Neha Kapoor: also please add a synthetic check from 3 geos (us-east, eu-west, apac) that hits:
- GET landing page
- GET latest.json
and validates HTTP 200 + body size > some minimum (avoid empty responses).

Logan Wright: can do. We can assert `latest.json` body contains `schema_version` string.

Sean Gallagher: nice. @Felix Schneider what key should we check? `schema_version` exists?

Felix Schneider: yep `"schema_version": "1."` should be present. Also `generated_at`.

Logan Wright: adding synthetics:
- “Benchmark landing page (HTML)”
- “Benchmark results JSON (public)”
Locations: AWS N. Virginia, Frankfurt, Singapore
Run every 1 min, alert on 2 consecutive failures.

Alicia Perez: can we get a “CDN cache status breakdown” chart? If cache starts missing, we’ll want to see it quickly.

Logan Wright: added widget: stack of cache statuses for `path:/api/benchmarks/v1/public/*` -> HIT/MISS/BYPASS.

Sean Gallagher: cool. Last bit: comms.
- Dashboard link goes to #marketing + #announcements?
- Paging goes to SRE oncall only.

Tyler Benson: I’ll post dashboard link in launch war-room (#marketing) + add to run-of-show doc. Please also drop quick “how to read” notes (what’s normal vs bad) so non-eng folks don’t panic.

Neha Kapoor: +1. Minimal legend:
- green: p95 TTFB < 1.2s
- yellow: 1.2–2.0s
- red: >2.0s or 5xx >1%

Sean Gallagher: action items:
- @Logan Wright finalize monitors + synthetics, tag `benchmark_launch:true`
- @Sean Gallagher connect monitors to PD, add Slack notif to #eng-sre
- @Tyler Benson share dashboard link + legend with Marketing

Datadog Bot: Logan Wright created monitor “Benchmark Launch: CDN 5xx rate” (tags: benchmark_launch:true)
Datadog Bot: Logan Wright created monitor “Benchmark Launch: CDN p95 TTFB” (tags: benchmark_launch:true)
Datadog Bot: Logan Wright created monitor “Benchmark Launch: Results JSON p95 latency” (tags: benchmark_launch:true)
Datadog Bot: Logan Wright created monitor “Benchmark Launch: Cache hit ratio low” (tags: benchmark_launch:true)
Datadog Bot: Logan Wright created synthetic test “Benchmark results JSON (public)”
Datadog Bot: Logan Wright created synthetic test “Benchmark landing page (HTML)”

Sean Gallagher: :thumbsup: thanks all. Let’s do a quick dry run tomorrow morning — we can temporarily drop thresholds to confirm PD pages correctly, then revert.

Neha Kapoor: please do it outside of peak. And add a note in the oncall handoff: if cache hit drops, first move is to increase CDN TTL / prewarm, not to scale origin blindly.

Felix Schneider: I can add a manual prewarm job (curl latest.json from a few POPs) if we want, but hopefully the cache holds.

Sean Gallagher: let’s keep prewarm as fallback. For now, dashboards + alerts are the priority. Closing loop tomorrow.

- RUM: `view.loading_time` p75 for view `benchmarks-latency-per-dollar`
- RUM: `resource.duration` for `latest.json` fetch

Logan Wright: 3) CDN edge TTFB p95
Query:
`p95:cf.edge.ttfb_ms{path:/benchmarks*,env:prod}`
Threshold: > 1200ms for 10m (warn), > 2000ms for 10m (alert)

Logan Wright: 4) Results JSON endpoint latency p95 (APM)
Query:
`p95:bench_results.request.duration{route:GET_/api/benchmarks/v1/public/latest.json,env:prod}`
Threshold: > 600ms for 10m (warn), > 1000ms for 10m (alert)

Logan Wright: 5) Cache hit ratio
We emit `bench_results.cache.hit` and `.miss` counters.
Query:
`sum:bench_results.cache.hit{env:prod}.as_count() / (sum:bench_results.cache.hit{env:prod}.as_count() + sum:bench_results.cache.miss{env:prod}.as_count())`
Threshold: < 0.90 for 5m (warn), < 0.80 for 5m (alert)

Neha Kapoor: thresholds look reasonable. For paging: I’d page on (a) elevated 5xx OR (b) sustained p95 TTFB > 2s OR (c) cache hit < 80%. Warns can stay Slack-only.

Sean Gallagher: +1. We should also catch “silent badness” where we’re 200s but super slow. So TTFB alert paging seems fine.

Harish Reddy: any chance we’ll see 429s from WAF/rate limit during spikes? That’ll look like “broken” to users.

Felix Schneider: if we start rate limiting benchmark JSON too aggressively, the table will fail. Can we monitor 429 separately so we can tune quickly?

Logan Wright: yep add non-paging monitor for 429 rate:
`sum:cf.edge.requests{path:/benchmarks*,env:prod,status:429}.as_count()`
and for the JSON endpoint specifically:
`sum:cf.edge.requests{path:/api/benchmarks/v1/public/latest.json,env:prod,status:429}.as_count()`

Sean Gallagher: 429 monitor should notify eng-sre + eng-platform (not PD) unless it’s huge.

PagerDuty: Sean Gallagher updated service “SRE On-Call” routing rule: source=Datadog monitors tagged `benchmark_launch:true`

Neha Kapoor: also please add a synthetic check from 3 geos (us-east, eu-west, apac) that hits:
- GET landing page
- GET latest.json
and validates HTTP 200 + body size > some minimum (avoid empty responses).

Logan Wright: can do. We can assert `latest.json` body contains `schema_version` string.

Sean Gallagher: nice. @Felix Schneider what key should we check? `schema_version` exists?

Felix Schneider: yep `"schema_version": "1."` should be present. Also `generated_at`.

Logan Wright: adding synthetics:
- “Benchmark landing page (HTML)”
- “Benchmark results JSON (public)”
Locations: AWS N. Virginia, Frankfurt, Singapore
Run every 1 min, alert on 2 consecutive failures.

Alicia Perez: can we get a “CDN cache status breakdown” chart? If cache starts missing, we’ll want to see it quickly.

Logan Wright: added widget: stack of cache statuses for `path:/api/benchmarks/v1/public/*` -> HIT/MISS/BYPASS.

Sean Gallagher: cool. Last bit: comms.
- Dashboard link goes to #marketing + #announcements?
- Paging goes to SRE oncall only.

Tyler Benson: I’ll post dashboard link in launch war-room (#marketing) + add to run-of-show doc. Please also drop quick “how to read” notes (what’s normal vs bad) so non-eng folks don’t panic.

Neha Kapoor: +1. Minimal legend:
- green: p95 TTFB < 1.2s
- yellow: 1.2–2.0s
- red: >2.0s or 5xx >1%

Sean Gallagher: action items:
- @Logan Wright finalize monitors + synthetics, tag `benchmark_launch:true`
- @Sean Gallagher connect monitors to PD, add Slack notif to #eng-sre
- @Tyler Benson share dashboard link + legend with Marketing

Datadog Bot: Logan Wright created monitor “Benchmark Launch: CDN 5xx rate” (tags: benchmark_launch:true)
Datadog Bot: Logan Wright created monitor “Benchmark Launch: CDN p95 TTFB” (tags: benchmark_launch:true)
Datadog Bot: Logan Wright created monitor “Benchmark Launch: Results JSON p95 latency” (tags: benchmark_launch:true)
Datadog Bot: Logan Wright created monitor “Benchmark Launch: Cache hit ratio low” (tags: benchmark_launch:true)
Datadog Bot: Logan Wright created synthetic test “Benchmark results JSON (public)”
Datadog Bot: Logan Wright created synthetic test “Benchmark landing page (HTML)”

Sean Gallagher: :thumbsup: thanks all. Let’s do a quick dry run tomorrow morning — we can temporarily drop thresholds to confirm PD pages correctly, then revert.

Neha Kapoor: please do it outside of peak. And add a note in the oncall handoff: if cache hit drops, first move is to increase CDN TTL / prewarm, not to scale origin blindly.

Felix Schneider: I can add a manual prewarm job (curl latest.json from a few POPs) if we want, but hopefully the cache holds.

Sean Gallagher: let’s keep prewarm as fallback. For now, dashboards + alerts are the priority. Closing loop tomorrow.

source.content:
Aisha Bello: Canary rollout update (bounds + predictive) — early read from last ~24h in us-east-1.

- Canary scope: 1 Dedicated deployment (internal canary acct), predictive mode ON, bounds set min=4 / max=20
- Latency: p95 stayed within baseline band (no sustained regressions). Brief +8–10% p95 during 09:00 ramp but recovered <10m.
- Bound-hit rate (max): ~1.7% of autoscaler loops clamped at max. When clamped, queue_depth p95 rose but didn’t trip paging.
- Bound-hit rate (min): ~0% (never tried to go below min, clamp events are basically nil)
- Prediction error: MAPE ~18% on req/s forecast, MAE ~0.9 replicas equivalent (using current mapping). Biggest miss on the ramp start (forecast lagging by ~1 interval).

Can we get eyes on whether we’re comfortable expanding pilot to 2–3 customer deployments next week? Also: any tweaks needed to alert thresholds before we do that.

Rafael Mendes: Thanks Aisha. For SRE: my main question is “did the canary prove determinism + safety invariants?”
- Any signs of oscillation (scale up/down flip-flop) when forecast is noisy?
- Any cases where predictor returned null/stale and we did something surprising?

Allison Grant: Dashboard for the canary is up here (Grafana): https://grafana.redwood.ai/d/dedicated-autoscaling-bounds/canary-us-east
Panels include:
- desired_replicas vs bounded_replicas
- bound_hit_total split by bound_type=min|max
- forecast_replicas vs actual_needed_replicas (derived)
- queue_depth + saturation

Quick notes from the graphs:
- There are a few single-interval downscales that get canceled next interval, but it’s within the new cooldown window (so actual replicas don’t flap).
- Bound-hit spikes correlate with the known daily ramp; it’s “hitting max” because max=20 is intentionally tight for the canary.

Sergio Costa: +1 on Allison’s read. I also added an annotation stream for scale events so we can see reason codes (reactive vs predictive vs clamped). It’s much easier to eyeball now.

Vanessa Ortiz: On oscillation: we’re seeing the *desired* move around a bit, but not the applied capacity. That’s expected given the clamp + cooldown.

Also: in the ramp window where p95 bumped, the autoscaler was in “reactive override” because queue_depth crossed the reactive threshold. So even though forecast lagged, reactive scaled up (still respecting max). The bump is basically “we hit max, can’t go further.”

Keiko Yamamoto: Re: null/stale forecasts — in this canary we didn’t see nulls. We did see 2 staleness drops (forecast timestamp older than the 15m freshness window), and we fell back to reactive cleanly.

Log line looks like:
`forecast_unavailable reason=stale age_sec=1312 -> mode=reactive`

No scale-to-zero behavior (and the guard from the hotfix is in this build anyway).

Miles Thornton: Bound-hit rate ~1.7% sounds fine, but what’s the proposed paging condition now?
I’m worried we either:
1) page too much because “max clamp” is expected when customer sets a low cap, or
2) don’t page and miss sustained queueing.

Aisha Bello: Current alert rule (canary) is *not paging* on clamp alone.
Paging is:
- `max_bound_clamp_ratio > 5% for 15m` AND
- `queue_depth_p95 > threshold` AND
- `request_error_rate` not already paging (to avoid duplicate pages)

Non-paging warning (Slack-only) when clamp_ratio > 2% for 30m even if queue is fine (mostly so we have a paper trail during rollout).

Rafael Mendes: That feels reasonable. The clamp itself is customer intent; the customer-impact signal is queue/latency.

Question: do we have “bounded state” surfaced to routing yet (so routing can degrade gracefully)? If we expand pilot without that, we might get weird downstream behavior when max is hit.

Mina Haddad: Routing integration is partially in. We can consume a “bounded_at_max” boolean and reduce traffic to that deployment if it’s saturating, but we’re not auto-failing over unless reactive signals also say “unhealthy.”

For pilot, we should at least have the signal visible so TAM/CS can explain “you hit your own max cap, so you’re queueing.”

Jordan Lee: Determinism/audit: we’re emitting `requested_desired`, `bounded_desired`, and `decision_reason` in the decision audit event stream. I spot-checked 10 events and they’re consistent. Example reason codes:
- `predictive_target`
- `reactive_override_queue_depth`
- `clamped_to_max`

We should confirm retention for enterprise pilot accounts but event shape looks good.

Lauren Bishop: For expanding pilot: customer list we discussed is Hexagon (daily ramp), Northstar (budget cap), and maybe Polaris (POC).

Two questions:
1) Any red flags that would make us hold off Northstar specifically (they’re most sensitive to “unexpected spend”, so max clamp is their friend)
2) Can we recommend initial min/max ranges without SRE feeling like we’re guessing

Vanessa Ortiz: Northstar is actually the easiest from a safety standpoint because they’re pushing for a hard max. Risk is they set max too low and then complain about queueing. That’s not a platform bug, but it will still be a support load.

We should require: before enablement, we do a quick capacity sizing using last 7d traffic and propose a “recommended max” + a “contractual max.”

Allison Grant: We can support that with a simple report from the dashboard: peak sustained RPS mapped to “replicas needed” + headroom. I can add a panel that computes “replicas_needed_p95” and overlays min/max.

Sergio Costa: If we’re going to tell customers “recommended range,” we should also log when they’re outside it (warning only), otherwise we’ll keep re-litigating in tickets.

Miles Thornton: +1. Also: we should be explicit in enablement notes that:
- hitting max is allowed/expected (customer cap)
- we only consider it an incident if it causes sustained SLO breach or can’t meet min due to regional shortage

Rafael Mendes: Next steps proposal:
1) Keep canary running through one more full weekday cycle to catch the full ramp + any weekend traffic pattern changes.
2) Before expanding: finalize alert rules + runbook section “customer max cap reached” (what to check, what not to do).
3) Expand to 2 pilot deployments (Hexagon + Northstar) behind per-deployment flag, staged 24h apart.
4) Define success gate for week 1 of pilot: no paging incidents attributable to oscillation/predictor, and prediction MAPE <25% on average (with reactive override behaving).

Lauren Bishop: Sounds good. I’ll coordinate with Miguel/Liam on the staged enablement and make sure CS has the “max cap reached” explanation ready.

Aisha Bello: Action items from this thread:
- Allison/Sergio: add panel for replicas_needed_p95 + overlay min/max, and add “outside recommended range” warning idea to backlog
- Rafael/Aisha: update paging rule text + runbook snippet (I’ll PR today)
- Vanessa/Keiko: keep an eye on the ramp window; if we see repeated clamp + queueing, we should recommend raising max for canary so we’re testing predictor not just the cap

I’ll post another update tomorrow after the next ramp.

source.messages:
Rafael Mendes: putting up for review: proposed paging thresholds for max-bound saturation + queue buildup for Dedicated autoscaling bounds. goal is catch "we're pinned at max and customers are queuing" without paging on normal bursty stuff.

Rafael Mendes: draft alert rules (names tbd):
1) WARN: max_bound_hit_rate > 0.10 for 15m
2) PAGE: max_bound_hit_rate > 0.25 for 10m AND p95_request_latency_ms > SLO_p95 for 10m
3) PAGE: max_bound_hit_rate > 0.25 for 10m AND queue_depth_p95 > 0 for 10m (if latency SLO not wired for that deployment)

Rafael Mendes: scope: per deployment_id + region. routed to Dedicated oncall (platform-infra) during pilot, then SRE primary after GA.

Aisha Bello: +1 on needing the "AND". if we page on bound_hit alone we'll get hammered any time someone sets max low intentionally.

Miles Thornton: agree. also, bound_hit_rate is new — do we have confidence it isn't going to be super noisy during the first week while folks tune cooldown/dampening?

Vanessa Ortiz: bound_hit_rate is emitted per autoscaler loop tick (every 15s). it’s basically "clamped_by_max" / total decisions. it will spike if desired jitters around max.

Vanessa Ortiz: we added cooldown + dampening in the loop, but early pilot did show some oscillation if predictor overshot. (that should be better post PR-48288 + the null predictor hotfix)

Allison Grant: from observability side: per deployment+region is okay cardinality-wise (Dedicated counts are low), but we should avoid labels like model_id or customer_name. please keep it to account_id, deployment_id, region.

Sergio Costa: on the dashboard spec we already have panels for desired vs bounded + queue depth. alert could link to drilldown panel. makes triage faster.

Miles Thornton: question: why 0.25 for 10m? what’s the expected baseline when max is correctly set but we hit occasional peaks?

Logan Wright: we replayed a couple traces in sim: "healthy" max-bound hits were usually <5% and very spiky. sustained >20% correlated with either (a) max set too low for ramp or (b) region shortage preventing scale, which also shows up in provisioning_failures.

Aisha Bello: i’d prefer PAGE be tied to user impact signal (latency or queue). if queue depth metric is flaky for some models (batching makes it weird), latency should be the primary.

Keiko Yamamoto: queue depth can be misleading when batching is high; it may sit >0 even when throughput is fine. we should use "queue_wait_ms_p95" if we have it instead of raw depth.

Allison Grant: we do have queue_wait_ms (from fleet gateway). it’s p95 over 1m windows, label set matches. recommend we alert on queue_wait_ms_p95 > 2000ms or something.

Rafael Mendes: good call. swapping queue_depth for queue_wait_ms. what threshold feels right? dedicated SLO is p95 end-to-end latency; queue wait is a component.

Aisha Bello: suggest queue_wait_ms_p95 > 1500ms for 10m as impact proxy. that’s big enough to matter but not so sensitive it pages on minor blips.

Miles Thornton: also need to consider customers with intentionally "queueing" behavior (they’ll accept slower during peak to stay within cap). do we want paging for that if it’s expected?

Rafael Mendes: that’s the tricky part. we don’t want to wake up on "expected cap". but we do want to know when customer is unknowingly pinned and breaching their own SLA.

Vanessa Ortiz: could we gate paging on a new config flag? like "customer_slo_enforced" or "autoscaling_bounds_contractual"? not sure it exists.

Rafael Mendes: not in v1. we have policy fields but not "page me" knobs.

Sergio Costa: maybe severity split:
- WARN when max bound hit sustained (so CS can see it / daily review)
- PAGE only when bound hit + latency SLO breach (explicit impact)

Aisha Bello: +1. WARNs can go to a Slack channel / ticketing, not PD.

Miles Thornton: +1. and for pilots we can be a bit more aggressive on WARN to learn.

Allison Grant: note: latency SLO label availability is inconsistent per deployment. some enterprise customers only ship app-level latency, not gateway-level.

Logan Wright: control plane has per deployment SLO target, but metrics pipeline might not have it. we can still alert on generic p95_request_latency_ms > 2x baseline maybe? (but that becomes hairy)

Rafael Mendes: let’s keep it simple for now:
- PAGE: max_bound_hit_rate > 0.25 for 10m AND (p95_request_latency_ms > slo_p95 for 10m OR queue_wait_ms_p95 > 1500 for 10m)
- WARN: max_bound_hit_rate > 0.10 for 15m (no impact requirement)

Keiko Yamamoto: pls add a third condition to avoid paging during known region shortages: if provisioning_error_rate is high, it’s not an autoscaler bug, it’s capacity. still customer impact though.

Miles Thornton: even if it’s capacity, we probably still want page? impact is impact. but the runbook should branch: "pinned due to max" vs "min unmet / capacity shortage".

Rafael Mendes: agree. page either way, but alert annotations should include "clamp_reason=max" vs "capacity_unavailable". we have clamp_reason label now right?

Vanessa Ortiz: for max-bound, yes. for min unmet due to shortage, separate metric (min_unmet_seconds_total). different alert.

Aisha Bello: can we add "for" to page = 15m instead of 10m? to reduce wakeups on transient ramps.

Rafael Mendes: if it’s a real ramp, 15m might be too slow (customers feel it). but with impact condition maybe 10m is ok.

Sergio Costa: what about requiring 2 consecutive evaluation windows (Grafana) => effectively 10m already. i can configure eval every 1m, for=10m.

Allison Grant: also watch out for aggregation. make sure bound_hit_rate is computed as a rate over decisions, not a gauge from last tick. otherwise prometheus will do weird stuff.

Logan Wright: it’s a counter pair (bound_hit_total, decision_total). recording rule computes ratio over 5m. so alert should use that recording rule.

Rafael Mendes: cool. action items:
- Sergio/Allison: update alert rule to use ratio recording rule + queue_wait_ms, and route WARN to #dedicated-scaling-alerts (no PD)
- Aisha/Miles: sanity check thresholds after first pilot week and adjust
- Keiko/Vanessa: ensure runbook has "customer intentionally capped" note + comms guidance

PagerDuty: (system) Rafael Mendes updated routing rules for service "Dedicated Autoscaling" (pilot) — WARN notifications to Slack, PAGE notifications to PD.

Miles Thornton: :thumbsup: please add to runbook: when paging, first check if bounded_desired == max AND saturation/latency. if yes: coordinate w/ CS to ask customer if max cap is intentional before scaling override.

Aisha Bello: +1. and we should explicitly say: do not raise max without customer approval unless it’s an incident and we have pre-approved emergency spend.

Rafael Mendes: yep, adding that. thanks all.

Miles Thornton: also need to consider customers with intentionally "queueing" behavior (they’ll accept slower during peak to stay within cap). do we want paging for that if it’s expected?

Rafael Mendes: that’s the tricky part. we don’t want to wake up on "expected cap". but we do want to know when customer is unknowingly pinned and breaching their own SLA.

Vanessa Ortiz: could we gate paging on a new config flag? like "customer_slo_enforced" or "autoscaling_bounds_contractual"? not sure it exists.

Rafael Mendes: not in v1. we have policy fields but not "page me" knobs.

Sergio Costa: maybe severity split:
- WARN when max bound hit sustained (so CS can see it / daily review)
- PAGE only when bound hit + latency SLO breach (explicit impact)

Aisha Bello: +1. WARNs can go to a Slack channel / ticketing, not PD.

Miles Thornton: +1. and for pilots we can be a bit more aggressive on WARN to learn.

Allison Grant: note: latency SLO label availability is inconsistent per deployment. some enterprise customers only ship app-level latency, not gateway-level.

Logan Wright: control plane has per deployment SLO target, but metrics pipeline might not have it. we can still alert on generic p95_request_latency_ms > 2x baseline maybe? (but that becomes hairy)

Rafael Mendes: let’s keep it simple for now:
- PAGE: max_bound_hit_rate > 0.25 for 10m AND (p95_request_latency_ms > slo_p95 for 10m OR queue_wait_ms_p95 > 1500 for 10m)
- WARN: max_bound_hit_rate > 0.10 for 15m (no impact requirement)

Keiko Yamamoto: pls add a third condition to avoid paging during known region shortages: if provisioning_error_rate is high, it’s not an autoscaler bug, it’s capacity. still customer impact though.

Miles Thornton: even if it’s capacity, we probably still want page? impact is impact. but the runbook should branch: "pinned due to max" vs "min unmet / capacity shortage".

Rafael Mendes: agree. page either way, but alert annotations should include "clamp_reason=max" vs "capacity_unavailable". we have clamp_reason label now right?

Vanessa Ortiz: for max-bound, yes. for min unmet due to shortage, separate metric (min_unmet_seconds_total). different alert.

Aisha Bello: can we add "for" to page = 15m instead of 10m? to reduce wakeups on transient ramps.

Rafael Mendes: if it’s a real ramp, 15m might be too slow (customers feel it). but with impact condition maybe 10m is ok.

Sergio Costa: what about requiring 2 consecutive evaluation windows (Grafana) => effectively 10m already. i can configure eval every 1m, for=10m.

Allison Grant: also watch out for aggregation. make sure bound_hit_rate is computed as a rate over decisions, not a gauge from last tick. otherwise prometheus will do weird stuff.

Logan Wright: it’s a counter pair (bound_hit_total, decision_total). recording rule computes ratio over 5m. so alert should use that recording rule.

Rafael Mendes: cool. action items:
- Sergio/Allison: update alert rule to use ratio recording rule + queue_wait_ms, and route WARN to #dedicated-scaling-alerts (no PD)
- Aisha/Miles: sanity check thresholds after first pilot week and adjust
- Keiko/Vanessa: ensure runbook has "customer intentionally capped" note + comms guidance

PagerDuty: (system) Rafael Mendes updated routing rules for service "Dedicated Autoscaling" (pilot) — WARN notifications to Slack, PAGE notifications to PD.

Miles Thornton: :thumbsup: please add to runbook: when paging, first check if bounded_desired == max AND saturation/latency. if yes: coordinate w/ CS to ask customer if max cap is intentional before scaling override.

Aisha Bello: +1. and we should explicitly say: do not raise max without customer approval unless it’s an incident and we have pre-approved emergency spend.

Rafael Mendes: yep, adding that. thanks all.

source.text:
Ava Chen: Heads-up — saw three p95 GPU-util spikes in us-west this morning that paged oncall. All recovered within 30–90s. We should adjust paging vs ticketing to avoid wakeups for these micro-flares. Thoughts?

Ravi Patel: +1. Do we know cardinality? per-pod or cluster-wide?

Maya Ortiz: Cluster-level spikes, not single pod. Prometheus shows short-lived 2–3x p95 bumps correlated with scheduling churn.

Luca Marino: Agree noise. Proposal: keep page for node OOM / node offline / sustained >10m above 90%. For p95 spikes that are <5m and not accompanied by OOM/evict — convert to ticket. We can implement with Alertmanager piping to ops-tickets if rule has 'severity: low' and 'duration: <5m'. :white_check_mark:

Ava Chen: Sketch of rule — use promql:
```promql
max_over_time(node_gpu_utilization[5m]) > 0.9
```
But only page if `increase(node_oom_total[15m]) > 0` or `node_up==0` else ticket.

Ravi Patel: We should also group by region and set mute windows for scheduled maintenance windows (us-west patching 0200-0300). Add silences via Alertmanager API.

Maya Ortiz: Also add a "burst smoothing" rule: require the p95 to be above threshold for 3/5 scrapes (e.g., 30s scrape interval *3 = ~90s). Example:
```promql
count_over_time((node_gpu_utilization > 0.9)[2m]) >= 3
```

Luca Marino: Action items: 1) update alert rules with new duration+oom exception (Ravi), 2) add grouping by region + route (Maya), 3) run a 2-week quieting experiment in us-west (Ava). I'll open ticket.

ops-bot: Ticket created: INF-4821 "quieting experiment: us-west transient gpu flares" — assignee: Luca Marino.

Ava Chen: Thanks. We'll monitor SLO impact in the GPU-ops dashboard — link https://grafana.internal/redwood/gpu-ops (useful for reviewers). Ping @oncall for awareness.

Ravi Patel: Can we also add cost tag? Some of these flares are caused by low-priority batch jobs — might be eligible for preemptible nodes. Tagging would help.

Maya Ortiz: Noted. I'll add a label 'workload_class' where possible.

Luca Marino: Thanks all. If no objections, I'll apply change at 09:30 PT and leave a 48h rollback window.

source.content:
Sean Gallagher: seeing some drift already in the KMS wrapper PRs. Metrics are showing up under a couple different prefixes (`kms.*`, `deps.kms.*`, and one `redwood_kms_*`). Before we bake this into dashboards/alerts, can we align with observability naming/tagging standards?

Nadia Rahman: +1. In pr-18434 I used `kms.client.latency_ms` based on an older dep pattern. But I can change it if we have a canonical.

Allison Grant: yep, let’s fix now. Current standard (for anything we ship as a shared lib) is:
- dot-separated, `redwood.<domain>.<component>.<metric>`
- units in name when applicable (`*_ms`, `*_bytes`, etc)
- counters end with `_total`
- tags are *bounded cardinality* only (no ARNs, no request ids, no customer ids)

Logan Wright: the rest of platform deps use `redwood.deps.<dep>.*` (db, redis, etc). do we want KMS to be “dep” or “security/kms” domain?

Rafael Mendes: from SRE POV it behaves like a dependency (availability/latency). But it’s also a security primitive and we’ll want a KMS dashboard next to other crypto stuff.

Kevin Osei: also need consistent tags for error taxonomy work (ENG-4822). We should carry `error_category` (our canonical) + optionally `provider_error_code` (bounded-ish) but not raw messages.

Tomáš Novák: what are we calling the operation tag? I’ve seen `op=decrypt` and `operation=Decrypt` mixed in logs.

Allison Grant: proposal:
Namespace: `redwood.kms.client.*` (explicitly KMS, not generic deps), because we’ll have KMS-specific panels + we don’t want to collide with other “deps” conventions. Still treat as dependency in alerts.

Metric set:
- `redwood.kms.client.request_latency_ms` (histogram)
- `redwood.kms.client.requests_total` (counter)
- `redwood.kms.client.errors_total` (counter)
- `redwood.kms.client.retry_attempts_total` (counter)
- `redwood.kms.client.circuit_breaker_state` (gauge; values: `0=closed,1=half_open,2=open`)

Required tags (same set across all above where applicable):
- `service` (bounded)
- `env` (prod/staging/dev)
- `region`
- `deployment_mode` (hosted/dedicated/private)
- `provider` (aws/gcp/azure)
- `operation` (Encrypt/Decrypt/GenerateDataKey/DescribeKey/etc) -> PascalCase
- `status` (ok/error)
- `error_category` (AccessDenied/NotFound/Throttling/Timeout/InvalidCiphertext/KeyDisabled/Unknown)

Optional tags:
- `http_status` (if we have it)
- `sdk_error_code` (but only if bounded; e.g. AWS `ThrottlingException`, `AccessDeniedException`)

Hard no tags:
- `key_arn`, `key_id`, `key_alias`, `principal`, `customer`, `tenant`, raw exception string

Sean Gallagher: +1 to `redwood.kms.client.*`. Having it separate makes dashboarding cleaner. For alerts we can still point at those metrics.

Logan Wright: works for me. We should also align tracing span names. Caleb, you had something in the PR?

Caleb Johnson: yep in the instrumentation branch I used span name `kms.decrypt` etc. I can update to `redwood.kms.client` as the span prefix and `operation` attribute.

Nadia Rahman: couple q’s:
1) for `operation` should we keep casing consistent w SDK (AWS uses `Decrypt`, `Encrypt`, `GenerateDataKey`)
2) do we want separate metric per operation? or tag only?

Allison Grant: 1) yes, match SDK naming exactly (PascalCase) so people can correlate docs
2) tag only. metric per op explodes quickly and the dashboards can break down by tag.

Kevin Osei: for error_category mapping, I’ll add a helper in the wrapper so it’s consistent. Also note we need `KeyDisabled` distinct from `NotFound` for runbook routing.

Tomáš Novák: can we also add a tag for `breaker_state`? or do we rely on gauge + logs?

Sean Gallagher: prefer gauge only. Tagging state on every request metric will skew cardinality and queries. We can chart `circuit_breaker_state` alongside request/error rates.

Rafael Mendes: +1. Also for game day we’ll want a quick query: “what % of requests are failing due to timeout vs throttling” -> `errors_total{error_category=Timeout}` etc.

Allison Grant: decision: lock the naming as above.

Action items:
- Nadia: update pr-18434 metrics names + tag keys to match (and remove any key id tags)
- Caleb: update tracing span name + attributes to use `operation` and `provider`
- Kevin: ensure wrapper emits `error_category` consistently and document in ENG-4822
- Sean/Rafael: update alert queries in observability pack PR to the new namespace once the rename lands

Nadia Rahman: ack. I’ll push a follow-up commit today and note the rename in the PR description so reviewers don’t miss it.

Logan Wright: once merged, I’ll add a tiny linter check in the telemetry helper to prevent `kms_` snake_case metrics from sneaking in again.

Sean Gallagher: thank you. also pls call out in release notes that old dashboards (if any) need to be migrated. We should avoid a silent break.

Allison Grant: yep. I’ll drop a note in #eng-observability too. :thumbsup:

Caleb Johnson: I’ll handle span rename + add `error_category` attribute on error spans so traces line up with metrics.

Kevin Osei: great. I’ll post in the error taxonomy thread once the mapping table is final.

Sean Gallagher: cool, closing the loop: final naming decision is `redwood.kms.client.*` with required tags listed above. Please stick to it in all KMS wrapper + dashboards going forward.

source.messages:
Rafael Mendes: hey all — prepping ENG-4829 game day. need ideas for KMS outage scenarios + who’s doing what (facilitator/observers/comms).

Sean Gallagher: +1. can we cover both “slow + throttled” and “hard down”? last incident pattern was latency spike → retry amplification.

Allison Grant: scenarios I’d like:
1) p95 latency jump (ex: 200ms → 3s) for decrypt/envelope
2) intermittent throttling (429) at ~5-10% then ramp to 50%
3) full outage (timeouts)
4) AccessDenied due to IAM regression
Also want to validate alerts aren’t paging nonstop in staging :)

Tomáš Novák: add “wrong key id / key disabled” too. we’ve seen misconfig look like InvalidCiphertext / NotFound depending on provider.

Rafael Mendes: yep. let’s list them cleanly:
A) Latency degradation
B) Throttling
C) Full outage/timeouts
D) Permission denied
E) Key disabled / scheduled deletion / wrong ARN
F) Ciphertext/key mismatch during rotation
Anything else?

Kevin Osei: for E/F: we should test the error taxonomy mapping + log fields. I want to confirm we emit `kms.error_category` + provider code consistently + trace span attr.

Ethan Park: for F (rotation mismatch), we can simulate by flipping the “dual-read” flag off prematurely in staging control plane (or use a test key version that can’t decrypt old ciphertext). but we need tight guardrails so we don’t brick staging.

Sean Gallagher: also “KMS returns success but with high tail latency” → should trip circuit breaker based on error/timeout rate? or do we include latency-only breaker?

Allison Grant: breaker needs to be careful. latency-only breaker can cause flapping. maybe we treat timeouts as errors and alert on latency separately.

Rafael Mendes: agreed. for game day we can focus breaker on timeout/error rate. latency spike scenario can validate retry budget + backpressure + dashboards.

Nadia Rahman: from observability side: we should confirm dashboards show:
- request rate to KMS
- error rate by category (Throttling vs AccessDenied vs Timeout)
- breaker state metric
- time spent waiting in retry/backoff
If we don’t have breaker state exported yet we at least need logs.

Marcus Lin: security asks: for D (AccessDenied), confirm the surfaced error is actionable (missing permission / wrong principal) but does not leak sensitive key ARNs in customer-facing paths (Private operators might see more, Hosted less).

Fiona McCarthy: comms-wise: are we running this as a formal incident sim (with #incidents updates) or just internal game day notes?

Rafael Mendes: internal game day but we should practice the muscle memory:
- open a lightweight incident in #incidents (or at least a stub thread)
- post status every ~15 min
- capture timeline + decisions
No external comms.

Connor O'Brien: if we touch any flags/cutovers, I want a checklist + rollback plan written before we start. can we keep it to staging only + one canary env?

Ethan Park: +1. staging only. we can also run the “flag flip” in a sandbox namespace so no shared control plane state is affected.

Tomáš Novák: for C (full outage), what’s expected app behavior? do we fail requests fast or hang until retry budget exhausted?

Sean Gallagher: expectation should be “cap latency”:
- strict per-call timeout (ex 500ms/1s)
- bounded retries w/ jitter
- if breaker trips → immediate fail-fast for new decrypts
But if we have DEK cache, some traffic may still succeed until TTL expires.

Allison Grant: + we should validate we *don’t* melt ourselves (thread pool / goroutines etc.) when KMS is slow. request queue should apply backpressure.

Kevin Osei: can we capture a specific success criterion per scenario? e.g.
- throttling 20% for 10 min → service p99 stays under X, 5xx under Y, and KMS QPS reduces after backpressure
- AccessDenied → alarms fire, logs show remediation hint, oncall can identify missing IAM action within 10 min

Rafael Mendes: love it. rough criteria draft:
A) Latency: dashboards show spike within 2 min; no retry storm; p99 for API doesn’t exceed 2x baseline for >5 min
B) Throttling: error category=Throttling visible; backoff reduces KMS QPS; no cascading 5xx across unrelated routes
C) Outage/timeouts: breaker trips; fail-fast kicks in; oncall sees runbook + mitigation steps; recovery clean (breaker resets)
D) AccessDenied: actionable error surfaced; triage path points to IAM module/runbook; no secret leakage
E) Key disabled/wrong ARN: surfaces as NotFound/Disabled (mapped); operator guidance points to validation/preflight
F) Rotation mismatch: dual-read window catches it; rollback path documented (re-enable dual-read / revert cutover)

Marcus Lin: for F, security requirement is we don’t silently decrypt with “unexpected” keys outside the approved window. but dual-read is explicitly approved during rotation. we should verify audit log event for cutover toggle exists (even in staging).

Nadia Rahman: we can add “audit log event observed in exporter” as a check.

Fiona McCarthy: roles:
- Facilitator: Rafael
- Comms lead: I can do internal incident updates + timekeeping
- Scribe: Tomáš?
- Observers: Allison (alerts/dashboards) + Kevin (logs/taxonomy)
Need an “injector” (the person doing the fault injection / config flips).

Sean Gallagher: I can inject throttling/latency via the test harness (or whatever chaos hook we have in staging). for IAM AccessDenied we might need Jordan/infra but we can simulate by using a principal missing decrypt.

Ethan Park: for flag flips / rotation mismatch simulation, I can be injector for scenario F (with Connor watching release hygiene).

Connor O'Brien: ok I’ll be “change control observer” lol. I’ll keep a rollback checklist and make sure we don’t go off-script.

Rafael Mendes: perfect. proposed schedule: next Tue 10:00–11:30 PT.
Run order: A (latency) → B (throttle) → C (timeout) → D (AccessDenied) → if time, F (rotation mismatch).
E (key disabled/wrong arn) can be a quick follow-up if we still have energy.

Allison Grant: can we ensure alerts are scoped to staging so we don’t page primary oncall? last time staging alerts weren’t muted correctly.

Sean Gallagher: yep. we’ll route to #eng-sre only. IncidentBot can still post but no PagerDuty.

IncidentBot: Reminder: For game days, use channel topic template and link follow-ups to Linear. If you open an incident, tag it as “game-day”.

Kevin Osei: can we link the doc where we’ll take notes + the success criteria list?

Rafael Mendes: dropping a wip doc link: https://drive.google.com/ (kms game day plan)
I’ll paste success criteria + owners there. Also linking ENG-4829 for follow-ups.

Marcus Lin: ask: include a “what would we tell an enterprise customer” section in the after-action. esp. around expected behavior during KMS outages (do we fail closed, do we serve cached data keys, etc.).

Tomáš Novák: +1. will add a blurb + map to runbook actions.

Fiona McCarthy: I’ll prep a comms template:
- start
- scenario injected
- impact observed
- mitigation
- recovery
- action items

Ethan Park: for F: we should pre-create a staging key w/ two versions and a small dataset encrypted under v1. then cutover to v2 without dual-read to trigger mismatch. safer than random production-like data.

Sean Gallagher: cool. for B: I’ll simulate 429s + `Retry-After` behavior. also want to validate our wrapper respects provider hints (where available) and doesn’t hammer.

Allison Grant: and I’ll watch the dashboard panels + alert thresholds. if noisy, we capture tuning notes but we don’t “fix live” unless it’s blocking.

Rafael Mendes: ok, next steps:
- Rafael: finalize agenda + invite
- Sean: confirm fault injection knobs for A/B/C
- Ethan: prep rotation mismatch setup
- Fiona: comms template
- Tomáš: scribe + after-action structure
- Allison/Kevin: define what to screenshot/export for evidence

Connor O'Brien: please add explicit “stop conditions” (if staging starts failing unrelated tests or we see customer-like blast radius).

Rafael Mendes: will do. stop conditions: runaway error rate, inability to recover within 10 min, or impact outside the target service namespace.

Kevin Osei: last thing: can we ensure we test “breaker reset” too? i.e., after recovery, does it heal quickly or do we need manual intervention.

Sean Gallagher: yup. we’ll include recovery phase as part of each scenario.

Rafael Mendes: awesome. I’ll update the doc + ENG-4829 with roles + scenario list today.

E) Key disabled/wrong ARN: surfaces as NotFound/Disabled (mapped); operator guidance points to validation/preflight
F) Rotation mismatch: dual-read window catches it; rollback path documented (re-enable dual-read / revert cutover)

Marcus Lin: for F, security requirement is we don’t silently decrypt with “unexpected” keys outside the approved window. but dual-read is explicitly approved during rotation. we should verify audit log event for cutover toggle exists (even in staging).

Nadia Rahman: we can add “audit log event observed in exporter” as a check.

Fiona McCarthy: roles:
- Facilitator: Rafael
- Comms lead: I can do internal incident updates + timekeeping
- Scribe: Tomáš?
- Observers: Allison (alerts/dashboards) + Kevin (logs/taxonomy)
Need an “injector” (the person doing the fault injection / config flips).

Sean Gallagher: I can inject throttling/latency via the test harness (or whatever chaos hook we have in staging). for IAM AccessDenied we might need Jordan/infra but we can simulate by using a principal missing decrypt.

Ethan Park: for flag flips / rotation mismatch simulation, I can be injector for scenario F (with Connor watching release hygiene).

Connor O'Brien: ok I’ll be “change control observer” lol. I’ll keep a rollback checklist and make sure we don’t go off-script.

Rafael Mendes: perfect. proposed schedule: next Tue 10:00–11:30 PT.
Run order: A (latency) → B (throttle) → C (timeout) → D (AccessDenied) → if time, F (rotation mismatch).
E (key disabled/wrong arn) can be a quick follow-up if we still have energy.

Allison Grant: can we ensure alerts are scoped to staging so we don’t page primary oncall? last time staging alerts weren’t muted correctly.

Sean Gallagher: yep. we’ll route to #eng-sre only. IncidentBot can still post but no PagerDuty.

IncidentBot: Reminder: For game days, use channel topic template and link follow-ups to Linear. If you open an incident, tag it as “game-day”.

Kevin Osei: can we link the doc where we’ll take notes + the success criteria list?

Rafael Mendes: dropping a wip doc link: https://drive.google.com/ (kms game day plan)
I’ll paste success criteria + owners there. Also linking ENG-4829 for follow-ups.

Marcus Lin: ask: include a “what would we tell an enterprise customer” section in the after-action. esp. around expected behavior during KMS outages (do we fail closed, do we serve cached data keys, etc.).

Tomáš Novák: +1. will add a blurb + map to runbook actions.

Fiona McCarthy: I’ll prep a comms template:
- start
- scenario injected
- impact observed
- mitigation
- recovery
- action items

Ethan Park: for F: we should pre-create a staging key w/ two versions and a small dataset encrypted under v1. then cutover to v2 without dual-read to trigger mismatch. safer than random production-like data.

Sean Gallagher: cool. for B: I’ll simulate 429s + `Retry-After` behavior. also want to validate our wrapper respects provider hints (where available) and doesn’t hammer.

Allison Grant: and I’ll watch the dashboard panels + alert thresholds. if noisy, we capture tuning notes but we don’t “fix live” unless it’s blocking.

Rafael Mendes: ok, next steps:
- Rafael: finalize agenda + invite
- Sean: confirm fault injection knobs for A/B/C
- Ethan: prep rotation mismatch setup
- Fiona: comms template
- Tomáš: scribe + after-action structure
- Allison/Kevin: define what to screenshot/export for evidence

Connor O'Brien: please add explicit “stop conditions” (if staging starts failing unrelated tests or we see customer-like blast radius).

Rafael Mendes: will do. stop conditions: runaway error rate, inability to recover within 10 min, or impact outside the target service namespace.

Kevin Osei: last thing: can we ensure we test “breaker reset” too? i.e., after recovery, does it heal quickly or do we need manual intervention.

Sean Gallagher: yup. we’ll include recovery phase as part of each scenario.

Rafael Mendes: awesome. I’ll update the doc + ENG-4829 with roles + scenario list today.

Rafael Mendes: hey all — prepping ENG-4829 game day. need ideas for KMS outage scenarios + who’s doing what (facilitator/observers/comms).

Sean Gallagher: +1. can we cover both “slow + throttled” and “hard down”? last incident pattern was latency spike → retry amplification.

Allison Grant: scenarios I’d like:
1) p95 latency jump (ex: 200ms → 3s) for decrypt/envelope
2) intermittent throttling (429) at ~5-10% then ramp to 50%
3) full outage (timeouts)
4) AccessDenied due to IAM regression
Also want to validate alerts aren’t paging nonstop in staging :)

Tomáš Novák: add “wrong key id / key disabled” too. we’ve seen misconfig look like InvalidCiphertext / NotFound depending on provider.

Rafael Mendes: yep. let’s list them cleanly:
A) Latency degradation
B) Throttling
C) Full outage/timeouts
D) Permission denied
E) Key disabled / scheduled deletion / wrong ARN
F) Ciphertext/key mismatch during rotation
Anything else?

Kevin Osei: for E/F: we should test the error taxonomy mapping + log fields. I want to confirm we emit `kms.error_category` + provider code consistently + trace span attr.

Ethan Park: for F (rotation mismatch), we can simulate by flipping the “dual-read” flag off prematurely in staging control plane (or use a test key version that can’t decrypt old ciphertext). but we need tight guardrails so we don’t brick staging.

Sean Gallagher: also “KMS returns success but with high tail latency” → should trip circuit breaker based on error/timeout rate? or do we include latency-only breaker?

Allison Grant: breaker needs to be careful. latency-only breaker can cause flapping. maybe we treat timeouts as errors and alert on latency separately.

Rafael Mendes: agreed. for game day we can focus breaker on timeout/error rate. latency spike scenario can validate retry budget + backpressure + dashboards.

Nadia Rahman: from observability side: we should confirm dashboards show:
- request rate to KMS
- error rate by category (Throttling vs AccessDenied vs Timeout)
- breaker state metric
- time spent waiting in retry/backoff
If we don’t have breaker state exported yet we at least need logs.

Marcus Lin: security asks: for D (AccessDenied), confirm the surfaced error is actionable (missing permission / wrong principal) but does not leak sensitive key ARNs in customer-facing paths (Private operators might see more, Hosted less).

- open a lightweight incident in #incidents (or at least a stub thread)
- post status every ~15 min
- capture timeline + decisions
No external comms.

Ethan Park: +1. staging only. we can also run the “flag flip” in a sandbox namespace so no shared control plane state is affected.

Tomáš Novák: for C (full outage), what’s expected app behavior? do we fail requests fast or hang until retry budget exhausted?

Sean Gallagher: expectation should be “cap latency”:
- strict per-call timeout (ex 500ms/1s)
- bounded retries w/ jitter
- if breaker trips → immediate fail-fast for new decrypts
But if we have DEK cache, some traffic may still succeed until TTL expires.

Allison Grant: + we should validate we *don’t* melt ourselves (thread pool / goroutines etc.) when KMS is slow. request queue should apply backpressure.

- throttling 20% for 10 min → service p99 stays under X, 5xx under Y, and KMS QPS reduces after backpressure
- AccessDenied → alarms fire, logs show remediation hint, oncall can identify missing IAM action within 10 min

Rafael Mendes: perfect. proposed schedule: next Tue 10:00–11:30 PT.
Run order: A (latency) → B (throttle) → C (timeout) → D (AccessDenied) → if time, F (rotation mismatch).
E (key disabled/wrong arn) can be a quick follow-up if we still have energy.

source.messages:
Rafael Mendes: Heads up: staging private cluster is paging *a lot* since we turned on the min-pack rules. Can we do a quick noise review today + decide what’s disabled by default for v1?

Rafael Mendes: Top offenders from last 12h:
- `RedwoodHighLatencyP99` (fires during warm-up + batch size changes)
- `RedwoodGPUUtilizationHigh` (basically constant)
- `KubePodCrashLooping` for log-collector when storage is tight
- `PrometheusTargetMissing` (grafana sidecar / dashboards configmap reload)

Neha Kapoor: +1. I pulled Alertmanager history too. A bunch are “technically true” but not actionable/page-worthy for Private customers.

Sean Gallagher: Can you paste the current thresholds / for: durations? Especially for the latency + GPU ones.

Rafael Mendes: Yep. Current rule snippets (from the staging branch):
```
- alert: RedwoodHighLatencyP99
  expr: histogram_quantile(0.99, sum(rate(redwood_request_latency_seconds_bucket[5m])) by (le, job)) > 2
  for: 10m
  labels:
    severity: page

- alert: RedwoodGPUUtilizationHigh
  expr: avg_over_time(nvidia_smi_utilization_gpu[5m]) > 0.9
  for: 15m
  labels:
    severity: page
```

Aisha Bello: Latency alert shouldn’t be a raw p99 > X without SLO context. For Private we said we’d do burn-rate style on *availability + latency* SLOs, with `page` only on fast burn.

Aisha Bello: If we keep a simple threshold alert as a backstop, it should be severity=ticket by default, and the `for:` needs to tolerate deploy/warmup.

Tomáš Novák: Also missing runbook links in annotations for some of these. Oncall is going to hate us if alerts page without “first 3 things to check.”

Sergio Costa: Agree. The rules I added were a first pass (copied from hosted-ish defaults). We can tune for Private. Which ones are we OK disabling by default vs keeping but down-severity?

Thomas Baird: For GPU util: `>0.9 for 15m` is *always* true under normal inference load. High utilization is not a failure mode; it’s “good.” We should alert on *sustained saturation with queueing / tail latency impact* or on *OOM / restarts*.

Thomas Baird: Better signals:
- queue depth rising + latency burn
- `DCGM_FI_DEV_FB_USED / FB_TOTAL` (memory pressure) trending to 0.95
- `container_oom_events_total` / `kube_pod_container_status_restarts_total` for runtime pods

Diego Santos: +1. In private installs customers will run hot; “GPU > 90%” is basically a badge of honor.

Neha Kapoor: Proposed policy for v1 minimum pack:
- Page only on: SLO burn (fast), control-plane down, runtime error rate spike, persistent scrape gaps for Redwood targets
- Ticket only on: resource pressure warnings, elevated latency without burn, log pipeline backpressure
- Disabled by default: “nice to know” alerts that have high baseline true rate

Sean Gallagher: For latency: can we switch to something like multiwindow burn? e.g. 2% budget per hour type? Or at least couple with error rate.

Aisha Bello: Yes. For Private guidance we recommended 99.9% API availability + a p95/p99 objective. But alerting should be:
- Fast burn: 5m/1h
- Slow burn: 30m/6h (ticket)

Aisha Bello: If we don’t have enough good recording rules yet, then the fallback should be conservative and non-paging.

Sergio Costa: We *do* have recording rules in PR-18356 branch (redwood_request_success_ratio, redwood_request_rate, redwood_latency_p99). We can build burn on top of those.

Rafael Mendes: The staging cluster is also tiny (2 GPUs) and gets periodic load tests that slam it, so we’re seeing the worst.

Thomas Baird: That’s fine; alerts should still be robust. If our own staging causes constant paging, customers will be worse.

Tomáš Novák: Can we list the alerts and mark them as:
A) on by default
B) on by default but ticket
C) off by default (opt-in)

Neha Kapoor: Here’s my rough cut (happy to edit):
A) ON + PAGE:
- `RedwoodAvailabilityBurnRateFast` (new)
- `RedwoodErrorRateHigh` (page if >X for Y)
- `RedwoodControlPlaneDown` (gateway/auth/policy)
- `RedwoodRuntimePodsNotReady` (but only if >N pods or >10m)
- `RedwoodPrometheusScrapeMissing` for Redwood jobs (for 15m)

B) ON + TICKET:
- `RedwoodLatencyBurnRateSlow` (new)
- `RedwoodHighLatencyP99` (fallback threshold, ticket)
- `RedwoodKVCacheHitRateLow` (if we keep it, needs long window)
- `RedwoodLogForwarderBackpressure` (warn)

C) OFF by default:
- `RedwoodGPUUtilizationHigh`
- `RedwoodCPUThrottlingHigh` (too environment dependent)
- `NodeFilesystemAlmostFull` (customers own node alerts; we can document)

Sean Gallagher: +1, but for `RuntimePodsNotReady` we should exclude deploy rollouts. Use `max_over_time` and require it’s not a rollout in progress (if we have that label/signal).

Diego Santos: We can annotate pods with `app.kubernetes.io/part-of=redwood` and alert only on those. Also, ignore the first 20m post-install / post-upgrade? Installer can set a temporary silence.

Rafael Mendes: Installer silences would be nice but not for this cut. For now, we can bump `for:` durations.

Sergio Costa: For `KubePodCrashLooping` noise: it’s mostly the log collector when PVC fills (staging retention is 24h but PVC is 5Gi). That’s “real,” but it’s not Redwood runtime breaking.

Neha Kapoor: Right. That should be ticket + actionable steps:
1) check PVC usage
2) reduce retention / increase PVC
3) verify exporter sink connectivity (Loki/ELK)

Tomáš Novák: Please embed that into alert annotations. Like:
```
summary: "Log collector crashlooping"
description: "Check PVC usage for <pvc>. If >85%, increase size or lower retention. Verify egress to sink. Runbook: <link>"
```

Thomas Baird: One more: `PrometheusTargetMissing` is firing due to the grafana dashboards sidecar metrics endpoint being blocked by NetworkPolicy. That shouldn’t page either.

Rafael Mendes: In staging it’s our own NP hardening. But customers will also do that. I think scrape-missing for *Redwood runtime/control plane* should be page (since dashboards and alerts become useless), scrape-missing for grafana sidecars should be ticket/off.

Sean Gallagher: +1. Scope the scrape alert to `job=~"redwood-.*"` not `.*`.

Sergio Costa: OK, action items:
- [me] Update rules:
  - remove `RedwoodGPUUtilizationHigh` from default
  - add burn-rate alerts (fast page, slow ticket)
  - downgrade `RedwoodHighLatencyP99` to ticket + raise threshold + longer `for:`
  - tighten scrape-missing selector to Redwood jobs
- [Rafael/Neha] re-run staging with same load test and report pages/tickets

Aisha Bello: For the fallback latency threshold: what’s a sane default? 2s p99 seems too low for private clusters with longer contexts.

Thomas Baird: Also p99 depends heavily on request mix + context length. If we keep it at all, make it p95 and make the threshold something like 5s with a long `for:` and label it “investigate,” not “incident.”

Neha Kapoor: Let’s do:
- p95 > 3s for 30m -> ticket
- p99 > 8s for 30m -> ticket
And rely on burn rate for paging.

Sean Gallagher: Works for me. The burn rate math matters more.

Rafael Mendes: +1. That should stop the constant pages.

Diego Santos: One more ask: ensure alerts don’t include high-cardinality labels (route/model/tenant). Staging is already pushing Prom cardinality.

Thomas Baird: Yes please. Alert labels should be cluster, namespace, job, maybe model_family at most (and even that’s questionable).

Sergio Costa: Ack. I’ll keep annotations generic and point to dashboards where you can drill down.

Tomáš Novák: Also set a consistent severity taxonomy: `page|ticket|info`. Right now I’m seeing `critical` and `warning` mixed in.

Allison Grant: +1 on taxonomy. Private standards doc says `page` + `ticket` + `info`, and runbook links required for page/ticket. Please align before we tag the pack.

Sergio Costa: Got it. I’ll push an update today and link the PR here.

Rafael Mendes: Thanks. Once the PR is up I’ll re-run the staging canary tonight and we’ll validate “no pages unless we break something.” (reaction: :eyes: x2)

Neha Kapoor: Please also test failure injection:
- block egress from runtime to model registry
- kill 1 runtime pod repeatedly
- fill Prometheus PVC to 90%
So we confirm the page/ticket split is sane.

Rafael Mendes: Will do. I’ll post results back in this thread.

Sergio Costa: PR is up: adjusts severities, disables GPU util by default, adds burn-rate alerts using recording rules, and updates scrape selectors. Also added runbook URLs in annotations. Link: https://github.com/redwood-inference/observability-pack/pull/18372 (reaction: :thumbsup: x3)

Rafael Mendes: Nice. I’ll deploy to staging now.

Rafael Mendes: Update after 2h soak + load test: zero pages, 3 tickets:
- slow latency burn (expected during peak load)
- log forwarder backpressure (PVC too small)
- scrape missing for one runtime pod (turned out to be NP misconfig)
This feels *much* better.

Aisha Bello: Great. That’s the behavior we want: pages only for true incidents. Ship it.

Allison Grant: Please make sure the “disabled by default” list is documented in the pack README/values so Support knows what to enable when troubleshooting.

Sergio Costa: Will add to docs in follow-up PR.

Tomáš Novák: And ensure alert annotations include “silence guidance” (when it’s OK to silence) for the ticket-level ones.

Neha Kapoor: +1. Closing the loop here once docs are updated.
