B) “Runtime LTS - Rollout” with breakdown by region + ring + runtime_version
C) “Runtime LTS - Dedicated top tenants” (top N by traffic, error rate, p95) gated to internal-only

Connor O'Brien: Works. Also need a “mandatory patch” indicator: if we declare a patch mandatory due to Sev1/security, we’ll want to monitor adoption. Can we graph % of LTS traffic by runtime_version?

Allison Grant: Yes if runtime_version exists. That chart is super useful. It’s basically a stacked area: requests by runtime_version. During rollout you want to see the curve shift.

Marcus Reed: And overlay perf metrics by GPU: tokens/sec, queue time, batch size distribution. Scheduler regressions often show up as queue_time spikes not just p95.

Sean Gallagher: Queue time = the scheduler wait time metric? Do we have that in dd?

Allison Grant: We have `scheduler_queue_ms` histogram from runtime, but it’s currently not tagged with version (only host). That’s one of the action items.

Sergio Costa: Ok action items:
1) Add/propagate `runtime_channel` + `runtime_version` tags to gateway request metrics.
2) Propagate same tags to runtime exporter metrics (scheduler queue, batch metrics, kv cache, etc).
3) Create SLO toolkit config for LTS availability + latency.
4) Dashboards + monitors for:
- LTS burn rate (paging)
- LTS 5xx spike (paging)
- LTS p95 regression vs baseline (warn -> page only if burn confirms)
- Rollout adoption (% traffic by version) (non-page + #eng-releases)

Rafael Mendes: On paging: who is primary? SRE on-call + runtime on-call? Or SRE only?

Sean Gallagher: For pilot: SRE primary, runtime secondary. Once stable we can revisit. But the page message needs to say “LTS SLO burn” explicitly.

Connor O'Brien: Please also post to #eng-releases automatically when LTS monitor triggers during patch week. We’ll have Release Eng watching but not necessarily on pager rotation.

Sergio Costa: We can route:
- PagerDuty: `runtime-sre` service
- Slack: #eng-releases + #eng-runtime (warning)
- Add runbook links in monitor body (release cockpit + rollback steps)

Allison Grant: Runbook link should point to the new LTS patch runbook section “Rollout monitoring + rollback signals”. Connor can you drop link once it’s in Confluence?

Connor O'Brien: Will do. It’s in draft now, will be published before first patch.

Datadog Bot: Allison Grant added a comment on monitor draft "[WIP] Runtime LTS Latency Burn Rate".

Jonas Weber: One more: thresholds. For p95 regression, I’d suggest per-workload thresholds are hard; start with absolute for core routes:
- chat.completions p95 > baseline * 1.10 for 15m (warn)
- embeddings p95 > baseline * 1.15 for 15m (warn)
But don’t page unless error budget burn rate > 2x as well.

Allison Grant: Agree. Also need GPU-specific panels. A100 vs H100 can diverge. Can we tag GPU SKU from node labels into metrics?

Sergio Costa: We already have `gpu_sku` in infra metrics; request metrics don’t have it. Might be joinable by cluster, but not ideal. I’ll see if we can enrich at collector level.

Sean Gallagher: Timeline: can we have v0 dashboard + 2 monitors by end of week for pilot readiness?

Sergio Costa: Yes for gateway + SLO. Runtime exporter tagging might slip depending on how invasive. I can do v0 using k8s `image_tag` + host mapping, then iterate.

Connor O'Brien: If v0 is “good enough for patch week”, that’s fine. But we should track the proper tagging as a must-have for GA.

Rafael Mendes: +1. Also please add a small panel for “release annotations”: when we start rollout / pause / rollback. Otherwise people guess based on graphs.

Allison Grant: We can add manual annotations from the release script or just a button in dd notebook. Connor/Devon might be able to automate.

Connor O'Brien: I’ll ask Devon Price if we can have release automation drop an annotation with runtime_version + stage.

Sergio Costa: Cool. I’ll open a PR in slo-toolkit for `runtime-lts-*` SLOs + start a dashboard draft and share link here.

Sean Gallagher: Thanks. Please include in the dashboard:
- error budget remaining (7d/30d)
- burn rate (1h/6h)
- p50/p95 latency by route
- 5xx + timeouts
- scheduler queue time
- adoption by runtime_version
- breakdown by region + ring

Allison Grant: I’ll work with Sergio on the panels + make sure the metric queries match what perf gates look at.

Sergio Costa: Posted draft board link in #eng-sre pins once it’s not embarrassing :)

Rafael Mendes: :thumbsup: Let’s make sure the monitors have clear “what to do” text. Half the battle is not digging through 5 dashboards at 2am.

Sean Gallagher: +1. Add “if during rollout: pause at ring X” and “rollback to previous LTS patch” guidance in the alert body. We’ll iterate after first patch retro.

source.text:
Sean Gallagher: We need to lock perf regression thresholds for Runtime LTS gates this week. Current proposal in doc is a bit hand-wavy. Can we align on p50/p95 + throughput + what to do when benches are noisy?

Allison Grant: +1. For LTS we should bias towards catching p95 regressions even if it means occasional reruns.

Connor O'Brien: Release Eng perspective: thresholds need to be deterministic enough that patch week isn’t "rerun until green". Also need an explicit waiver path (who can override, what evidence required).

Marcus Reed: Here’s what we’ve been using informally for mainline perf-canary:
- latency p50 regression <= +5%
- latency p95 regression <= +8%
- tok/s throughput regression <= -3%
…but the variance on A100 runners is sometimes +/-4% on p95, depending on contention.

Jonas Weber: For LTS, I’d tighten p95, but only if we also change methodology (more repetitions / better aggregation). Otherwise we’ll just create flakes.

Claire Dubois: Agree. Our measurement noise is dominated by runner contention + occasional thermal / clock drift. If we keep single-run comparisons, any threshold <~6% on p95 will be flaky.

Rafael Mendes: From incident history: p95 regressions are what trigger customer escalations. I’d rather have a gating system that forces a second look (rerun + manual review) than ship a p95 regression because "it was within +8%".

Sean Gallagher: Ok, let’s separate:
(1) what numbers constitute "hard fail" vs "needs review"
(2) rerun policy
(3) waiver policy.

Kira Volkov: I can implement whatever logic in the gate parser, but need exact rules. Right now perf-canary outputs a JSON summary w/ deltas vs baseline.

Marcus Reed: Suggestion:
- Run 3 iterations per benchmark scenario, use median for p50/p95, and mean for throughput.
- Compare against baseline median-of-3.
That will cut noise without exploding runtime too much.

Claire Dubois: median-of-3 is good. For throughput, use median too. Mean is sensitive to one slow run.

Jonas Weber: +1 median everywhere. Also we should pin GPU clocks (where possible) + isolate runners, but that’s infra work.

Allison Grant: Thresholds proposal (LTS):
- p50 latency: warn at +3%, fail at +6%
- p95 latency: warn at +4%, fail at +7%
- throughput tok/s: warn at -2%, fail at -5%
This assumes median-of-3.

Connor O'Brien: That seems workable. Q: do we apply per-workload, or aggregate across canonical workloads?

Marcus Reed: Per-workload + per-GPU SKU. We’ve seen regressions only show up on H100 for long-context.

Sergio Costa: If we do per-workload + per-SKU, we need to be strict about the benchmark set. Otherwise every patch will be 2h of benchmarks.

Sean Gallagher: Right, scope it. For LTS patches, canonical set should be:
- short prompts (128-512)
- mixed prompts (128-4k)
- long context (8k+)
- with/without streaming
and GPU SKUs: A100 80GB + H100 SXM (at least those two).

Claire Dubois: Please also include one "scheduler stress" scenario (bursty arrivals). That’s where p95 gets weird.

Marcus Reed: We already have a bursty queue scenario in perf-canary, it’s just not part of default gating. I can add it.

Perf Canary Bot: Benchmark summary available: https://perf-canary.redwood.dev/runs/1741025512 (note: runner contention detected on a100-03)

Rafael Mendes: lol bot calling out contention. That’s exactly why we need a standard "noisy run" procedure.

Allison Grant: Noisy run rule of thumb:
- If contention detected OR variance across the 3 iterations > 5% on p95, auto-mark as "inconclusive" and require rerun on a different runner.

Kira Volkov: I can code:
- compute coefficient of variation (CV) across the 3 runs; if CV > threshold, flag as noisy.
But we need to pick numbers.

Jonas Weber: CV on p95 is usually high even when fine. Maybe simpler:
- if max(p95) - min(p95) > 7% of median(p95) => noisy.
Same for throughput.

Claire Dubois: +1. CV is messy with small N.

Sean Gallagher: Let’s lock:
- Noisy if (max-min)/median > 0.07 for p95 OR throughput.
- Noisy if perf-canary marks "contention detected".
Noisy => rerun once. If rerun also noisy, manual review required (don’t block forever).

Connor O'Brien: How does manual review work? We need an explicit waiver process that’s auditable.

Rafael Mendes: Proposed waiver:
- Release manager (Connor or delegate) can request waiver.
- Requires: perf owner (Marcus) + SRE on-call manager (Rafael or Sean) sign-off.
- Requires attaching evidence: links to both runs + justification (e.g. change is scheduler-only, regression isolated to non-customer workload, etc.).
- Must add a line item in LTS release notes under "Performance" that a waiver was granted + what to monitor.

Allison Grant: +1, and waiver should be time-bounded: "waiver applies only to this patch" not permanent.

Marcus Reed: Also add: if fail threshold exceeded (not just warn), waiver requires a third run on isolated runner (if available). I don’t want us waving away a -8% throughput hit.

Sergio Costa: And for monitoring, we should tag the rollout w/ an "LTS-perf-waiver" annotation so dashboards can be watched. I can add that to SLO dashboards.

Kira Volkov: So logic:
- WARN band: gate is yellow -> requires perf review comment but can proceed.
- FAIL band: gate is red -> blocks unless waiver.
- If NOISY: gate is gray -> rerun required.
That ok?

Sean Gallagher: Yes. But define what "requires perf review comment" means.

Connor O'Brien: For WARN, we’ll require a comment in the release PR:
"Perf gate WARN on <workload>/<sku> with delta <x>. Reviewed by <name>. Acceptable because <reason>. Monitoring plan: <link>."
No separate waiver needed.

Rafael Mendes: For FAIL + waiver, do it as a checklist item with sign-off lines. Something like:
- [ ] Waiver requested by:
- [ ] Approved by Perf (Marcus):
- [ ] Approved by SRE (Sean/Rafael):
- [ ] Additional run link (if required):
- [ ] Release notes updated:

Allison Grant: Can we also say: any p95 fail (> +7%) on canonical long-context workload is basically "no waiver unless exec sponsor"? That’s the one that hurts.

Sean Gallagher: I’d rather avoid exec escalation in the written policy. Maybe: "waivers for p95 fail on long-context require explicit SRE Director approval"… but we don’t have that role listed for this project.

Connor O'Brien: Let’s keep it to named roles for now. We can state: "p95 fail on long-context requires SRE + Runtime leads sync before proceeding". (Not a formal new approver, just required discussion.)

Marcus Reed: Works.

Claire Dubois: One more: baseline drift. If baseline is older than 30 days, the "delta" might be less meaningful due to driver updates / infra changes. Can we pin baseline to the LTS branch cut tag?

Connor O'Brien: Yes, baseline should be "branch cut baseline" for that LTS line, updated only when we intentionally re-baseline (with note). That’s already in the draft, but we’ll make it explicit.

Kira Volkov: Cool. I’ll update the gate config with:
- warn/fail thresholds
- noisy detection and rerun required
- output includes links to runs + which runner

Sergio Costa: I’ll add a dashboard panel that overlays release tag + perf gate status + p95 deltas during rollout.

Sean Gallagher: Summarizing what we’re locking (reply if objections):
1) Method: median-of-3 runs per scenario/SKU for p50/p95/throughput.
2) Threshold bands (LTS):
   - p50 latency: warn +3%, fail +6%
   - p95 latency: warn +4%, fail +7%
   - throughput tok/s: warn -2%, fail -5%
3) Noisy criteria: contention detected OR (max-min)/median > 7% on p95 or throughput.
   - Noisy => rerun once on different runner if possible.
4) WARN handling: perf owner review comment in release PR.
5) FAIL handling: blocks unless waiver.
6) Waiver: requested by release manager; approvals required from Perf owner + SRE (Sean/Rafael). Evidence: run links, rationale, monitoring plan, and release notes entry.
7) Extra scrutiny: any p95 FAIL on long-context workload requires synchronous review between Release Eng + Perf owner + SRE before waiver.

Allison Grant: +1 :thumbsup:

Rafael Mendes: +1

Marcus Reed: +1 (and I’ll make sure long-context scenario is included in the canonical set for LTS)

Connor O'Brien: +1, I’ll incorporate into the LTS patch runbook + release PR template.

Claire Dubois: +1, thanks. Please also capture the baseline policy in the doc (baseline pinned to branch cut; rebaseline is an explicit change w/ sign-off).

Sean Gallagher: Will do. Kira, once config lands, tag me on the PR.

Kira Volkov: yep, will do today.

Claire Dubois: One more: baseline drift. If baseline is older than 30 days, the "delta" might be less meaningful due to driver updates / infra changes. Can we pin baseline to the LTS branch cut tag?

Connor O'Brien: Yes, baseline should be "branch cut baseline" for that LTS line, updated only when we intentionally re-baseline (with note). That’s already in the draft, but we’ll make it explicit.

Kira Volkov: Cool. I’ll update the gate config with:
- warn/fail thresholds
- noisy detection and rerun required
- output includes links to runs + which runner

Sergio Costa: I’ll add a dashboard panel that overlays release tag + perf gate status + p95 deltas during rollout.

Sean Gallagher: Summarizing what we’re locking (reply if objections):
1) Method: median-of-3 runs per scenario/SKU for p50/p95/throughput.
2) Threshold bands (LTS):
   - p50 latency: warn +3%, fail +6%
   - p95 latency: warn +4%, fail +7%
   - throughput tok/s: warn -2%, fail -5%
3) Noisy criteria: contention detected OR (max-min)/median > 7% on p95 or throughput.
   - Noisy => rerun once on different runner if possible.
4) WARN handling: perf owner review comment in release PR.
5) FAIL handling: blocks unless waiver.
6) Waiver: requested by release manager; approvals required from Perf owner + SRE (Sean/Rafael). Evidence: run links, rationale, monitoring plan, and release notes entry.
7) Extra scrutiny: any p95 FAIL on long-context workload requires synchronous review between Release Eng + Perf owner + SRE before waiver.

Allison Grant: +1 :thumbsup:

Rafael Mendes: +1

Marcus Reed: +1 (and I’ll make sure long-context scenario is included in the canonical set for LTS)

Connor O'Brien: +1, I’ll incorporate into the LTS patch runbook + release PR template.

Claire Dubois: +1, thanks. Please also capture the baseline policy in the doc (baseline pinned to branch cut; rebaseline is an explicit change w/ sign-off).

Sean Gallagher: Will do. Kira, once config lands, tag me on the PR.

Kira Volkov: yep, will do today.

source.messages:
Allison Grant: Dropping the cache telemetry incident runbook here for final review before we call it “oncall-ready”: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/PLAT/cache-telemetry-pipeline-incident-runbook

Allison Grant: Would love eyes on (1) symptoms/triage flow, (2) ownership + escalation, (3) what we consider a “real cache regression” vs telemetry missing.

Sean Gallagher: Skimmed. This is solid. Biggest ask: make the first 5 mins super explicit. Like “is data missing?” check before we chase cache runtime issues.

Aisha Bello: +1. Also can we add a quick note about expected diurnal dips / workload mix changes so we don’t page ourselves into oblivion.

Sergio Costa: I’ll add a top section: “Sanity checks (no paging)” w/ links to the ingestion drop panel + metric volume panels.

Logan Wright: On telemetry side, the failure modes list is accurate. One tweak: call out label-drop/cardinality guard as its own class of incident.

Logan Wright: We’ve already had a “looks like 0 hits” that was actually ingestion rejecting high-card label combos.

Nadia Rahman: + add explicit mention of allowed label set + where to find it. People always ask “why is route missing?”

Allison Grant: Yep. I’ll add: “If you see sudden zeros but request volume is normal, check cardinality guard drops”.

Omar El-Sayed: Runtime comments:
- In the “hit-rate drop” section, recommend checking miss_reason breakdown before changing cache knobs.
- We should be careful with the mitigation guidance “increase max_prefix_cache_bytes” — that can blow GPU mem on small cards.

Omar El-Sayed: Maybe phrase as: "consider increasing max_prefix_cache_bytes within approved profile; validate memory pressure gauge".

Sean Gallagher: Agree. Don’t want oncall blindly flipping runtime flags. Keep mitigations conservative.

Ethan Park: Join keys: runbook currently says “model_version from gateway + runtime are consistent”. That’s true post-18426, but during rollout we’ll have mixed tags.

Ethan Park: Suggest adding note: “If model_version is missing, slice by model_family/model_id as fallback; don’t conclude version mismatch until rollout complete”.

Connor O'Brien: +1, and add rollout context: while canarying, some regions may lag. Oncall should check release status first.

Redwood Deploy Bot: Release status: runtime-cache-telemetry v0.9.3-canary is ACTIVE in us-east-1, eu-west-1. Next wave scheduled in 6h.

Allison Grant: Great call. I’ll add a “Check release state” step + link to the deploy dashboard.

Leila Farouk: QA note: runbook should mention counter resets on pod restarts. We saw false “hit-rate cliff” when denominator restarted.

Leila Farouk: If you can add “look for reset markers / use rate() not raw counter” in queries, that’d help.

Sergio Costa: I can update the example queries in the runbook to use `rate()` / `increase()` and include a snippet.

Aisha Bello: Also the alert section: can we clarify when it’s a page vs ticket?

Sean Gallagher: Paging policy:
- Page only if (a) cache_efficiency_guardrail breached AND (b) request volume stable AND (c) telemetry health green.
- If telemetry health red -> ticket Telemetry, don’t page runtime.

Sean Gallagher: Put that logic in the runbook and also in the alert annotations.

Allison Grant: Noted. I’ll add a simple decision table.

Logan Wright: Ownership: who owns ongoing runbook maintenance? Telemetry docs change a lot.

Allison Grant: Proposal:
- Primary owner: Observability/SRE (me) for the runbook structure + triage flow.
- Section owners: Telemetry ingestion (Logan) + Runtime metrics semantics (Omar) + Tagging/join keys (Ethan).
- Sean as approver for paging policy changes.

Omar El-Sayed: Works. For runtime semantics, loop in Samir when we change miss_reason taxonomy.

Nadia Rahman: I can be backup for schema/label standards. Please add link to the naming/cardinality policy doc.

Ethan Park: I’ll take tagging/join keys section. I’ll add a short “how to verify route_id/model_version present” checklist.

Connor O'Brien: I can own the “rollout state / known limitations” subsection as long as it’s not too deep.

Allison Grant: Perfect. I’ll add an “Owners” block at top w/ @mentions and a quarterly review reminder.

Aisha Bello: Can we schedule the first review for 2 weeks post-prod rollout? Things always shift after first customer ticket.

Sean Gallagher: Do it. Also add to oncall handoff checklist: “verify cache telemetry dashboards not broken”.

Allison Grant: Action items:
1) Allison: add first-5-min sanity checks + page vs ticket decision table + owners block.
2) Sergio: update example queries (rate/increase) + add cardinality-drop panel link.
3) Logan/Nadia: provide exact wording for cardinality guard + label allowlist references.
4) Omar: revise mitigation wording around cache bytes / memory pressure.
5) Ethan/Connor: add rollout/join-key caveats.

Allison Grant: Targeting EOD tomorrow for final publish.

Sean Gallagher: :thumbsup: once those are in, consider it approved.

Logan Wright: :thumbsup: I’ll drop the label allowlist link + “what gets dropped” explanation in-thread.

Omar El-Sayed: :thumbsup: Will PR the mitigation wording and add note re: version_mismatch semantics.

Sergio Costa: I’ll update the panels + queries this afternoon and ping when ready.

Allison Grant: Thanks all. Closing the loop once edits are merged + runbook is marked “Production”.

source.messages:
Aisha Bello: hey folks — for the quantization profile beta canary, we need a few new dashboard panels ASAP. can someone confirm metric names + labels so we don’t invent new ones?

Aisha Bello: panels requested (per route + rollup):
- enabled routes (count + % of traffic)
- fallback rate (applied vs fallback vs disabled)
- error rate (5xx + policy/runtime errors)
- latency (p50/p95 decode + total)
- cost/token delta if we have it (nice to have)

Aisha Bello: also would like filters: `route_id`, `tenant_id` (or org), `model_id`, `region`, `deployment_tier`.

Logan Wright: :eyes: yep, we should align on names. what’s currently exported from runtime vs control plane? (don’t want 2 sources).

Caleb Johnson: runtime already emits request-level counters + histos w/ `quant_profile_id` annotation (post-18555). control plane has config state but not request truth.

Nadia Rahman: cost/token delta is in the cost attribution pipeline, but it’s lagging + sampled. better to show “tokens/sec + GPU util + avg prompt/decode tokens” as leading indicators; we can add cost later.

Aisha Bello: makes sense. for “enabled routes” i was thinking we can show % of traffic where profile is applied (not just config says enabled).

Caleb Johnson: +1. config toggle alone doesn’t mean applied (compat can fail). i’d recommend:
- `redwood_inference_requests_total{quant_resolution="applied|fallback|disabled"}`
- optionally break out fallback reason.

Allison Grant: jumping in — we should keep metric prefix consistent with other runtime panels. i think it’s `redwood.runtime.*` in OTEL -> Prom bridge? can we paste exact names?

Logan Wright: quick check from metrics catalog (latest):
- Counter: `redwood_runtime_requests_total`
  labels: `route_id`, `model_id`, `region`, `deployment_tier`, `status_code`, `quant_resolution`, `quant_profile_id` (empty if disabled)
- Counter: `redwood_runtime_quant_fallback_total`
  labels: above + `fallback_reason`
- Histogram: `redwood_runtime_request_latency_ms_bucket` (and _sum/_count)
  labels: above + `phase` (prefill|decode|total)

Logan Wright: does that match what you’re seeing, @Caleb?

Caleb Johnson: mostly. small correction: latency is split into two histos right now:
- `redwood_runtime_phase_latency_ms` (histo) w/ `phase=prefill|decode`
- `redwood_runtime_total_latency_ms` (histo) no phase label

Caleb Johnson: labels include `route_id`, `model_id`, `region`, `deployment_tier`, `quant_resolution`, `quant_profile_id`. we dropped `status_code` label on latency to reduce cardinality.

Allison Grant: thank you. please avoid `tenant_id` label on runtime metrics — that will explode. we can filter by tenant via route mapping (Console can show route->tenant), but dashboards should be route-centric.

Aisha Bello: ok, we’ll do route-centric. for “error rate”, can we use `redwood_runtime_requests_total` w/ `status_class` label? or just status_code?

Logan Wright: `status_code` is on the requests counter, yes. we also have `error_class` for internal failures:
- `redwood_runtime_errors_total{error_class="policy_incompatible|profile_fetch|kernel_unsupported|oom|timeout"}`

Chloe Martin: on control plane side, we can add a panel for “routes with config enabled” from `redwood_controlplane_route_quant_setting` gauge (0/1) if SRE wants to compare configured vs applied.

Aisha Bello: that comparison panel would be super useful (esp for incompat). what’s the gauge name + labels?

Ethan Park: gauge is `redwood_controlplane_route_quant_enabled` (0/1). labels: `route_id`, `deployment_tier`, `requested_profile_ref` (string), `resolved_profile_id` (string, can be empty), `compatibility="compatible|incompatible|unknown"`.

Ethan Park: (note: no region label here; region happens at routing/runtime).

Connor O'Brien: for alerts during canary, we’re planning:
- fallback_rate > 1% sustained 10m on canary routes
- error_rate (5xx) > baseline + 0.2% for 5m
- p95 total latency > baseline + 10% for 10m

Connor O'Brien: so pls include panels that make those easy to read + a quick “baseline vs quant” breakdown.

Aisha Bello: got it. metric names summary (tell me if wrong):
- applied/fallback breakdown: `redwood_runtime_requests_total` w/ `quant_resolution`
- fallback reasons: `redwood_runtime_quant_fallback_total` w/ `fallback_reason`
- errors: `redwood_runtime_errors_total`
- latency: `redwood_runtime_total_latency_ms` + `redwood_runtime_phase_latency_ms`
- config enabled: `redwood_controlplane_route_quant_enabled`

Aisha Bello: labels we’ll depend on: `route_id`, `model_id`, `region`, `deployment_tier`, `quant_profile_id`, `quant_resolution`, `fallback_reason`.

Caleb Johnson: yep. also `quant_profile_id` will be `"none"` not empty in some exporters — handle both.

Logan Wright: +1 and please don’t group by `quant_profile_id` by default (cardinality). make it a drilldown.

Allison Grant: :thumbsup: also make sure fallback panel includes both “fallback” and “disabled” so it’s obvious when kill-switch is on.

Aisha Bello: perfect. i’ll update the dashboard JSON + add 3 panels:
1) Traffic by quant_resolution (stacked) + filter by route_id
2) Fallback rate + fallback_reason breakdown
3) Config enabled vs applied % (side-by-side)

Incident Bot: (info) Reminder: use folder `Inference / Quant Profiles` in Grafana and tag dashboards with `team:sre` and `feature:quant-profiles`.

Chloe Martin: when you have the dashboard link, drop it here — i can sanity check the control plane gauge in staging.

Connor O'Brien: pls aim EOD today so we can include in tomorrow’s canary checklist.

Aisha Bello: will do. thanks all.

Caleb Johnson: yep. also `quant_profile_id` will be `"none"` not empty in some exporters — handle both.

Logan Wright: +1 and please don’t group by `quant_profile_id` by default (cardinality). make it a drilldown.

Allison Grant: :thumbsup: also make sure fallback panel includes both “fallback” and “disabled” so it’s obvious when kill-switch is on.

Aisha Bello: perfect. i’ll update the dashboard JSON + add 3 panels:
1) Traffic by quant_resolution (stacked) + filter by route_id
2) Fallback rate + fallback_reason breakdown
3) Config enabled vs applied % (side-by-side)

Incident Bot: (info) Reminder: use folder `Inference / Quant Profiles` in Grafana and tag dashboards with `team:sre` and `feature:quant-profiles`.

Chloe Martin: when you have the dashboard link, drop it here — i can sanity check the control plane gauge in staging.

Connor O'Brien: pls aim EOD today so we can include in tomorrow’s canary checklist.

Aisha Bello: will do. thanks all.
text:
Aisha Bello: hey folks — for the quantization profile beta canary, we need a few new dashboard panels ASAP. can someone confirm metric names + labels so we don’t invent new ones?

Aisha Bello: panels requested (per route + rollup):
- enabled routes (count + % of traffic)
- fallback rate (applied vs fallback vs disabled)
- error rate (5xx + policy/runtime errors)
- latency (p50/p95 decode + total)
- cost/token delta if we have it (nice to have)

Aisha Bello: also would like filters: `route_id`, `tenant_id` (or org), `model_id`, `region`, `deployment_tier`.

Logan Wright: :eyes: yep, we should align on names. what’s currently exported from runtime vs control plane? (don’t want 2 sources).

Caleb Johnson: runtime already emits request-level counters + histos w/ `quant_profile_id` annotation (post-18555). control plane has config state but not request truth.

Nadia Rahman: cost/token delta is in the cost attribution pipeline, but it’s lagging + sampled. better to show “tokens/sec + GPU util + avg prompt/decode tokens” as leading indicators; we can add cost later.

Aisha Bello: makes sense. for “enabled routes” i was thinking we can show % of traffic where profile is applied (not just config says enabled).

Caleb Johnson: +1. config toggle alone doesn’t mean applied (compat can fail). i’d recommend:
- `redwood_inference_requests_total{quant_resolution="applied|fallback|disabled"}`
- optionally break out fallback reason.

Allison Grant: jumping in — we should keep metric prefix consistent with other runtime panels. i think it’s `redwood.runtime.*` in OTEL -> Prom bridge? can we paste exact names?

Logan Wright: quick check from metrics catalog (latest):
- Counter: `redwood_runtime_requests_total`
  labels: `route_id`, `model_id`, `region`, `deployment_tier`, `status_code`, `quant_resolution`, `quant_profile_id` (empty if disabled)
- Counter: `redwood_runtime_quant_fallback_total`
  labels: above + `fallback_reason`
- Histogram: `redwood_runtime_request_latency_ms_bucket` (and _sum/_count)
  labels: above + `phase` (prefill|decode|total)

Logan Wright: does that match what you’re seeing, @Caleb?

Caleb Johnson: mostly. small correction: latency is split into two histos right now:
- `redwood_runtime_phase_latency_ms` (histo) w/ `phase=prefill|decode`
- `redwood_runtime_total_latency_ms` (histo) no phase label

Caleb Johnson: labels include `route_id`, `model_id`, `region`, `deployment_tier`, `quant_resolution`, `quant_profile_id`. we dropped `status_code` label on latency to reduce cardinality.

Allison Grant: thank you. please avoid `tenant_id` label on runtime metrics — that will explode. we can filter by tenant via route mapping (Console can show route->tenant), but dashboards should be route-centric.

Aisha Bello: ok, we’ll do route-centric. for “error rate”, can we use `redwood_runtime_requests_total` w/ `status_class` label? or just status_code?

Logan Wright: `status_code` is on the requests counter, yes. we also have `error_class` for internal failures:
- `redwood_runtime_errors_total{error_class="policy_incompatible|profile_fetch|kernel_unsupported|oom|timeout"}`

Chloe Martin: on control plane side, we can add a panel for “routes with config enabled” from `redwood_controlplane_route_quant_setting` gauge (0/1) if SRE wants to compare configured vs applied.

Aisha Bello: that comparison panel would be super useful (esp for incompat). what’s the gauge name + labels?

Ethan Park: gauge is `redwood_controlplane_route_quant_enabled` (0/1). labels: `route_id`, `deployment_tier`, `requested_profile_ref` (string), `resolved_profile_id` (string, can be empty), `compatibility="compatible|incompatible|unknown"`.

Ethan Park: (note: no region label here; region happens at routing/runtime).

Connor O'Brien: for alerts during canary, we’re planning:
- fallback_rate > 1% sustained 10m on canary routes
- error_rate (5xx) > baseline + 0.2% for 5m
- p95 total latency > baseline + 10% for 10m

Connor O'Brien: so pls include panels that make those easy to read + a quick “baseline vs quant” breakdown.

Aisha Bello: got it. metric names summary (tell me if wrong):
- applied/fallback breakdown: `redwood_runtime_requests_total` w/ `quant_resolution`
- fallback reasons: `redwood_runtime_quant_fallback_total` w/ `fallback_reason`
- errors: `redwood_runtime_errors_total`
- latency: `redwood_runtime_total_latency_ms` + `redwood_runtime_phase_latency_ms`
- config enabled: `redwood_controlplane_route_quant_enabled`

Aisha Bello: labels we’ll depend on: `route_id`, `model_id`, `region`, `deployment_tier`, `quant_profile_id`, `quant_resolution`, `fallback_reason`.

Caleb Johnson: yep. also `quant_profile_id` will be `"none"` not empty in some exporters — handle both.

Logan Wright: +1 and please don’t group by `quant_profile_id` by default (cardinality). make it a drilldown.

Allison Grant: :thumbsup: also make sure fallback panel includes both “fallback” and “disabled” so it’s obvious when kill-switch is on.

Aisha Bello: perfect. i’ll update the dashboard JSON + add 3 panels:
1) Traffic by quant_resolution (stacked) + filter by route_id
2) Fallback rate + fallback_reason breakdown
3) Config enabled vs applied % (side-by-side)

Incident Bot: (info) Reminder: use folder `Inference / Quant Profiles` in Grafana and tag dashboards with `team:sre` and `feature:quant-profiles`.

Chloe Martin: when you have the dashboard link, drop it here — i can sanity check the control plane gauge in staging.

Connor O'Brien: pls aim EOD today so we can include in tomorrow’s canary checklist.

Aisha Bello: will do. thanks all.

source.text:
Sean Gallagher: seeing a dip on the cache hit-rate dashboard in hosted. question for oncall policy: do we page on this kind of hit-rate drop or just ticket it?

Sean Gallagher: screenshot-ish numbers (global hosted): prefix_hr down ~0.44 -> ~0.36 over ~25m. kv reuse also down a bit. no customer-reported issues yet.

Allison Grant: pls don’t page on hit-rate drop alone. we said guardrail only unless there’s evidence of regression (pressure/evictions/telemetry missing). does it line up w/ memory pressure?

Sean Gallagher: checking now. pressure gauge looks flat-ish, hovering ~0.62. evictions not spiking. wasted prefill ratio up though (0.08 -> 0.12).

Aisha Bello: wasted prefill can go up with traffic mix shifts too (more novel prompts / longer first turns). did prompt length buckets shift?

Sean Gallagher: yeah, prompt-length histogram shifted toward 4k-8k bucket in the last hour. also more traffic on /chat/stream route.

Allison Grant: that sounds like mix change. I’d ticket it + annotate dashboard (workload shift) but no page.

Sergio Costa: +1. if we page on this we’re going to get flappy pages every time a big tenant changes behavior.

Neha Kapoor: agree. paging criteria should require *platform signal* (pressure > threshold OR eviction rate up OR telemetry missing / ingestion drop). hit-rate dips by themselves are expected.

Sean Gallagher: ok but what about customer impact? lower hit rate => cost up, maybe latency up.

Aisha Bello: for paging, I’d rather key off latency SLO + error rate. cache is a lever; a hit-rate dip only pages if it’s likely a regression we can fix quickly.

Logan Wright: also want to differentiate “real dip” vs “metrics weird”. any sign of ingestion label drops / sample rate drop?

Sean Gallagher: telemetry sample rate looks normal. not a “flatline to zero” situation. so not telemetry-missing.

Allison Grant: then definitely ticket.

Connor O'Brien: fyi we did a small routing tweak earlier today to push more traffic to us-east for capacity reasons. could also be locality issues (less cache reuse across nodes).

Sean Gallagher: that’s interesting. could cause lower reuse without pressure. still: ticket, not page.

Sergio Costa: if we want to automate that logic: 
- PAGE only if (hr_now < hr_7d - 0.12) AND (kv_memory_pressure > 0.90 OR eviction_rate > 2x hr_7d) AND qps > X
- otherwise SEV4 ticket (triage)

Aisha Bello: and add “mix shift guardrail” in runbook: if prompt-length distribution changes by >N% (e.g. 1k+ bucket share up) treat as likely workload-driven.

Allison Grant: careful on “mix shift detector” in alerts, might be complicated. but we can put it in triage checklist.

Neha Kapoor: +1. keep alert rules simple, keep runbook smart.

Sean Gallagher: ok decision: no page for this event. I’ll open a ticket to look into routing/locality + confirm no regression.

Logan Wright: when you file the ticket, include miss-reason breakdown too. if version_mismatch/hash_mismatch spikes, it’s a rollout issue; if capacity/eviction spikes, it’s runtime/infra.

Sean Gallagher: will do.

Observability Bot: created Jira ticket https://redwood-inference.atlassian.net/browse/OBS-2147 (summary: "Hosted prefix_hr dip ~25m; likely workload mix shift / routing locality")

Allison Grant: thanks. can someone (Sergio?) add a short note to the alert docs: “hit-rate dip == ticket unless pressure/evictions/telemetry missing”.

Sergio Costa: yep, I’ll update the alert annotations in observability-pack + link to runbook section.

Aisha Bello: also suggest we add an annotation stream on the dashboard for routing changes / model rollouts so these are obvious.

Connor O'Brien: can do. we already emit deploy annotations; I can add routing config change annotations (at least for hosted).

Neha Kapoor: perfect. goal is fewer false pages, faster triage.

Sean Gallagher: closing loop: ticket filed, no page. will post update if it turns into pressure/evictions/latency.

Allison Grant: :thumbsup:

source.text:
Sergio Costa: Heads up: after the last couple burst beta exercises, the new burst alerts are *chatty*. We had ~14 pages in 24h, most looked like short-lived saturation / scrape gaps. Want to tune before we re-enable for the next beta tenants.

Sean Gallagher: yeah agree. goal is still: fast detection for neighbor impact + protect non-burst SLOs. But we can’t page on every 90s blip.

Allison Grant: I pulled the alert history. Biggest offenders:
- `BurstPoolSaturationHigh` firing on single-scrape spikes (pool_free_gpus dips to 0 for 1-2 samples)
- `BurstDenialRateHigh` in eu-west during telemetry lag (denial reasons flip to `TELEMETRY_STALE` for ~3-4m)
- label cardinality causing Grafana panel timeouts (per-tenant + per-model labels on the same alert)

Rafael Mendes: +1 on the cardinality. Also the page noise made oncall start ignoring the channel which is… not ideal.

Peter Holtz: from routing side, the “bad” events we actually care about looked like:
- non-burst tenants p95 jump + tail amplification
- admission controller latency / timeouts (routing waits, then falls back)
- circuit breaker opening too late
The saturation spikes by themselves weren’t harmful when the fallback policy did the right thing.

Keiko Yamamoto: infra perspective: pool telemetry is noisy at 1m windows. autoscaler + binpack reshuffles cause brief `free=0` but it’s not a real “no capacity” situation unless it persists.

Sergio Costa: proposal v1:
1) change `BurstPoolSaturationHigh` from `free_gpus == 0` to `pool_utilization > 0.98` AND `queue_depth > 0` for 10m.
2) demote that to ticket unless also `burst_admit_latency_p95 > 250ms`.

Sean Gallagher: ok. but we need explicit neighbor protection alert that stays paging.

Allison Grant: we have the neighbor signal in the metrics taxonomy draft: `dedicated_nonburst_p95_latency_delta` (computed vs rolling baseline per tenant route).
Right now the alert is:
- page if delta > 15% for 5m for *any* tenant
That’s why it’s paging constantly for small tenants.

Logan Wright: careful: that delta is super sensitive when baseline is low traffic. We need a min sample threshold or traffic gate, otherwise the stats are garbage.

Rafael Mendes: can we gate by request rate? e.g. only evaluate if `rps > 1` (or whatever) and `n_requests >= 500` in the window.

Allison Grant: yep. We can do `sum(rate(requests_total[5m])) by (tenant) > X` as a condition. Also we should move from “any tenant pages” to “top-N impacted tenants + pool-level signal”.

Peter Holtz: also could page on *aggregate* non-burst impact across the pool:
- `p95_latency_delta_nonburst_weighted` > 20% for 10m
and then have a dashboard panel showing per-tenant breakdown.

Sean Gallagher: I like: page on pool-level impact, ticket on per-tenant outliers.

Sergio Costa: ok so:
- Paging alert: `NonBurstNeighborImpactPool` if weighted p95 delta > 20% for 10m AND nonburst error rate doesn’t explain it (to avoid paging on unrelated deploy issues).
- Ticket alert: `NonBurstNeighborImpactTenant` if a specific tenant delta > 30% for 15m with traffic gate.

Keiko Yamamoto: thresholds: 20% for 10m seems reasonable. we saw real “bad” events were more like 35-50% sustained.

Allison Grant: +1. Also add a separate page for `tail_amp_ratio` (p99/p50) if it jumps. Tail amplification was the first symptom during the beta overload case.

Logan Wright: if we add tail amp, please don’t label it with tenant+model+route. Keep it at `{region, hw_class, pool}`. Otherwise we’re back to cardinality hell.

Sergio Costa: agreed. We’ll keep alert labels minimal:
- region
- hw_class
- pool
- severity
and link to a drilldown dashboard for tenant/model.

Rafael Mendes: what about `BurstDenialRateHigh`? it paged a bunch but the customer experience was just “more 429s” which is expected at cap.

Peter Holtz: denial rate alone isn’t enough. paging only if denial reason indicates a platform fault:
- `ADMISSION_TIMEOUT`
- `POLICY_EVAL_ERROR`
- `TELEMETRY_STALE` (if > 10m)
If it’s `TENANT_CAP_REACHED` or `POOL_CAP_REACHED`, that’s not a page; it’s either expected or an account config problem.

Allison Grant: we already have denial reason codes. We can do alert filtering with `reason=~"ADMISSION_TIMEOUT|POLICY_EVAL_ERROR"`.
For telemetry stale: page if `telemetry_lag_seconds_p95 > 120` for 10m (since that breaks determinism + fairness).

Sean Gallagher: +1. telemetry lag should be paging because it causes unfair denials + can hide real saturation.

Keiko Yamamoto: make sure we don’t page during planned metric pipeline deploys.

Logan Wright: we can add a maintenance silencer in the deploy pipeline; or better, use the existing `telemetry_pipeline_maintenance` flag as an inhibit.

Sergio Costa: action items:
- [Sergio] update Grafana alerts: add traffic gate + window changes + severity split (page vs ticket)
- [Allison] define the weighted pool-level nonburst p95 delta query + tail amp query (low-cardinality)
- [Logan] confirm min sample thresholds that won’t misfire for low-traffic tenants
- [Peter] confirm which denial reasons are considered platform faults vs expected caps

Sean Gallagher: also: align with rollout gate. If `NonBurstNeighborImpactPool` pages or burns > X error budget, we should auto-disable burst in that region/hw class.

Sergio Costa: yep, I’ll add an annotation on the paging alert pointing to the kill-switch + runbook section.

Rafael Mendes: can we test the new thresholds in replay mode before we flip? don’t want to find out oncall night.

Allison Grant: we can run them as “mute” alerts for 48h (record-only) and compare against incident timeline.

Sean Gallagher: do that. then we’ll re-review Friday and decide if we’re comfortable re-enabling eu-west.

Sergio Costa: sounds good. I’ll post PR link in this thread once it’s up.

- `TELEMETRY_STALE` (if > 10m)
If it’s `TENANT_CAP_REACHED` or `POOL_CAP_REACHED`, that’s not a page; it’s either expected or an account config problem.

Allison Grant: we already have denial reason codes. We can do alert filtering with `reason=~"ADMISSION_TIMEOUT|POLICY_EVAL_ERROR"`.
For telemetry stale: page if `telemetry_lag_seconds_p95 > 120` for 10m (since that breaks determinism + fairness).

Sean Gallagher: +1. telemetry lag should be paging because it causes unfair denials + can hide real saturation.

Keiko Yamamoto: make sure we don’t page during planned metric pipeline deploys.

Logan Wright: we can add a maintenance silencer in the deploy pipeline; or better, use the existing `telemetry_pipeline_maintenance` flag as an inhibit.

Sergio Costa: action items:
- [Sergio] update Grafana alerts: add traffic gate + window changes + severity split (page vs ticket)
- [Allison] define the weighted pool-level nonburst p95 delta query + tail amp query (low-cardinality)
- [Logan] confirm min sample thresholds that won’t misfire for low-traffic tenants
- [Peter] confirm which denial reasons are considered platform faults vs expected caps

Sean Gallagher: also: align with rollout gate. If `NonBurstNeighborImpactPool` pages or burns > X error budget, we should auto-disable burst in that region/hw class.

Sergio Costa: yep, I’ll add an annotation on the paging alert pointing to the kill-switch + runbook section.

Rafael Mendes: can we test the new thresholds in replay mode before we flip? don’t want to find out oncall night.

Allison Grant: we can run them as “mute” alerts for 48h (record-only) and compare against incident timeline.

Sean Gallagher: do that. then we’ll re-review Friday and decide if we’re comfortable re-enabling eu-west.

Sergio Costa: sounds good. I’ll post PR link in this thread once it’s up.
text:
Sergio Costa: Heads up: after the last couple burst beta exercises, the new burst alerts are *chatty*. We had ~14 pages in 24h, most looked like short-lived saturation / scrape gaps. Want to tune before we re-enable for the next beta tenants.

Sean Gallagher: yeah agree. goal is still: fast detection for neighbor impact + protect non-burst SLOs. But we can’t page on every 90s blip.

Allison Grant: I pulled the alert history. Biggest offenders:
- `BurstPoolSaturationHigh` firing on single-scrape spikes (pool_free_gpus dips to 0 for 1-2 samples)
- `BurstDenialRateHigh` in eu-west during telemetry lag (denial reasons flip to `TELEMETRY_STALE` for ~3-4m)
- label cardinality causing Grafana panel timeouts (per-tenant + per-model labels on the same alert)

Rafael Mendes: +1 on the cardinality. Also the page noise made oncall start ignoring the channel which is… not ideal.

Peter Holtz: from routing side, the “bad” events we actually care about looked like:
- non-burst tenants p95 jump + tail amplification
- admission controller latency / timeouts (routing waits, then falls back)
- circuit breaker opening too late
The saturation spikes by themselves weren’t harmful when the fallback policy did the right thing.

Keiko Yamamoto: infra perspective: pool telemetry is noisy at 1m windows. autoscaler + binpack reshuffles cause brief `free=0` but it’s not a real “no capacity” situation unless it persists.

Sergio Costa: proposal v1:
1) change `BurstPoolSaturationHigh` from `free_gpus == 0` to `pool_utilization > 0.98` AND `queue_depth > 0` for 10m.
2) demote that to ticket unless also `burst_admit_latency_p95 > 250ms`.

Sean Gallagher: ok. but we need explicit neighbor protection alert that stays paging.

Allison Grant: we have the neighbor signal in the metrics taxonomy draft: `dedicated_nonburst_p95_latency_delta` (computed vs rolling baseline per tenant route).
Right now the alert is:
- page if delta > 15% for 5m for *any* tenant
That’s why it’s paging constantly for small tenants.

Logan Wright: careful: that delta is super sensitive when baseline is low traffic. We need a min sample threshold or traffic gate, otherwise the stats are garbage.

Rafael Mendes: can we gate by request rate? e.g. only evaluate if `rps > 1` (or whatever) and `n_requests >= 500` in the window.

Allison Grant: yep. We can do `sum(rate(requests_total[5m])) by (tenant) > X` as a condition. Also we should move from “any tenant pages” to “top-N impacted tenants + pool-level signal”.

Peter Holtz: also could page on *aggregate* non-burst impact across the pool:
- `p95_latency_delta_nonburst_weighted` > 20% for 10m
and then have a dashboard panel showing per-tenant breakdown.

Sean Gallagher: I like: page on pool-level impact, ticket on per-tenant outliers.

Sergio Costa: ok so:
- Paging alert: `NonBurstNeighborImpactPool` if weighted p95 delta > 20% for 10m AND nonburst error rate doesn’t explain it (to avoid paging on unrelated deploy issues).
- Ticket alert: `NonBurstNeighborImpactTenant` if a specific tenant delta > 30% for 15m with traffic gate.

Keiko Yamamoto: thresholds: 20% for 10m seems reasonable. we saw real “bad” events were more like 35-50% sustained.

Allison Grant: +1. Also add a separate page for `tail_amp_ratio` (p99/p50) if it jumps. Tail amplification was the first symptom during the beta overload case.

Logan Wright: if we add tail amp, please don’t label it with tenant+model+route. Keep it at `{region, hw_class, pool}`. Otherwise we’re back to cardinality hell.

Sergio Costa: agreed. We’ll keep alert labels minimal:
- region
- hw_class
- pool
- severity
and link to a drilldown dashboard for tenant/model.

Rafael Mendes: what about `BurstDenialRateHigh`? it paged a bunch but the customer experience was just “more 429s” which is expected at cap.

Peter Holtz: denial rate alone isn’t enough. paging only if denial reason indicates a platform fault:
- `ADMISSION_TIMEOUT`
- `POLICY_EVAL_ERROR`
- `TELEMETRY_STALE` (if > 10m)
If it’s `TENANT_CAP_REACHED` or `POOL_CAP_REACHED`, that’s not a page; it’s either expected or an account config problem.

Allison Grant: we already have denial reason codes. We can do alert filtering with `reason=~"ADMISSION_TIMEOUT|POLICY_EVAL_ERROR"`.
For telemetry stale: page if `telemetry_lag_seconds_p95 > 120` for 10m (since that breaks determinism + fairness).

Sean Gallagher: +1. telemetry lag should be paging because it causes unfair denials + can hide real saturation.

Keiko Yamamoto: make sure we don’t page during planned metric pipeline deploys.

Logan Wright: we can add a maintenance silencer in the deploy pipeline; or better, use the existing `telemetry_pipeline_maintenance` flag as an inhibit.

Sergio Costa: action items:
- [Sergio] update Grafana alerts: add traffic gate + window changes + severity split (page vs ticket)
- [Allison] define the weighted pool-level nonburst p95 delta query + tail amp query (low-cardinality)
- [Logan] confirm min sample thresholds that won’t misfire for low-traffic tenants
- [Peter] confirm which denial reasons are considered platform faults vs expected caps

Sean Gallagher: also: align with rollout gate. If `NonBurstNeighborImpactPool` pages or burns > X error budget, we should auto-disable burst in that region/hw class.

Sergio Costa: yep, I’ll add an annotation on the paging alert pointing to the kill-switch + runbook section.

Rafael Mendes: can we test the new thresholds in replay mode before we flip? don’t want to find out oncall night.

Allison Grant: we can run them as “mute” alerts for 48h (record-only) and compare against incident timeline.

Sean Gallagher: do that. then we’ll re-review Friday and decide if we’re comfortable re-enabling eu-west.

Sergio Costa: sounds good. I’ll post PR link in this thread once it’s up.

source.content:
Rafael Mendes: Can we get a gameday on the calendar for the provision-to-ready rollout? Before we expand beyond us-east/A100 I want to validate incident response + handoffs (Infra vs Platform vs SRE) w/ real dashboards + runbook steps.

Aisha Bello: +1. We’ve got alerts staged but we haven’t exercised the “page vs ticket” thresholds end-to-end. I’d rather find the gaps in a gameday than at 3am.

Miles Thornton: Agree. We should define 3–4 scenarios that map to our top failure modes + a couple that test observability assumptions (cardinality, missing spans, etc).

Sean Gallagher: Do it. Let’s keep it tight (60–90 min) but realistic: pages fire, someone is incident commander, someone is comms, and we practice the mitigation decision tree.

Rafael Mendes: Proposed scenarios (pick 3?):
1) Nodes stuck in `Validating` due to NCCL smoke test flaking/timeouts (fabric jitter)
2) Golden image mismatch: driver/CUDA parity fails after bake (drift)
3) Kubelet join failure (node never reaches `Provisioning -> Validating`), agent heartbeat absent
4) Metric tag/cardinality explosion causes alerting noise + dashboard lag (we already saw hints of this in early metrics)
5) Quarantine spike causes capacity shortfall for a Dedicated pool (business impact)

Vanessa Ortiz: 1 + 2 + 5 are the most representative of what we’re seeing. NCCL flakes + driver parity have been our top “human time sink” issues.

Ethan Park: For 3: if the node never joins, control plane visibility is limited (no agent). But we can simulate it by dropping agent auth or blocking heartbeat writes so state gets stuck in `Provisioning`.

Sergio Costa: Scenario 4 is super valuable. If we don’t practice turning off the noisy dimension + verifying dashboards recover, we’ll repeat the same incident pattern. I can prep a “bad config” branch for the gameday in staging.

Connor O'Brien: Timing: if we want this before Week 4 staged rollout exit, we should do it early next week. Also: we should align with release train windows so we’re not overlapping with other changes.

Aisha Bello: For 1 (NCCL flakes): we should explicitly test the playbook path:
- confirm failure reason in readiness artifacts
- check per-step timing dashboard (is it timeouts vs immediate fail)
- decide: retry validation vs drain+reimage vs quarantine
- confirm we don’t page on a single node (only on fleet-level threshold)

Miles Thornton: For 5 (quarantine spike): we should include business/ops step:
- when do we temporarily relax thresholds vs pause rollout?
- how do we communicate “capacity at risk” to capacity ops + oncall?
- what’s the rollback switch? (feature flag -> stop gating? or stop new nodes joining pool?)

Rafael Mendes: Owners suggestion (happy to adjust):
- Scenario 1 (NCCL): Vanessa (Infra) + Aisha (SRE) as responders
- Scenario 2 (image parity): Sahana (Release Eng) primary, Vanessa secondary (I can loop Sahana)
- Scenario 4 (cardinality/noise): Sergio (Obs) primary, Aisha secondary
- Scenario 5 (quarantine/capacity): Rafael (SRE) + Connor (Release) + Vanessa (Infra)
We’ll rotate who plays IC.

Sean Gallagher: Make sure the gameday has explicit success criteria. E.g. “detect within X min”, “identify root cause within Y”, “mitigation applied safely”, “customer impact assessed + comms draft created”.

incident-bot: Reminder: For gamedays, use the #incidents channel template if you want to practice the workflow. You can create a test incident with `/incident start test`.

Ethan Park: If we do the test incident flow, we should also validate permissions on the readiness APIs (who can mutate state vs read-only). We can add a sub-step: attempt an operator override and ensure it’s audited.

Sergio Costa: I’ll put together a short doc w/ links to:
- Provision-to-ready dashboard folder
- Alert rules (test mode)
- “How to silence safely” guidance
Also I can add a “known-good” query snippet to verify metric health after changes.

Connor O'Brien: Please include a comms plan snippet too (internal only): who posts in #eng-infra / #eng-platform and when. Even in a gameday, it’s good practice.

Vanessa Ortiz: Works. Also: can we explicitly test “rollback image channel candidate -> stable” as a decision point? That’s a new muscle.

Aisha Bello: +1. And verify we don’t flip-flop (cooldown). In gameday we can simulate spike in validation failures and confirm the rollback trigger is clear and not ambiguous.

Rafael Mendes: Cool. I’ll schedule 90 mins Tue 10:00–11:30 PT, invite Infra/Platform/Obs/Release. Agenda:
- 10 min setup (roles, links)
- 60 min scenarios (2–3)
- 20 min retro + action items
I’ll draft the scenario scripts + success criteria and share for review EOD.

Sean Gallagher: Approved. Keep it blameless and focus on “is the system operable” + “are the docs usable by someone who’s not the author”.

Miles Thornton: When you draft scripts, please include the “expected signal” for each step (which metric/log/artifact should confirm the hypothesis). Otherwise people wander.

Rafael Mendes: Will do. I’ll post the doc link here once it’s ready.

Aisha Bello: We need to nail down a measurable “context completeness” score for Incident Bot v2. The ask is simple: do we have deploy context + config diff + saturation signals in the first 5 mins, and can we log it in a way we can analyze week over week.

Aisha Bello: Proposal: score is computed off the *initial* bot context post (not subsequent button clicks). Components:
- Recent deploys card present
- Config diff present (customer-safe)
- Saturation signals present

Aisha Bello: Questions:
1) What counts as “present” vs “missing”? (timeout? permission denied? no data?)
2) Do we weight components equally?
3) Logging format: metrics vs event logs, and where do we send it.

Rafael Mendes: +1 to keeping it tied to the initial auto post. Otherwise folks will mash refresh and we’ll inflate.

Rafael Mendes: I’d keep it dead simple v1:
- Each component yields 0/1
- Score = (deploy + config + saturation) / eligible_components

Rafael Mendes: Eligibility matters: if we can’t resolve service/region from the incident thread context, deploy/saturation might be ineligible. We should not punish the bot for missing identifiers.

Jacob Stein: Agree on eligibility. E.g. config diff depends on tenant + “config scope” (route/policy key). If we don’t have tenant_id (or a safe surrogate), config diff is ineligible.

Jacob Stein: Also “present” should mean “returned usable data”, not just “card rendered”. We can render a card that says “timed out, here’s a link” — that’s helpful but it’s not “present” for completeness.

Miles Thornton: Implementation-wise we can do both:
- A strict completeness score (data present)
- A “degraded-but-actionable” flag (we produced links + manual steps)

Miles Thornton: For reasons, we should bucket:
- ok
- no_data (source responded but empty)
- timeout
- rate_limited / circuit_open
- permission_denied
- upstream_error
- ineligible (missing keys)

Logan Wright: Please please keep cardinality under control. If we do metrics, labels should be limited to component + status bucket, and maybe environment (prod/stage). No tenant, no service labels.

Logan Wright: Suggestion:
- Counter: incident_bot_context_component_total{component, status}
- Histogram: incident_bot_context_completeness_score (0..1)
- Histogram: incident_bot_context_gather_latency_ms{component}

Logan Wright: For deeper slice/dice (service/region/incident type), use structured logs/events sampled.

Allison Grant: +1. If we attach service/region tags to metrics we’ll relive the “dashboard is down during incident” incident :)

Allison Grant: Also, define the window explicitly: “first 5 minutes” is human language; we should define it as: first bot post timestamp. The score should be calculated at generation-time and logged once.

Paula Mendes: From PM side, I need something we can report as a KPI during beta + GA.

Paula Mendes: What I want to chart:
- % of incidents with completeness >= 0.67 (2/3) and >= 1.0 (3/3)
- Breakdown of missing reasons (timeout vs permission vs no_data)
- Trend over time

Paula Mendes: If we do eligibility, those denominators need to be clear in reporting. Otherwise people will game it by marking things ineligible.

Sean Gallagher: We should explicitly document: eligibility rules are deterministic based on what’s in the incident context (service/region/tenant). No manual flags.

Sean Gallagher: Also, for config diff: permission_denied is *not* a bot failure if we purposely gate it. But it is a “missing component” for oncall experience. So let’s track it separately.

Aisha Bello: How about we log two scores:
- completeness_score_strict = ok_components / eligible_components
- completeness_score_oncall = ok_components / expected_components

Aisha Bello: Where expected_components is always 3 (deploy/config/saturation). Then we can see “oncall experience” vs “bot eligibility”.

Rafael Mendes: That’s gonna confuse people. I’d prefer one score + also log eligibility_count and status reasons.

Rafael Mendes: Then dashboards can show:
- avg score
- distribution
- % eligible=3

Jacob Stein: Another nuance: permission_denied is an expected outcome for config diff unless incident is Sev0/Sev1 break-glass. We should distinguish:
- permission_denied_expected
- permission_denied_unexpected (bot mis-scoped)

Dylan Brooks: For logging pipeline: easiest is structured event into our existing internal event stream (same one used by incident-bot audits), then we can fan out to warehouse.

Dylan Brooks: Event schema (high-level):
- event_name = incident_bot.context_completeness
- version = 1
- incident_thread_ts
- generated_at
- mode = initial_post|refresh
- eligible_components = [deploy, config, saturation]
- component_statuses = {deploy: ok/timeout/..., ...}
- score = 0..1
- gather_latency_ms_total

Dylan Brooks: We can keep service/region out of the metric path, but include them in the event (with sampling) if we want later analysis.

Allison Grant: Re sampling: yes. And if we include service/region, do NOT include tenant_id in the event unless we have a hard requirement + redaction. Even then, probably store a hashed tenant key.

Miles Thornton: On the bot side I can do:
- compute score after we attempt all component fetches (with per-component timeouts)
- emit one event for initial auto post (mode=initial_post)
- do not emit on user interactions (or emit separately as mode=refresh but we exclude from KPI)

Devon Price: Small detail: Slack ack deadline means we sometimes post partial and then update message blocks async.

Devon Price: If we compute at the very start we’ll undercount. Suggest we compute at “finalize” step after async completes OR after a hard cap (e.g., 12s) and mark remaining as timeout.

Logan Wright: +1 finalize with a cap. Also log a boolean like finalized=true/false so we know if we’re missing due to Slack constraints.

Aisha Bello: Summarizing a proposal (v1):
1) Components: deploy, config_diff, saturation
2) Eligibility:
- deploy eligible if service_id present (and optionally region)
- saturation eligible if service_id + region present
- config_diff eligible if tenant_key present AND config_scope present
3) Status buckets per component: ok | no_data | timeout | rate_limited | circuit_open | permission_denied_expected | permission_denied_unexpected | upstream_error | ineligible
4) Score = ok_count / eligible_count (if eligible_count=0, score=null + log reason “no_eligible_components”)
5) Log once per incident thread for initial post after finalize cap.

Sean Gallagher: Works for me. Please ensure “permission_denied_unexpected” triggers a bug/alert somewhere; that’s a bot regression.

Allison Grant: I’ll add a small dashboard panel in the v2 rollout dashboard:
- completeness score distribution
- top missing reasons by component

Allison Grant: But I’ll need the exact metric names + label values (Logan’s suggestion).

Logan Wright: I’ll draft the metrics spec and sanity check label cardinality. We can do counters only; histogram for score is fine.

Paula Mendes: Can we also log “context_generation_ms” end-to-end? That’s part of oncall experience.

Miles Thornton: yup. I’ll emit:
- gather_latency_ms_total
- gather_latency_ms_by_component (in event only)

Jacob Stein: For config diff: “no_data” should include “no changes in last N mins” vs “diff service returned empty unexpectedly”. Those are different.

Jacob Stein: Maybe split:
- no_change (legit)
- no_data (unexpected)

Rafael Mendes: Careful, that’s getting too granular. Can we keep status bucket stable and add a subreason in the event only?

Jacob Stein: Works. Status=no_data, subreason=no_change vs empty_response.

Aisha Bello: Action items:
- Miles: implement event emission + score compute (initial_post only), include eligibility + status + latency, finalize cap behavior.
- Logan: metrics spec + where to wire in.
- Allison: dashboard panels + weekly rollup view.
- Aisha: write up definition in the incident context guidelines + align w/ MTTR measurement doc.

incident-bot: :memo: Reminder set by Aisha Bello for “Context completeness v1 spec draft” in 2 days.

Miles Thornton: I’ll get a small PR up behind the v2 feature flag so we can validate events in staging.

Sean Gallagher: Please also sanity-check that audit logging doesn’t accidentally treat this as sensitive access. It’s meta telemetry, but it’s still incident tooling.

Dylan Brooks: I’ll loop in Kevin Osei if we need to route the event through the same audit pipeline. Should be fine but we should be explicit.

Aisha Bello: Cool. I’ll post the written spec in confluence once we agree on metric names.

source.text:
Sean Gallagher: We need to lock observability requirements for retention purge jobs before ENG starts wiring more stores. What are the *must-have* metrics + alerts + runbook content? Also please call out label/cardinality landmines.

Allison Grant: +1. If we ship this without sane cardinality we’re gonna regret it. Let’s define:
- metrics names + labels
- dashboard panels
- alert thresholds (page vs ticket)
- runbook: how to re-run + how to confirm deletes + how to throttle

Logan Wright: From telemetry side, we can emit from purge job framework + per-store adapters. Current draft metric list:
- `retention_ttl_lag_seconds` (gauge)
- `retention_purge_rows_deleted_total` (counter)
- `retention_purge_failures_total` (counter)
- `retention_purge_backlog_rows` (gauge) OR backlog by oldest row age
- `retention_purge_job_duration_seconds` (histogram)
- `retention_purge_job_runs_total` (counter)

Caleb Johnson: For SRE we should model lag + failure rate + “stuckness”. Lag alone is ambiguous if the job is disabled.

Sean Gallagher: Agree. Biggest oncall pain is “purge silently not running” or “running but never catching up”. We need a hard pager on those.

Allison Grant: Cardinality: please do NOT label by `tenant_id`, `org_id`, `customer`, `project`, `route`, `model`, etc. If you need tenant-level debugging, do it via logs / trace IDs, not Prom labels.

Logan Wright: Yep. Proposed labels:
- `store` (enum: request_logs, audit_logs, traces, metrics, artifacts)
- `env` (prod/stage)
- `region` (us-east-1, eu-west-1, etc)
- `result` (success/failure/skipped)
- `tier` (hosted/dedicated/private)
- `reason` only on skip/fail, but kept very small set (validation, hold, dependency, throttled)

Caleb Johnson: +1. Also keep `job_name` out unless it’s a bounded enum. Framework sometimes generates per-tenant job names — we cannot export that.

Kevin Osei: On legal hold: purge job will “skip” deletions for holds. We’ll need a metric to confirm holds are preventing deletions without looking like failure.

Jordan Lee: The evaluator can return `effective_policy_state` for a store/tenant: enabled | disabled | hold. But again, we can’t label with tenant.

Allison Grant: Exactly. We can add a gauge aggregated across tenants via periodic aggregation job (low cardinality):
- `retention_policy_state_tenants` with labels `store`, `region`, `tier`, `state` and value = count of tenants.
This gives SRE a sanity check without cardinality blowup.

Sean Gallagher: Nice. That helps catch “oops everyone is in disabled logging mode” too.

Logan Wright: For `retention_ttl_lag_seconds`, what should it represent? We can compute as (now - oldest_row_past_ttl_timestamp) for each store/region, aggregated max.

Caleb Johnson: Make it explicit:
- `retention_ttl_lag_seconds{store,region,tier}` = max lag across partitions (or max of shard lags)
- plus maybe p95 lag if we can afford it, but max is what triggers paging.

Allison Grant: Be careful: max across all tenants could be noisy if one tenant has a huge backlog, but that’s still a real issue. I’m okay paging on max if we also have a safe threshold + some suppression.

Sean Gallagher: Let’s do:
Pager:
- lag > 6h for audit_logs or request_logs in prod for 30m
Ticket:
- lag > 1h for 2h (early warning)
Also we should tune per store. Audit logs are the one customers will ask about.

Kevin Osei: Audit logs exporter uses windows (e.g., last 90 days). If purge lags by days, evidence exports might contain too much.

Allison Grant: For paging thresholds: I’d prefer:
- Request logs: page if `lag > 12h` for 1h
- Audit logs: page if `lag > 6h` for 30m
- Traces/metrics: ticket if `lag > 24h` (unless we have an SLO)
But if product wants a tighter SLO, we can adjust.

Sean Gallagher: Also page on job failure rate:
- `increase(retention_purge_failures_total[15m]) / increase(retention_purge_job_runs_total[15m]) > 0.2` for 30m (per store/region)
Or simpler: consecutive failures.

Caleb Johnson: Consecutive failure alert is much more readable.
- `retention_purge_last_success_timestamp` gauge (seconds since epoch)
- page if `time() - last_success > 1h` for audit_logs/request_logs
This avoids weirdness when runs are low volume.

Logan Wright: We can emit `retention_purge_last_success_timestamp` from the scheduler per store/region.

Allison Grant: Add also `retention_purge_last_run_timestamp` so we can differentiate “scheduler dead” vs “job runs but never succeeds”.

Sean Gallagher: Dashboard panels I want:
1) TTL Lag (by store, region)
2) Purge throughput (rows/sec) and rows deleted
3) Purge duration p50/p95
4) Failure counts + last success age
5) Backlog size / backlog age
6) Policy state tenant counts (enabled/disabled/hold)

Caleb Johnson: + panel for “DB pressure guardrail” (if applicable): if purge uses batch deletes with a safety limit, we should visualize “throttled” runs.

Logan Wright: We have a throttle mode when DB load is high. We can tag result=skipped, reason=throttled and emit `retention_purge_throttle_events_total`.

Allison Grant: `reason` label is OK if the set is fixed and small. Please document the allowed values in the runbook and enforce in code.

Sean Gallagher: Paging policy / oncall flow:
- Pager triggers for prod audit_logs/request_logs only (for now)
- Everything else to ticket in #eng-sre + Jira SRE queue
- Include runbook link in alert annotations

Kevin Osei: Runbook needs “verification” steps too. We keep getting asked “how do we prove purge happened”. We should standardize:
- check `retention_ttl_lag_seconds` trending down
- check `rows_deleted_total` increments
- spot-check DB count query (internal only)
- check sample tenant via secure admin query tool

Allison Grant: Yes, but runbook must be safe/limited. Anything “query per tenant” should be break-glass and audited.

Sean Gallagher: Put it as: “If customer escalation / compliance incident: follow break-glass procedure, link INT ticket template.”

Caleb Johnson: Also please add a section: “What NOT to do” (don’t bump batch size unbounded, don’t disable hold checks, don’t restart without checking backlog).

Logan Wright: One more note: metrics scrape interval is 60s in prod. Histograms can be heavy. For `job_duration_seconds`, do we need histogram or just summary gauge?

Allison Grant: Histogram is fine but keep buckets tight and limited. Most jobs should be seconds-minutes. Proposed buckets: 1s, 5s, 15s, 30s, 60s, 120s, 300s, 600s.

Sean Gallagher: Works. Also “rows deleted per run” is useful for seeing if we’re hitting safety cap.

Logan Wright: We can emit `retention_purge_rows_deleted_per_run` gauge at end of run, labeled store/region/tier.

Allison Grant: That’s okay (bounded labels). But don’t make it per shard.

Jordan Lee: For policy drift: we planned a separate dashboard panel “tenants with policy drift vs defaults”. That requires tenant dimension though.

Allison Grant: For now keep drift as *counts* by tier/region. If you need tenant list, generate a daily report artifact stored internally (not Prom). SRE can use that for targeted follow-up.

Sean Gallagher: Cool. Action items:
- Logan/Caleb: confirm final metric names + labels and post here for signoff
- Allison: draft alert rules (pager vs ticket) with thresholds above
- Kevin: add runbook section for verification + break-glass steps

Grafana Bot: Alert preview (staging): `retention_ttl_lag_seconds{store="audit_logs",region="us-east-1"} > 21600` firing for 0m (rule not yet enabled)

Allison Grant: lol staging already yelling at us :)

Sean Gallagher: ok but that’s why we do this. Please keep staging rules non-paging.

Logan Wright: Will post concrete metric contract later today. Also will add label whitelist enforcement in framework so we can’t accidentally add `tenant_id`.

Caleb Johnson: +1 on label whitelist. Make it a unit test. We’ve been burned.

Sean Gallagher: Thanks all. Let’s treat this as the contract for ENG-18452 dashboards/alerts + the purge framework PR hooks.

title: #eng-sre: 1740662244
preview:
Sean Gallagher: We need to lock observability requirements for retention purge jobs before ENG starts wiring more stores. What are the *must-have* metrics + alerts + runbook content? Also please call out label/cardinality landmines.

Allison Grant: +1. If we ship this without sane cardinality we’re gonna regret it. Let’s define:
- metrics names + labels
- dashboard panels
- alert thresholds (page vs ticket)
- runbook: how to re-run + how to confirm deletes + how to throttle

Logan Wright: From telemetry side, we can emit from purge job framework + per-store adapters. Current draft metric list:
- `retention_ttl_lag_seconds` (gauge)
- `retention_purge_rows_deleted_total` (counter)
- `retention_purge_failures_total` (counter)
- `retention_purge_backlog_rows` (gauge) OR backlog by oldest row age
- `retention_purge_job_duration_seconds` (histogram)
- `retention_purge_job_runs_total` (counter)

Caleb Johnson: For SRE we should model lag + failure rate + “stuckness”. Lag alone is ambiguous if the job is disabled.

Sean Gallagher: Agree. Biggest oncall pain is “purge silently not running” or “running but never catching up”. We need a hard pager on those.

Allison Grant: Cardinality: please do NOT label by `tenant_id`, `org_id`, `customer`, `project`, `route`, `model`, etc. If you need tenant-level debugging, do it via logs / trace IDs, not Prom labels.

Logan Wright: Yep. Proposed labels:
- `store` (enum: request_logs, audit_logs, traces, metrics, artifacts)
- `env` (prod/stage)
- `region` (us-east-1, eu-west-1, etc)
- `result` (success/failure/skipped)
- `tier` (hosted/dedicated/private)
- `reason` only on skip/fail, but kept very small set (validation, hold, dependency, throttled)

Caleb Johnson: +1. Also keep `job_name` out unless it’s a bounded enum. Framework sometimes generates per-tenant job names — we cannot export that.

Kevin Osei: On legal hold: purge job will “skip” deletions for holds. We’ll need a metric to confirm holds are preventing deletions without looking like failure.

Jordan Lee: The evaluator can return `effective_policy_state` for a store/tenant: enabled | disabled | hold. But again, we can’t label with tenant.

Allison Grant: Exactly. We can add a gauge aggregated across tenants via periodic aggregation job (low cardinality):
- `retention_policy_state_tenants` with labels `store`, `region`, `tier`, `state` and value = count of tenants.
This gives SRE a sanity check without cardinality blowup.

Sean Gallagher: Nice. That helps catch “oops everyone is in disabled logging mode” too.

Logan Wright: For `retention_ttl_lag_seconds`, what should it represent? We can compute as (now - oldest_row_past_ttl_timestamp) for each store/region, aggregated max.

Caleb Johnson: Make it explicit:
- `retention_ttl_lag_seconds{store,region,tier}` = max lag across partitions (or max of shard lags)
- plus maybe p95 lag if we can afford it, but max is what triggers paging.

Allison Grant: Be careful: max across all tenants could be noisy if one tenant has a huge backlog, but that’s still a real issue. I’m okay paging on max if we also have a safe threshold + some suppression.

Sean Gallagher: Let’s do:
Pager:
- lag > 6h for audit_logs or request_logs in prod for 30m
Ticket:
- lag > 1h for 2h (early warning)
Also we should tune per store. Audit logs are the one customers will ask about.

Kevin Osei: Audit logs exporter uses windows (e.g., last 90 days). If purge lags by days, evidence exports might contain too much.

Allison Grant: For paging thresholds: I’d prefer:
- Request logs: page if `lag > 12h` for 1h
- Audit logs: page if `lag > 6h` for 30m
- Traces/metrics: ticket if `lag > 24h` (unless we have an SLO)
But if product wants a tighter SLO, we can adjust.

Sean Gallagher: Also page on job failure rate:
- `increase(retention_purge_failures_total[15m]) / increase(retention_purge_job_runs_total[15m]) > 0.2` for 30m (per store/region)
Or simpler: consecutive failures.

Caleb Johnson: Consecutive failure alert is much more readable.
- `retention_purge_last_success_timestamp` gauge (seconds since epoch)
- page if `time() - last_success > 1h` for audit_logs/request_logs
This avoids weirdness when runs are low volume.

Logan Wright: We can emit `retention_purge_last_success_timestamp` from the scheduler per store/region.

Allison Grant: Add also `retention_purge_last_run_timestamp` so we can differentiate “scheduler dead” vs “job runs but never succeeds”.

Sean Gallagher: Dashboard panels I want:
1) TTL Lag (by store, region)
2) Purge throughput (rows/sec) and rows deleted
3) Purge duration p50/p95
4) Failure counts + last success age
5) Backlog size / backlog age
6) Policy state tenant counts (enabled/disabled/hold)

Caleb Johnson: + panel for “DB pressure guardrail” (if applicable): if purge uses batch deletes with a safety limit, we should visualize “throttled” runs.

Logan Wright: We have a throttle mode when DB load is high. We can tag result=skipped, reason=throttled and emit `retention_purge_throttle_events_total`.

Allison Grant: `reason` label is OK if the set is fixed and small. Please document the allowed values in the runbook and enforce in code.

Sean Gallagher: Paging policy / oncall flow:
- Pager triggers for prod audit_logs/request_logs only (for now)
- Everything else to ticket in #eng-sre + Jira SRE queue
- Include runbook link in alert annotations

Kevin Osei: Runbook needs “verification” steps too. We keep getting asked “how do we prove purge happened”. We should standardize:
- check `retention_ttl_lag_seconds` trending down
- check `rows_deleted_total` increments
- spot-check DB count query (internal only)
- check sample tenant via secure admin query tool

Allison Grant: Yes, but runbook must be safe/limited. Anything “query per tenant” should be break-glass and audited.

Sean Gallagher: Put it as: “If customer escalation / compliance incident: follow break-glass procedure, link INT ticket template.”

Caleb Johnson: Also please add a section: “What NOT to do” (don’t bump batch size unbounded, don’t disable hold checks, don’t restart without checking backlog).

Logan Wright: One more note: metrics scrape interval is 60s in prod. Histograms can be heavy. For `job_duration_seconds`, do we need histogram or just summary gauge?

Allison Grant: Histogram is fine but keep buckets tight and limited. Most jobs should be seconds-minutes. Proposed buckets: 1s, 5s, 15s, 30s, 60s, 120s, 300s, 600s.

Sean Gallagher: Works. Also “rows deleted per run” is useful for seeing if we’re hitting safety cap.

Logan Wright: We can emit `retention_purge_rows_deleted_per_run` gauge at end of run, labeled store/region/tier.

Allison Grant: That’s okay (bounded labels). But don’t make it per shard.

Jordan Lee: For policy drift: we planned a separate dashboard panel “tenants with policy drift vs defaults”. That requires tenant dimension though.

Allison Grant: For now keep drift as *counts* by tier/region. If you need tenant list, generate a daily report artifact stored internally (not Prom). SRE can use that for targeted follow-up.

Sean Gallagher: Cool. Action items:
- Logan/Caleb: confirm final metric names + labels and post here for signoff
- Allison: draft alert rules (pager vs ticket) with thresholds above
- Kevin: add runbook section for verification + break-glass steps

Grafana Bot: Alert preview (staging): `retention_ttl_lag_seconds{store="audit_logs",region="us-east-1"} > 21600` firing for 0m (rule not yet enabled)

Allison Grant: lol staging already yelling at us :)

Sean Gallagher: ok but that’s why we do this. Please keep staging rules non-paging.

Logan Wright: Will post concrete metric contract later today. Also will add label whitelist enforcement in framework so we can’t accidentally add `tenant_id`.

Caleb Johnson: +1 on label whitelist. Make it a unit test. We’ve been burned.

Sean Gallagher: Thanks all. Let’s treat this as the contract for ENG-18452 dashboards/alerts + the purge framework PR hooks.
